Tuyệt vời và ai đó sẽ cần loại đào tạo nào để có được vị trí của bạn? Những người đang học trung học và đại học nên làm gì bây giờ? Chắc chắn. Vì vậy, bạn biết đấy, điều mà chúng tôi luôn hướng đến mọi người khi bắt đầu là con số đầu tiên trong các chứng chỉ loại CompTIA, là A +, N + và S +. Nó thực sự cung cấp cho bạn một cơ sở tốt và một nền tảng để bắt đầu. Và đó thực sự là những gì chúng tôi tìm kiếm ở cấp độ của chúng tôi là , chúng tôi đang cố gắng tìm kiếm rất nhiều tiềm năng. Vì vậy, thật tốt khi họ đến với một nền tảng rất vững chắc, rằng họ hiểu rất nhiều từ về thuật ngữ mà chúng ta đang nói đến Một khi bạn đã thất bại, ý tôi là, thực sự là vấn đề chuyên môn hóa vào thời điểm đó và đó là nơi chúng tôi muốn thực sự giúp bạn sớm có được. Cho bạn tiếp xúc với rất nhiều công nghệ, chỉ cho bạn cách mọi thứ hoạt động, đặc biệt là trong tình hình của chính phủ và sau đó bạn có thể bắt đầu thực sự điều chỉnh giáo dục của mình, tại thời điểm đó. Bạn có thể chuyển sang hệ thống email; Bạn có thể hướng tới các hệ thống dựa trên đám mây. Vì vậy, có rất nhiều cơ hội nhưng chúng tôi thực sự muốn giúp bạn bắt đầu với một nền tảng vững chắc và sau đó thực sự tìm kiếm người có tiềm năng đó để thực sự vượt ra ngoài việc chỉ học và thực sự áp dụng kỹ năng đó. Vì vậy, đối với những người có thể muốn làm những gì bạn đang làm, loại theo bước chân của bạn, bạn có lời khuyên nào cho họ không? Vì vậy, bạn biết đấy, một trong những năm có lẽ là hình thành sớm nhất. Tôi đã dành nhiều thời gian Tôi sẽ nói rằng hoạt động tình nguyện thực sự hoạt động miễn phí, nhưng tôi dành nhiều thời gian để có được nhiều kinh nghiệm. Ý tôi là, đó là điều lớn nhất thực sự định hình bạn trong lĩnh vực này là bạn đi học ở những trường này hoặc bạn đi học những chương trình chứng nhận này và họ dạy bạn thế giới hoàn hảo trông như thế nào. Điều bạn nhận ra rất nhanh khi thoát ra là nó không bao giờ thực sự giống như vậy. Vì vậy, hãy sớm tận dụng mọi cơ hội để học hỏi. Nếu có bất kỳ chương trình hoặc nhóm mà bạn có thể tham gia, hãy chắc chắn là tình nguyện viên. Chắc chắn, nếu có một số cơ quan địa phương có thể sử dụng sự trợ giúp của bạn, hãy nhảy vào đó càng nhiều càng tốt. Cuối cùng nó thực sự mang lại lợi nhuận rất lớn. Steve, cảm ơn bạn rất nhiều vì đã dành thời gian. Thật tuyệt khi được biết bạn và biết một chút về thế giới CNTT và công nghệ không gian địa lý Tôi là Katelyn và đây là Gladeo trên Go Zoom Edition. Hẹn gặp lại.

title: Other Resources to Explore (US only)
transcript: Other Resources to Explore If you're interested in more specialized learning and real world experience, consider these platforms. Kaggle Competitions, perfect for those looking to showcase their data science or AI skills in competitive environments. Hack the North, participate in hackathons and collaborate on tech projects. Career Village, a community where students can ask professionals career related questions. Pathful, a career readiness platform, offering opportunities to connect with employers. Stay proactive and keep exploring these platforms for virtual and in-person opportunities that fit your career goals.

title: Remote Apprenticeships & Sponsored Projects
transcript: Remote Apprenticeships and Company Sponsored Projects Besides volunteering, there are also opportunities to work on company sponsored projects. These projects provide real-time challenges that allow you to showcase your skills to potential employers. Completing these projects can not only boost your resume, but also make you stand out to hiring managers. Here are a couple of platforms where you can find remote work-based learning opportunities. Parker Dewey offers short-term paid projects from employers looking to discover new talent. Apprenticeship.gov. A great resource for finding remote apprenticeships in various industries. Multiverse, a platform that connects you with professional apprenticeship programs to gain hands-on experience. Don't forget, if you're in college, you can also ask your career services department if they have connections with local employers or organizations that offer remote internships or apprenticeships.

title: Adding Reccommendations
transcript: Adding Skills In chapter three, we reviewed common hard and soft skills that employers are looking for. You can also figure out the best skills to add by researching current job postings related to your field of study and the type of job that you want. For example, indeed.com currently lists a job advertisement for a staff chemist. The employer has included the key qualifications applicants must possess, such as a college degree. But most of the qualifications listed are skills like effective verbal and written communication, self-motivation, flexibility, teamwork, and good safety discipline. These skills might seem generic, but employers are very serious about wanting candidates with these types of qualifications. Of course, job applicants need the right educational background, but what good is having a college degree if they can't explain their thoughts clearly or collaborate well with others. So as you add relevant skills to your profile, keep in mind what employers are looking for. Put yourself in their shoes. This will help demonstrate that you're a great candidate.

title: How Long Should a Cover Letter Be?
transcript: ﻿1 How long should a cover letter be? When it comes to the length and content of your cover letter, the general recommendation is to keep it one page. According to the experts at Indeed, your cover letter should be three to four paragraphs long and include around 200 to 400 words. Just enough to introduce yourself and explain why you're a great fit for the position without overwhelming the reader. The goal is to keep it concise but impactful. Formatting your cover letter. Header. At the top of your cover letter, include your name, address, email, and phone number. After that, add the date and the employer's contact information such as the name, title, company, and address. Greeting. Start with a professional greeting like, "Dear Hiring Manager," if possible. If you can't find the hiring manager's name, use "Dear Hiring Team" or "To Whom It May Concern." Introduction. Your first paragraph should grab the reader's attention. Introduce yourself, mention the job you're applying for, and explain why you're excited about the opportunity. Body. In the main part of your letter, connect your experiences, skills, and qualifications to the job ad. Use specific examples to show how you're a good fit for the company. Conclusion. Wrap up your cover letter by reiterating your interest in the role and thinking the employer for their time. Be sure to include a call to action, like expressing your enthusiasm for an interview. Signature. End with a polite closing, such as "Sincerely" or "Best regards," followed by your name. If you're printing a letter, leave space to sign it above your typed name. Additional formatting tips. Margins. Keep your margins around one inch on all sides to give your cover letter a clean and professional look. Font. Use a simple readable font like Arial, Times New Roman, or Calibri in a 10 to 12 point size, you want to ensure it's easy to read, both on screen and in print. Line Spacing. Use single-spacing throughout your cover letter, but leave a space between paragraphs for readability. By following these guidelines, you can ensure that your cover letter is not only professional and content but also in appearance. We have some examples on the next page for you to read over.

title: Profile Photo
transcript: Adding skills and recommendations to your LinkedIn profile. In a previous module, we talked about how including keywords in your profile helps potential employers find you. Another way to boost your visibility is by adding skills. You can add up to five skills for every employment period you add. Plus five skills for every educational experience. But what skills should you add?

title: Resume Assistance
transcript: Resume Assistance It might be awhile before you have to write a resume, but it's good to know what a resume is and how AI can assist. A resume is a list of work and educational experiences you've had in the past. When someone wants a new job, they show the potential employer their resume. This lets the employer see whether or not the person has the right skills for the job. A great resume won't get you a job, but it can get you a job interview. Unfortunately, many resumes never get seen by employers. Why not? Because employers use software called Applicant tracking systems that decide which resumes employers should review. These programs look for keywords and phrases, then filter out resumes that don't seem to match what the employer is looking for. So now job seekers use AI tools to help write resumes that will get past those programs. Popular resume websites include Jobscan Kickresume, resumaker.ai, ResumeGenius, Rezi AI, Skillroads. OpenAI's free chatGPT can write resumes too.

title: Job Search
transcript: Job Search It might seem like a long way off, but eventually you'll be looking for a job. Today, job seekers use employment websites to look for job openings. Indeed.com is one of the biggest sites, but other job websites include Monster, Glassdoor. FlexJobs, Simply Hired, USA Jobs and ZipRecruiter. These portals list millions of job advertisements. The problem is with so many results listed, sometimes it's hard to find the best matches. AI can help with that. Websites like Kick Resume's, Pyjama Jobs ask users to put in their information. Then the sites do the rest. They find companies with job openings that match the user's inputs and even suggest career types that users never thought of. Our Gladeo quiz is similar and it uses your quiz results to suggest careers and industries that match your interests.

title: Personalized Learning
transcript: Personalized Learning When you're getting ready for a career, you're learning lots of new things. AI powered personalized learning platforms help with that through one or more of the following ways. Adaptive learning and Assessment AI adapts the materials, speed of delivery, and level of difficulty based on how the user is doing. Intelligent Tutoring AI acts like a tutor, offering live explanations and feedback based on the user's inputs. Natural Language Processing AI responds to questions and it chats with the user to explain topics. Customized Insights and Recommendations AI analyzes how the user is doing and offers advice on ways to improve. AI Personalized learning programs include Aleks for math, chemistry, and other subjects. Century for personalized learning paths on lots of subjects. Cogni an AI virtual tutor. DreamBox Learning, adaptive Learning for math and reading skills. Duolingo, an AI language learning platform. Knewton featuring personalized STEM materials and Smart Sparrow, an adaptive learning platform for educators.

title: Bonus Tips
transcript: Bonus tips. Before you dive into writing your experience section, let's review a few bonus tips. First, add skills. For every employment period you add, LinkedIn lets you select five skills you use for that job. Add media. LinkedIn also lets you add media such as images, presentations, or documents. You can even drop a link to an external website. Review other profiles. Look up a few successful professionals to see how they phrase their LinkedIn profiles. These individuals don't necessarily have to be in the same industry as you. Add keywords. Incorporate relevant keywords into your profile to make it more search friendly. If you're not sure which keywords to use, scan some job descriptions or job posts related to your position. Highlight the keywords that stand out. Use powerful action verbs. Your profile needs to be stocked with plenty of action verbs. Some examples of this include: Achieved, as in achieved a 100% success rate. Strategized, as in strategized effective plans. Optimized, as in optimized conversations. Generated, as in generated 5 million in sales. Quantify achievements include quantifiable data such as dollar amounts, percentages, or statistics. For example, if you manage an inventory, list the number of items or the dollar value of the inventory as a whole. Now that we've discussed what goes on in the experience section, it's time to take the knowledge check before we move on.

title: Resume: Final Thoughts
transcript: ﻿1 This video is presented to you by Gladeo on the Go and the Los Angeles Opportunity Youth Collaborative. My name is Cheyenne, but I go by Shy and I currently serve as the executive assistant of current programming at ITV America and I'm also the founder of my own foster youth advocacy brand called Jenni's Flower. At ITV America, we are actually a production company, so there are two kind of main components in the, in Hollywood, in the entertainment industry. There's the network side and then there's the production side. So there's the people that are making the TV shows and the movies, and then there's the people that are distributing them. And you know, Hulu, Netflix, HBO Max. As an executive assistant, I'm kind of on the corporate side, so just a lot of emails and a lot of calendar management is pretty much 90% of my job. And what's your favorite part about being in the position you're in right now? I think one of my favorite things about this job is getting like an inside look into how all these things are made and getting an inside look into how executives actually work. My ultimate goal is to be a filmmaker, specifically a director. I really want to tell the stories of transitional age foster youth through unscripted documentary series. I also would love to explore the world of scripted, so getting into scripted shows such as Shameless, or shows that really touch on the complexities of foster youth and the experiences that they have when they're transitioning out of the system. So watching my executive, reading the emails he sends, and seeing what he says, and seeing why they chose to pick up this pitch from this guy and not this pitch from this person. It's all insight that I can take and I can use that when I'm making my pitch decks or when I'm having meetings with NBC executives. My boss will invite me to join calls sometimes, but he thinks might be important or he thinks I might learn something from that call that will help me on my career path. That was definitely one of the biggest things that I would recommend to anyone starting off, to really sit down and have like a, a one-on-one conversation with your executives. Kind of give them a little bit of insight into where you're hoping to get, and so that way they can just keep it in the back of their mind whenever things are happening. What inspired you to go into this? What was the journey toward making that decision? So I was in my last quarter at UCLA when the pandemic hit and I had my boss, anytime she might come across an email or an internship or she would always just send it to me via email and be like, Hey, I think you'd be good for this. So she had sent me one from the Television Academy Foundation, which is the organization that puts on the Emmys. But they have a specific initiative that they started about five or six years ago called the Foster Forward Initiative. And the Foster Forward Initiative is a internship program specifically for foster youth that want to break into film and television. I didn't have any film and television experience, even though I went to UCLA, I didn't go there for film and TV. So I was placed at Moonbug Entertainment, which is a preschool entertainment company, it's a production company. At the time, they had just acquired two of the biggest preschool shows, which was Cocomelon and Blippi. That's where I really got to learn development. And I got, I wrote like 25 songs for the channel. I got to write a couple different scripts. I was involved in the development of our podcast that launched on Spotify, and I was involved in our Netflix show that we were making. So I kind of got to see like the whole pot, but I wasn't particularly inspired by the preschool demographic. I've always just been more drawn to like teenagers, specifically the transitional age. That's kind of where I've always felt that I could make the most impact. And that's where I came across ITV America by working here and exactly what I was saying, learning all the different roles and seeing who does what. I was able to like look at the director and be like, oh, that's, I wanna be doing what he's doing. Can you talk about some of the qualities you would find in a really good executive assistant? Clearly you have to be organized, but like what other things would you find in a person who would really do well? You have to kind of be able to adapt. So one day you might be working on this, and then the next day something happens and your new priority is, is this. I think another one is resilient. In this industry, there's just, there's a lot of pressure on set. There can be a lot of just buildup and there can be a lot of things thrown at you, a lot of misplaced anger, or you might make a mistake, and you have to be able to not let that just completely shut you down. You have already done so many amazing things and you're so young. So what are some of your goals in the coming years? In the coming years, I really want to shift the narrative on foster youth and really change the way people view foster care and foster youth. I really want to make the next, Annie, I'd love to do a new foster youth story through a musical, but really kind of taking and stripping out all of the, the negative stereotypes that they implemented in there. And I think just the, the way that they portray the stories isn't as authentic as it could be. And then I think lastly, just being at peace. I think that's something that I've always wanted to do and always wanted to feel. And so I think getting to a point where I'm able to express myself creatively and help foster youth through Jenni's Flower, that would kind of be one of my main goals. Amazing. Well Cheyenne, you're gonna change the world. I truly believe that. Thank you so much for taking the time out of your day to sit down and share all of this with us. Of course. Thank you, Katelyn. I'm Katelyn and this is Gladeo on the Go Zoom edition in partnership with OYC. See ya.

title: What is a Cover Letter?
transcript: ﻿1 What to Avoid in a Cover Letter. While cover letters are a great way to stand out, there are a few common things to watch out for: One. Skip the gimmicks. You don't need fancy fonts or colored paper to stand out. Keep it simple and professional. Let your words do the talking, not the design. For example: Using flashy colors or quirky fonts can make your application look unprofessional. Stick to the standard fonts like Arial or Times New Roman on plain white paper. Two. Don't just repeat your resume. Your cover letter should add to what's in your resume, not just repeat it. Use it to explain why your experience matters for this job. Instead of saying, "I worked as a sales associate," try, "I developed strong customer service skills by helping up to 50 customers daily." Three. Be honest. It's tempting to exaggerate your skills, but it's important to always keep it real. Employers appreciate authenticity, and they'll likely verify your experience. For example: If you're still learning Spanish, say, "I have basic conversational skills," instead of claiming fluency.

title: Why Do I Need a Cover Letter?
transcript: ﻿1 Why do I need a cover letter? Not every job requires a cover letter. But when one does, it's a great way to stand out. While resumes are useful for listing your experience, they don't show much about who you are. A cover letter lets an employer see the person behind the resume. According to a survey from Resume Genius, "Out of 625, hiring managers, 83% said cover letters are an important part of their hiring decision. And even when they're not required, 49% of employers still expect to receive one. A well-written cover letter can be the deciding factor when qualifications are similar between candidates." The goal of the cover letter is simple: to get you an interview. Think of it like your personal "ad" - something that gets the employer interested enough to say, "I wanna know more about this person!"

title: Intro to Using AI for Career Readiness
transcript: How to Use AI for Career Readiness and Development Artificial intelligence is part of our lives now. It's embedded in apps, search engines, websites, and programs used in almost every industry on earth. It's even reading this voiceover you're listening to. So how can we use AI to get ready for our future careers and how can it help us advance and get promotions after we start those careers? You are about to find out. First, let's define career readiness and career development. Career readiness is the process of preparing yourself to enter the workforce. It involves learning new skills, creating goals, and preparing for a career. Career development is a series of activities in an ongoing, lifelong process of developing one's career. That includes defining new goals regularly and acquiring skills to achieve them. Here are some ways AI tools can help in both areas.

title: Shaping Your Future With AI
transcript: Shaping your Future with AI Some technology leaders are concerned about the powerful potential of AI. Many even consider it a threat, but AI will be a huge part of your future no matter what career you choose. And as it evolves, the opportunities and challenges associated with it will grow too. So stay informed and get involved. The more you understand AI, the better you can use it responsibly. As the digital leaders of tomorrow, the future isn't just something you'll inherit, it's something you are shaping right now.

title: Dealing With AI Bias
transcript: Dealing with AI Bias Can AI be biased? Of course it can because it's created by humans. It's also trained using inputs by humans or by analyzing data created by humans. Thus, it's almost unavoidable that AI programs will pick up certain biases along the way. This can lead to unfair outcomes in some situations like job recruitment or law enforcement. As we continue to incorporate AI into our lives, we must keep in mind that AI is far from perfect. Like humans, it's not always fair, objective, or accurate.

title: Privacy Concerns With AI
transcript: Privacy concerns with AI Every time you use an app or browse the web, there's a chance an AI program is monitoring your activity. Countless companies incorporate features in their software that collect data about you, like your personal information, you location, your browsing habits and other online behavior. The more they can learn about you, the better they can market to you. Your data is also a valuable asset companies can share or sell to others, which many of them do. Think about it like this. If you use a site, program or app that's free, that's because you're paying them by giving them your information so they can target you for ads or sell your data to third parties. So adjust those privacy and permission settings. Think twice about the information you share and read the fine print before downloading any software and apps or clicking to accept all cookies from websites.

title: Recognizing & Countering Misinformation
transcript: Recognizing and Countering Misinformation The entertainment industry has long used technology such as CGI for movies. Now, thanks to generative AI tools like Stable Diffusion and Unreal Engine, almost anyone can produce highly realistic images or video content. Some content is so realistic, it's hard to tell the difference anymore. That's fine for certain purposes, but many people exploit this technology for malicious purposes. For instance, they may manipulate existing content to create deepfake videos, photos, or audio tracks designed to deceive audiences. This unfortunate trend will continue to grow, adding to the rapid rise of online disinformation, such as false information spread for the purpose of misleading others. Students should be aware of and avoid such unethical AI practices and learn how to critically evaluate content and sources.

title: Ethical Use of AI in Academics
transcript: Ethical Use of AI in Academics AI programs like ChatGPT are productive tools. They're great for doing research, making suggestions, and automating a wide range of tasks. But when it comes to homework, teachers expect students to do their own assignments because that process is how students learn. Having an AI program do your work may be tempting, but it defeats the purpose of taking the class. It may also be considered plagiarism. If you're interested in using AI to help with homework, talk with your instructors to understand their policies and restrictions. Some may be fine with using AI as long as its role is limited, and the work you turn in is your own, but it's best to follow their guidance.

title: How Does a Cover Letter Help?
transcript: ﻿1 How does a cover letter help? Think of it like this: Your resume is full of facts like dates, job titles and skills, but your cover letter breathes life into that data. It explains why those facts matter. For example: Your resume might say you let a project. Awesome! But your cover letter explains why you having led the project is important for this job. Maybe that project taught you how to lead a team, hit deadlines, or solve problems under pressure. That's the kind of thing. A cover letter lets you explain. Let's say you're applying for a cashier position, and one of the skills your employer is looking for is customer service. To make your cover letter stand out, you could talk about a project where you helped organize a school event or volunteered at a community fundraiser. Maybe you worked at their welcome table, where you greeted attendees, answered their questions, and made sure they had a great experience. This shows that you've handled customer facing tasks before and highlights your ability to interact with people - a key part of customer service.

title: Introduction
transcript: ﻿1 Introduction to Self-Awareness. As students, you're doing your best to get through school so you can be prepared for your future. But what do you want to do in your future" And who do you want to be? Sometimes that's hard to figure out.. especially when we don't fully understand who we are now. So before you start exploring career paths and the required education and training for them, in this chapter, let's explore YOU! This chapter is all about you getting to know yourself better. Discovering what you truly want in life begins with exploring who you are, what you believe, and what matters most to you. Self-awareness is a lifelong journey, and each step helps you understand yourself a little better. To do that, you'll have to deep dive and explore your core values, motivations, and priorities. And also your interests, skills, and aptitude. Being self-aware also involves knowing your goals in life, your own definition of success, and how you learn from experiences. All these unique variables weave together to make you the fascinating person you currently are. And like the rest of us, "who you are" changes and evolves as you get older. Getting to know yourself better will help you align your future career choices with who you truly are. The more you understand yourself, the more confidently you can explore the world of career possibilities. But remember, exploring your own self-awareness isn't a one-time process; it's a lifelong journey.

title: Skills to Develop for AI Career
transcript: Skills to Develop for an AI Career. Career advice for the future. "Focus on skills you will learn, not job titles." says Gaurav Ashisha from Nevus Security. What does he mean by that? Since AI is changing how things are done, jobs in the future will be different than they are today. When you plan for the education and training you'll need for your future career, think about the skills that will be needed in the future. Keep in mind that AI isn't going to make all jobs disappear, but it will replace some and help with others. So, it won't hurt to study artificial intelligence and other evolving technologies. Some of the AI skills you could study would be related to computing, data science, intelligent user interfaces, machine learning, natural language processing, programming, robotics. As we mentioned, AI is going to automate many jobs, but we'll always need workers with soft skills. What are those? Soft skills are things like communication, flexibility, problem solving, teamwork, the ability to work independently, time management. Remember, the key to finding jobs in the future is to either do things AI can't do or do things better than AI can.

title: Keeping Up with AI and Technology
transcript: Keeping up with AI and Technology. Digital marketing is a perfect case study of the importance of keeping up with ever evolving technologies. Technologies, including artificial intelligence, are rapidly causing careers to change. Most of these changes are highly beneficial, but they're nonetheless disruptive. That's why one of the most important markers of success in the future of work will be one's ability to stay flexible, ready to adapt, grow, and evolve along with the world around us.

title: Jobs That Won't be Impacted
transcript: Jobs That Won't Be Impacted By AI Some jobs aren't going to be affected much by AI either way. For example, Mike Rowe, the host of the TV show Dirty Jobs, says, "I haven't seen any plumbing robots." AI can't replace human workers in certain trade jobs, aka skilled labor, like plumbing, electrical work, carpentry, or other hands-on professions such as mechanic work, truck driving, cooking, et cetera. AI might assist with some parts of the job, but it's too expensive to use AI powered robots for those jobs, except for things like manufacturing and other assembly line processes. Cool trivia. Many trade/ skilled labor jobs don't require a college degree. Workers can learn and get paid at the same time through apprenticeship programs. Trade / skilled labor jobs often pay as much or more than white collar office jobs. The term skilled labor can apply to some hands on white collar jobs, such as doctors, nurses, or engineers.

title: Steps on Writing Your Cover Letter
transcript: ﻿1 Resumes 101. Writing your ticket to a job interview. Writing a resume might sound hard if you've never done it, but it's easy if you know what to do. A resume is just a list of things you've done in the past so employers can decide if you are a good fit for what they need. Your resume only has one job - to get you interviews. It's like an ad to get employers interested in you, so they'll want to learn more about you. By itself, your resume cannot get you hired. But it can get you in a room or on a video chat with an employer or hiring manager. From there, the interview will be the deciding factor. And to get that interview, you'll need an incredible resume! Of course, not every job requires a resume. Some just need an application form. But eventually, you will need one - plus they're a great way to keep track of your work history, education, and other accomplishments. Keeping a core resume. Resumes are living documents that you'll want to update every time you have something new to add. You'll start with a core resume where you add new experiences. That version will always be a "work in progress." Then, when you apply for a job, just make a copy of that core resume and customize it to the job advertisement. Save the copy with a file name that indicates the job you're applying to. This will help you stay organized, so you don't accidentally type over your core resume. Before we move on, let's go over some "behind-the-scenes" information that many job-seekers don't know about - Automated Tracking Systems.

title: Jobs That Might Grow
transcript: Jobs That Might Grow Because of AI AI careers are hot and projected to grow with job titles like big data engineer, business intelligence developer, data scientist, machine learning engineer, natural language processing NLP engineer, robotics engineer, software architect. These jobs require studying computer science, math, and statistics. Many require a college degree, but that is starting to change because of something called the talent gap. The talent gap means there are not enough qualified people to do a particular job. When there's a talent gap, companies might change their requirements to be qualified. In other words, jobs that require a college degree today might not in the near future. Instead of degrees, boot camps and certification programs are shorter, more specialized ways to get the education and training needed.

title: Jobs That Might Shrink
transcript: Jobs that Might Shrink Because of AI Many career fields have already been impacted by the explosion of AI. For example, creative careers. AI is surprisingly creative. DALLE 2 can create realistic images and art from a description in natural language. That means users can type in a text description and the program will make works of art in seconds. This will impact graphic design and visual media career fields. ChatGPT-4 is affecting writing jobs such as copywriting, content creation, and even news reporting. Open AI's Jukebox generates music including rudimentary singing as raw audio in a variety of genres and artist styles. MuseNet can produce four minute musical compositions with 10 different instruments and can combine styles from country to Mozart to the Beatles. These programs will impact musician's work. AI is also used for photo manipulation, deep fake videos and voice cloning, all of which will affect creative fields. Customer Service Careers Ever seen a self-service checkout counter at a store, fast food franchise or airport? Well, those counters are going to get more advanced because of AI. This may mean fewer human customer service agents. As AI gets better at answering questions, chatbots for store websites, apps, and call centers will keep taking over customer service tasks too. One day customers might not even know if they're talking to an AI customer service representative or not. The Turing Test and Digital Humans Consider the Turing test. If a machine can engage in a conversation with a human without being detected as a machine, it has demonstrated human intelligence, writes Investopedia. The Turing test involves a human tester communicating with something they can't see. That something is either another person or an artificial intelligence program. The tester doesn't know which. If they are communicating with an AI that fools them into believing they're communicating with a person, then the AI passes the Turing test. The Turing test has already been beaten in recent years. Now, companies want to use AI for customer service roles more than ever. Using AI is cheaper than using human customer service workers. The only thing missing, a human face, but even that's being worked on. Companies are using deep fake technology to create digital humans. A hybrid of AI and computer graphics that puts human-like faces on chatbots and other computer-based interfaces. Technology careers. It's ironic, but high tech jobs are at risk of being replaced by high tech AI. AI can write code, analyze data, and do much of the work that programmers and software engineers do. And AI can do it in less time. Does that mean those jobs will disappear? Not entirely, but AI is being trained to replace many of these workers. There will still be tech jobs in the future, but maybe not as many.

title: What to Avoid in a Cover Letter
transcript: ﻿1 What to Avoid in a Cover Letter. While cover letters are a great way to stand out, there are a few common things to watch out for: One. Skip the gimmicks. You don't need fancy fonts or colored paper to stand out. Keep it simple and professional. Let your words do the talking, not the design. For example: Using flashy colors or quirky fonts can make your application look unprofessional. Stick to the standard fonts like Arial or Times New Roman on plain white paper. Two. Don't just repeat your resume. Your cover letter should add to what's in your resume, not just repeat it. Use it to explain why your experience matters for this job. Instead of saying, "I worked as a sales associate," try, "I developed strong customer service skills by helping up to 50 customers daily." Three. Be honest. It's tempting to exaggerate your skills, but it's important to always keep it real. Employers appreciate authenticity, and they'll likely verify your experience. For example: If you're still learning Spanish, say, "I have basic conversational skills," instead of claiming fluency.

title: In-Person Mentorship and Skill Development
transcript: In-person Mentorship and Skill Development Beyond volunteering and internships, you can gain valuable career insights through local mentorship programs. Mentors can help guide your career, offer advice, and sometimes introduce you to new job opportunities. Plus, you'll get personalized feedback and the chance to build lasting relationships with experienced professionals. Consider these resources for finding mentorship. Local Business Groups Many cities have Chambers of Commerce or business networks that offer mentorship programs, pairing young professionals with experienced leaders. College Alumni Networks Reach out to your school's alumni office to find professionals who can offer in-person mentorship opportunities.

title: Intro to Volunteering
transcript: ﻿1 Developing Career Skills The goal of seeking advanced education and training is to prepare for a career. A career is not the same as a job. A job is a specific position of employment. While a career is a long-term professional journey with progressive development and growth. Many workers switch jobs over the course of their careers. As they grow professionally and develop new skills. As they progress on their career trajectories, they can qualify for higher earnings, better opportunities and options with increased job satisfaction and security. Education and training are the keys to career success. They provide the foundational knowledge and skills needed to grow and adapt in an ever changing job market. Employers of today and tomorrow are looking for applicants with a mix of hard and soft skills, practical experience, and academic knowledge. Unlike hard technical skills, soft skills like effective communication and attention to detail are developed over time through personal experiences, social interactions, and both formal and informal education. It's time to explore opportunities in high school.

title: A Viable Career Path?
transcript: 이 직업 경로가 어떻게 당신을 찾았는지 알고 싶습니다. 그럼 조금은 집안에 있었던 것 같은데요? 나는 20년 정도 바텐더로 일했고 그 다음에 다른 직업을 좋아했다. 나는 술집에서 일하는 것이 조금 피곤했다. 그래서 35세에는 나은 것을 찾으러 가자고 생각했습니다. 그리고 바로 그때 내 직업 조언자 나에게 직업을 지시했습니다. 그녀가 말했다, 거래에 대해 어떻게 생각하세요? 그리고 저는 아시다시피, 우리 가족 안에 있었습니다'라고 생각했습니다. 그건 내 피속에 있는 것 같아. 나는 아무것도 두려워한 적이 없다. 그래서 저는 그래, 한번 해보자고 생각했어요. 용접 같은 기계를 켜는 방법도 몰랐고 0 나를 찾는 데 오랜 이 걸렸지만 결국 찾았고 이보다 더 행복할 수는 없었다. 그것은 내린 결정 중 가장 인생을 바꾸는 최고의 결정이었습니다. 그런 말을 들으니까 너무 기분이 좋아요. 당신 당신의 목적을 찾았습니다. 정말 멋지네요! 선물이에요. 그것은 나에게 선물이었다. 그럼, 방금 뛰어들었던 것처럼 어떻게 배웠나요? 그러면 무역은 어떻게 배웠나요? 내 나이에는 보다 20년, 적어도 10년 전에는 했어야 할 일을 으로 하게 된다. 나는 시간이 많지 않다는 것을 알고 있었다. 나의 유일한 선택은 배우고, 실행하고, 성공시키는 것이었습니다. 나머지 절반은 솔직히 남성 산업에서 여성 중심의 이었습니다. 학교와 일을 병행하는 데 약 4년 걸렸습니다. 평균 정도입니다. 응. 알았어, 멋지다! 알아두면 좋아요.

title: Dream Big, But Start Small!
transcript: ﻿1 Dream Big, But Start Small. Of course, it's awesome to have big dreams. You should absolutely allow yourself the freedom to dream as large as you want! Maybe you want to become a doctor, build an app that changes the world, or train to be an olympic athlete. Those big dreams can serve as your navigation application. But the key to making them happen is breaking them down into bite-sized tasks You can actually do. For instance, if you want to start a successful YouTube channel, the "big goal" might be gaining a certain number of subscribers or landing a sponsorship deal. But you won't get there overnight. Your smaller goals could be one, Researching equipment and editing software: Figure out what camera, microphone, or editing app you need. Two, Planning your content: Brainstorm ideas for your first five videos, outlining what you'll talk about or demonstrate. Three, Filming and editing one video: Commit to recording and editing just one piece of content before thinking about the next. Four, Promoting your work: Share your first few videos on social media or with friends to get initial feedback. Each smaller goal is a step that moves you closer to that big dream. And with each step you complete, you build both your skills and your confidence!

title: Why Set Realistic Goals?
transcript: ﻿1 Why Set Realistic Goals? When it comes to building resilience, Setting realistic goals is a game-changer. Why? Because unrealistic goals can leave us feeling defeated if we don't reach them on time or if they seem too far away from where we are right now. On the other hand, realistic goals set us up for small wins that boost our confidence and keep us motivated. Every time you accomplish a mini goal, it's like charging your personal battery - it powers you up to keep going! Think about climbing a mountain. You wouldn't try to leap straight from the base to the summit in one jump. Instead, you'd plan out a path, stopping at checkpoints or camps along the way to rest and reassess. Each checkpoint is a smaller goal that eventually leads you to the breathtaking view at the top. Setting realistic goals in your life works the same way.

title: Turning Big Dreams Into Realistic Goals
transcript: ﻿1 Celebrate Small Wins. People often overlook celebrating small achievements because they're so focused on the end goal. But every time you hit a milestone, like filming your first YouTube video, or acing a quiz in a tough subject, it's important to acknowledge it. Celebrating these moments doesn't mean throwing a huge party every time you finish a single homework assignment, though that could be fun. It just means taking a moment to appreciate what you've accomplished. Small celebrations can be treating yourself to your favorite snack, taking a relaxing break, like going for a walk, or reading a chapter of a non-school book. Sharing your success with someone who cares, like a friend, parent, or mentor. Writing down what you learned and how you feel about the progress. Acknowledging your mini-successes provides quick hits of motivation and reminds you that you're on the right track. It feels good, and that good feeling can keep you pushing forward.

title: Knowledge Check Video Instructions
transcript: ﻿1 Connecting Passions and Interests: Knowledge Check. Now that we've explored how passions and interests guide us toward meaningful careers, let's see how your passions and interests align by answering a few key questions. Think carefully about what excites and motivates you, and how your values and goals connect to your interests. When you're ready, start the knowledge check.

title: Education Section
transcript: Education Section The education section is mainly for post-secondary educational experiences. In other words, education beyond the 12th grade level. This section is very straightforward and most of the fields are optional. The only mandatory field is the school name. School: Add entries for current and previous schools, such as colleges, universities, vocational or technical schools, et cetera. If you're still in high school, you can add that. Otherwise, you don't really need to add your high school. Degree: If you're in college or went to college, list your degree type, such as a bachelor's, master's, et cetera. You can also include certificates. Field of study: List whatever you're majoring in or majored in if you finished or leave it blank if you prefer. Start date: Select the month and year you began at that school. If you're not sure, look back at your transcripts. End date: Select the month and year you finished at that school. If you're still attending, list your expected completion date if possible. Grade: Type in your cumulative grade point average. If you're unsure of what your GPA is, you can ask your academic advisor for help calculating. Activities and societies: List activities you've engaged in such as athletics, as well as organizations you've belonged to, such as Greek life societies and student clubs. This section is limited to 500 characters. Description: This space can be used to describe academic experiences, details about favorite courses, research, projects, or anything not covered elsewhere. This section is limited to 1000 characters. Skills: For every education experience, LinkedIn lets you select five skills. Media: LinkedIn also lets you add media such as images, presentations, or documents. You can even drop a link to an external website such as your online portfolio.

title: Learning New Things
transcript: Learning New Things Students can gain invaluable insights by strategically following certain organizations and individuals on LinkedIn. This allows you to receive regular updates and information directly to your feed. Reading such updates with consistency and intention can be a highly beneficial practice. It's like getting free mentorship and advice directly from the most successful and motivational people on earth. You can usually leave comments on their posts too, which may be read by countless other professionals. To find accounts you want to follow, just type your entry into the search bar.

title: Looking For Work
transcript: Looking for Work LinkedIn can help job seekers in a few different ways. For starters, users can advertise when they are open to work. This signals employers that you're available if they want to reach out to you, in case your profile matches what they're searching for. The Open to Work feature can be found on your profile under the number of connections you have. Just click the Open to button, then choose the Finding a new Job option. From there, LinkedIn will prompt you to add information about the type of work you're open to. The platform also features a Jobs tab located next to the My Network tab at the top of the screen. This feature allows users to browse job postings related to their profile data and search history. You can easily modify the job search filters by clicking the preference icon and updating the options.

title: Growing Your Network
transcript: Growing your Network Why do so many people obsess about growing their network? For starters, networks carry inherent value in the form of social capital. In financial terms, capital means money or assets used to invest in or grow a business. Social capital is the value we get in the form of knowledge, benefits or favors from people who can help us. For example, if you're friends with a famous director, you might be able to use that connection to land a role in an upcoming movie. Thus, that social connection has a lot of potential value. And that's why it's important to grow your network, but also why you wanna grow it strategically, not randomly.

title: Making a Step-by-Step Plan
transcript: ﻿1 Making A Step-by-Step Plan. Once you've identified your big dream, try reverse-engineering it. Ask yourself, "What has to happen first, second, third..?" Then write down those steps in the order you need to do them. A step-by-step plan can look like a list, a simple timeline, or even a digital spreadsheet - whatever helps you visualize the path you'll follow. Let's say you're aiming to get into a competitive college program: One, Research the requirements. Look up the test scores, GPA, or specific classes you need. Check the school's acceptance rate so you know how competitive it is, and remember that meeting a minimum GPA or SAT score doesn't guarantee acceptance. Two, Gather materials and prep. Start organizing your personal statement, letters of recommendation, and any portfolio work. Three, Set deadlines. Put key dates (like application due dates or test registrations) into a calendar or reminder app. It's also wise to schedule your own personal deadline a bit earlier so you have a buffer if something unexpected comes up. Four, Schedule your study time. Instead of cramming for entrance exams a week before, set aside daily or weekly sessions to review material. By breaking these things down, you can track your progress in smaller increments.

title: How to Connect
transcript: How to Find and Apply to Community College Before Applying Applying to a community college is usually a straightforward process. The tricky part is choosing the right one with the right courses for your goals. That's why it's so helpful to know your end goal in advance. Do you want to earn a certificate or associate? Do you intend to transfer to a four year college or university? Do you plan to go full-time or part-time? Do you want to attend in person, online, or both via a hybrid class or program? Note, some courses require in-person attendance, so students can gain real world skills. Knowing your purpose, schedule, and manner of attendance is key to selecting the right school. Once you're ready, it's time to look at your local community colleges. Find out if they offer programs that align with your interests and career goals. If you plan to attend online, you won't have to worry as much about where the school is located, but if it's in a different state, make sure to determine if they charge you in-state tuition or out-of-state tuition. Visit Campuses If you plan to attend in person, try to visit the campus to get a sense of the environment and facilities. Check if there are guided campus tour dates you can attend. Check Accreditation Make sure the community college is accredited because that can affect credit transferability and employability. Review Admissions Requirements Review the school's admissions criteria carefully, such as high school GPA requirements, placement tests for subjects like English or math, proof of residency, et cetera. Review Program Requirements Some programs may have requirements beyond those of the school itself. Make sure to read them carefully before applying. Financial Aid and Scholarships Apply for financial aid through the FAFSA process and search for scholarships or grants you might be eligible for including school sponsored scholarships. The Application Process Complete Application Carefully fill out the application form, which may be available online on the college's website. For entries that require long responses, such as essay questions, we suggest writing them out on a separate document, editing and spell checking, then cutting and pasting them into the online form. At the time of submission, there's usually an application fee you must pay. In some cases, this can be waived, but check with the school. Submit Transcripts and Other Materials Request your official high school transcripts or equivalents, and have them sent to the college's admissions office by the listed deadline. Don't wait until the last minute to order them because it can take time for official transcripts to be prepared and mailed out. Upon Acceptance Attend Orientation New student orientation is a helpful way to get acquainted with campus resources and policies, so try to attend. Meet with an Advisor Schedule an appointment with your academic advisor to discuss your educational plans and get help with suitable course selection. Take Placement Tests If the school requires placement tests prior to registering for classes, be sure to knock those out as soon as you can, but leave enough time to study! Register for Classes Once accepted and advised, register for classes in accordance with your academic plan. Buy Textbooks and Supplies When you register, you should get details about the textbooks or supplies you'll need. Many students buy used books or rent them versus buying them, but be sure to select the correct book additions. Pay Tuition and Fees Now it's time for everyone's favorite part, paying tuition and fees. Schedule an appointment with the financial aid office if you need assistance, such as ensuring scholarship funds are flowing smoothly, or if you need to establish a payment plan. Be careful not to miss the payment deadlines. Prepare for the First Day The first day of classes is always an exciting and sometimes stressful time. Plan ahead so you'll feel more relaxed. Make sure your schedule is organized, your commute to class is laid out, and you have everything you'll need, such as a notebook, pen, charged laptop, et cetera.

title: Types of Connections
transcript: Types of Connections There's a popular theory that between any two people on earth, there are only six degrees of separation or less. For instance, between two friends, there is one degree of separation. A friend of a friend is two degrees of separation and so on. LinkedIn uses this concept when labeling user connections, so if you see first, second, or third plus by someone's name, those numbers represent the type of connection you have with the person. First degree connections are people you have connected with on the site. You can message them directly. Second degree connections are the first degree connections of your first degree connections. Third degree connections are connected to your second degree connections.

title: LinkedIn: The "Skills" and "Recommendations" Section Intro
transcript: Adding Recommendations Recommendations go beyond endorsements because they allow first person connections to write whatever they want in your profile. So it may go without saying, but before you ask someone to do this, be sure the person you ask is inclined to write a strong recommendation. Of course, LinkedIn does allow users to hide recommendations that they don't want to be viewed publicly. There are two ways to request a recommendation. Option 1: Click the My Network tab at the top of the site. Then navigate to your connections. From there, select someone's profile. Click the more button on their profile, and you should see an option to request a recommendation. Option 2: Request a recommendation directly from your profile. If you've never done this before, click the add profile section on your profile, then select recommended and add recommendations. From there, you can type in the name of the person you'd like to ask. If you've had a recommendation before, you should see a recommendation section already on your profile. So from there, just click Ask to be recommended and follow the instructions. Note, LinkedIn recommendations have up to 3000 characters, which is plenty of room to write a glowing report. However, there's nothing wrong with keeping things short and impactful. Here's an example of a solid recommendation. "As the founder of Geo Lad, I had the distinct pleasure of working with and overseeing Wade on a variety of projects. During his time with our company, he exhibited great initiative in opening our first ever internship program to recruit college students. His efforts help to provide them with invaluable experiences while growing their professional networks. Always up to try new things, Wade loves learning fresh skills and understands that hard work is the key to executing a successful product. The phrase I don't know how to do that, isn't in his vocabulary. He's incredibly versatile and we relied on his resourcefulness countless times to find unique solutions to problems. During our startup phase, he has creativity, always empowered him to think outside the box. Wade carries himself with confidence and professionalism beyond his years, while exhibiting humility and a commitment to teamwork. We could always trust him to share his ideas and when needed to take feedback and return with even better ones. It's been a true pleasure working and getting to know Wade whose opinion I still seek out whenever we have critical decisions to make related to our branding and marketing. The example we just covered is clear, concise, and highly complimentary without going overboard. It also lists specific skills such as initiative, versatility, creativity, confidence, professionalism, teamwork and openness to criticism. Now it's time for a quick knowledge check to practice what we just went over.

title: Etiquette & Tips for Adding LinkedIn Connections
transcript: Etiquette and tips for adding LinkedIn connections LinkedIn has over 900 million users around the world with only about one third of them actively using the site on a monthly basis. So when it comes to connecting with other professionals, where do you begin? To answer that, think about your purpose for using the site in the first place. People use LinkedIn for different reasons. For example, employers use it to scout for new workers. Job seekers use it to look for work and to advertise themselves to potential employers. Entrepreneurs use it to grow their businesses and showcase their products and services. Many people use it to learn new things and socialize. As a student, odds are, you'll want to use LinkedIn to grow your network, look for jobs, and maybe learn new things. So let's strategize about how to use the platform to reach your specific goals.

title: Engaging Respectfully in the Comments
transcript: Engaging respectfully in the comments Besides posting and sharing content yourself, you should also be active in responding to others' posts. Participating in discussions on LinkedIn can enhance your visibility and in turn, help you expand your network. Just remember, when commenting, always be professional and respectful. Your comments become part of your online digital footprint and can be viewed by anyone. Ready to start posting? Few other platforms offer the raw potential to connect to some of the most influential professionals around the world. From congresspeople to corporate CEOs, entrepreneurs, and global influencers, LinkedIn is where leaders go to to share long form content and discuss ideas in a respectful environment. Regular, strategic posting on LinkedIn can absolutely contribute to your professional image, help you make valuable connections and support your long-term career growth.

title: Adding Skills
transcript: Adding Skills In chapter three, we reviewed common hard and soft skills that employers are looking for. You can also figure out the best skills to add by researching current job postings related to your field of study and the type of job that you want. For example, indeed.com currently lists a job advertisement for a staff chemist. The employer has included the key qualifications applicants must possess, such as a college degree. But most of the qualifications listed are skills like effective verbal and written communication, self-motivation, flexibility, teamwork, and good safety discipline. These skills might seem generic, but employers are very serious about wanting candidates with these types of qualifications. Of course, job applicants need the right educational background, but what good is having a college degree if they can't explain their thoughts clearly or collaborate well with others. So as you add relevant skills to your profile, keep in mind what employers are looking for. Put yourself in their shoes. This will help demonstrate that you're a great candidate.

title: Writing Engaging and Impactful Posts
transcript: Writing engaging and impactful posts. If you're gonna take the time to write something, make sure it'll be interesting to your potential readers. So, if you're trying to gain followers, write things that are value added for them. If you're trying to establish your reputation, write well-researched thought leadership pieces relevant to your career path. If you're a job seeker trying to impress potential employers, include posts about your accomplishments and industry trends. Whoever your target audience is, put yourself in their shoes. What information do you want them to take away? What do you want them to learn about you or about the thing you're posting about? Use clear active language to get your message across and focus on impacts and results.

title: Crafting Posts with Professionsal Flair
transcript: Crafting posts with professional flare. A common complaint about LinkedIn is that posts sometimes seem phony or insincere. That's probably because many people focus so much on trying to sound professional, that they forget to be authentic. Being professional doesn't mean you have to act like someone you're not. When writing a post, be genuine. Be true to yourself and let your personality shine through, but do it all with an appropriate style, like you'd use around a teacher or manager.

title: What to Post on LinkedIn
transcript: What to post on LinkedIn In this module, we'll review what types of content are suitable for posting on LinkedIn and what to avoid.

title: Volunteer Section
transcript: ﻿1 Volunteer Section Entry. Organization. Name the organization you volunteered for. Role. List the title of your volunteer position. Cause. What cause did you volunteer to support? Start Date. List the month and year. End Date. List the month and year. If you don't know a specific date, just use an approximate date. If you're still filling the volunteer role, you'd just put a check in the box that says "I am currently volunteering in this role." Description. What did you do in your volunteering experience? For example, "I raise funds for our annual charity 5K run" or "I work the visitor booth of a local trade show." Media. For an actual profile, you could add media such as images, documents, or presentations here. Note, for the sake of this exercise, you don't need to upload media, but please list an example of what you could upload.

title: Celebrate Small Wins
transcript: ﻿1 Celebrate Small Wins. People often overlook celebrating small achievements because they're so focused on the end goal. But every time you hit a milestone, like filming your first YouTube video, or acing a quiz in a tough subject, it's important to acknowledge it. Celebrating these moments doesn't mean throwing a huge party every time you finish a single homework assignment, though that could be fun. It just means taking a moment to appreciate what you've accomplished. Small celebrations can be treating yourself to your favorite snack, taking a relaxing break, like going for a walk, or reading a chapter of a non-school book. Sharing your success with someone who cares, like a friend, parent, or mentor. Writing down what you learned and how you feel about the progress. Acknowledging your mini-successes provides quick hits of motivation and reminds you that you're on the right track. It feels good, and that good feeling can keep you pushing forward.

title: Balancing Ambition with Reality
transcript: ﻿1 Balancing Ambition with Reality. We often hear motivational phrases like, "Dream Big" or "Go hard or Go home". And while enthusiasm is great it has to be balanced with realistic timelines and expectations. If you give yourself too little time or expect yourself to make leaps that aren't possible yet, you could get discouraged. For example, if you want to run a marathon but have never even run a mile, it's not realistic to sign up for a 26.2-mile race next month. Instead, you'd gradually build your endurance - maybe start with a goal of running a mile without stopping, then 5K, then 10K, and so on. Each smaller goal you achieve makes the bigger goal feel more possible. And each time you hit a milestone, you'll feel that jolt of pride and excitement telling you, "Hey, I can do this!"

title: Dealing With Setbacks
transcript: Dealing with AI Bias Can AI be biased? Of course it can because it's created by humans. It's also trained using inputs by humans or by analyzing data created by humans. Thus, it's almost unavoidable that AI programs will pick up certain biases along the way. This can lead to unfair outcomes in some situations like job recruitment or law enforcement. As we continue to incorporate AI into our lives, we must keep in mind that AI is far from perfect. Like humans, it's not always fair, objective, or accurate.

title: Knowledge Check
transcript: ﻿1 Connecting Passions and Interests: Knowledge Check. Now that we've explored how passions and interests guide us toward meaningful careers, let's see how your passions and interests align by answering a few key questions. Think carefully about what excites and motivates you, and how your values and goals connect to your interests. When you're ready, start the knowledge check.

title: How to Write the "About" Section
transcript: How to write your LinkedIn profile's About section. LinkedIn is a fantastic platform for professional networking. It's a valuable tool for promoting yourself and getting the attention of potential employers, clients, customers, or followers. But your profile has to capture people's attention. It must present your experiences in a way that compels others to reach out and connect with you. LinkedIn profiles features several elements, including an about section where you can write anything you want. This critical section allows up to 2,600 characters worth of text. That gives you roughly 500 words to play around with, which is more than enough if you keep things clear and concise. So let's review some tips about writing an amazing about section for your LinkedIn profile.

title: Tips for "About" Section
transcript: Tips for your LinkedIn profile's About section. Rather than reinventing the wheel, we borrowed from LinkedIn's own list of best practices and interpreted them to apply to our Gladeo audiences. So without further ado, let's dive in. Tip 1: Describe what makes you tick. If an employer asked you, what motivates you to succeed in your professional life, what would you say? Put some thought into it. Then write a few versions of your response. Describe your motivations in clear, compelling terms that you'd feel comfortable sharing with everyone. Tip 2: Explain your present role. What are you currently up to? If you're a student, write an explanation of your studies in areas of interest. If you're working, write about your typical duties and the impact you're making. Explain who your end customers are and how your work helps them. Tip 3: Frame your past. When it comes to writing about ourselves, our past can be defined however we choose. It's your profile. So how would you summarize your past professional experiences up to this point. What do you want readers to know and take away? Tip 4: Highlight your successes. What are the highlights of your past accomplishments in your own brief words? For example, if you had to list your proudest moments and achievements, how would you phrase them? There isn't room to list everything, so focus on the big stuff. Now, how can you connect those things to your present role and responsibilities? Tip 5: Reveal your character. We all have unique personalities, but we also have something called character. "Character refers to the person's moral and ethical qualities." says Dr. Tara Lally. Character consists of beliefs and moral principles that can guide their behavior in discrete ways. Think about the internal core beliefs that define your character. Then find a way to reveal these within the text of your profile. Tip 6: Show life outside work. LinkedIn is a platform for busy professionals, but there's more to life than work. Offer a glimpse of who you are beyond your professional persona. Share a bit about your hobbies or interests, something that's relatable and humanizes you. Be genuine and let your personality shine. Tip 7: Tell stories. People are drawn to stories and the best stories are always character driven. Tell the world about yourself by sharing a short story that reveals insights into who you are and what you stand for. Tip 8: Ask for what you want. In marketing, there's a term called call to action. This is a simple bit of language designed to prompt the audience to take a specific action, such as hit that subscribe button now. You can use a call to action in your LinkedIn profile too. Decide what action you want people to take. Then include it as a short sentence, such as Follow me for news and updates, or Send me a message and let's chat.

title: Freelancing Websites
transcript: Freelancing Websites Online freelancing is a great way to gain experience while getting paid. Based on current trends, the top 10 most popular sites freelancers use to find gigs or short-term jobs and projects are Upwork, Fiverr, Freelancer.com, Toptal, Guru PeoplePerHour, 99Designs, Behance, Flex Jobs, and Simply Hired. Not all the gigs posted on these sites are short term, and there are numerous other platforms available such as TaskRabbit and SolidGigs. You can also search for freelance opportunities on traditional job portals like Indeed, LinkedIn, Glassdoor, and Monster. Note. Some sites are geared towards specific types of work. For example, 99Designs and Behance are mainly for graphic design or other visual arts related gigs.

title: What is Freelance Work?
transcript: ﻿1 What is Self-Advocacy? Self-advocacy means being able to explain what you need- clearly, calmly, and with confidence. It's not about demanding special treatment. It's about sharing facts and solutions that help you thrive. There are three core pieces. Know yourself. Understand how your disability shows up for you- and what you are great at. Know your rights. Laws like IDEA, Section 50 , and the ADA protect your access to fair support. Speak up. The way you ask matters. Being clear and confident helps people understand and take action. One way to speak up clearly is by using a simple, goal-oriented script. Here's how to build one: One. Start with your disability. For example: "I have ADHD." Two. Explain how it affects your learning or work, which means it's hard for me to focus when taking quizzes in a loud environment. Three. Describe what support helps. Having access to a quiet testing room makes a big difference. Four. Add the goal that support helps you reach, "so I can stay focused and do my best during exams." Let's put it all together. "I have ADHD, which means it's hard for me to focus when taking quizzes in a loud environment. Having access to a quiet testing room makes a big difference, so I can stay focused and do my best during exams." This is a goal-oriented, clear, and concise statement because it tells the listener what your disability is, how it impacts you, what support you need, and how that support helps you succeed. Let's look at a few more examples. For example, Camilla has dyslexia and has trouble keeping up with lecture slides being presented quickly during class. She needs to bring this up to her professors so she can fully participate in discussions. What's the clearest way to communicate that? A. "I have dyslexia, which means I read slowly. It helps when I get lecture slides in advance, so I can participate fully in class discussions." B. "I can't keep up with your slides. You need to slow down or give me the materials ahead of time." C. "I have a disability, so you're supposed to give me accommodations like slides or something." D. "Hi, just wondering if I could maybe get the slide sometime? If not, that's okay too." The correct answer is A. It clearly explains the barrier and offers a practical solution in a respectful, goal-oriented way. B is too accusatory. C is vague and entitled. D is polite but doesn't explain the need or here's another example. Jordan has anxiety, especially during fast-paced meetings. He wants to ask his supervisor for something that will help him prepare and participate more fully. What's the best way to say it? A. "I have anxiety, which makes it harder to speak up in fast-paced meetings. It helps when I get the agenda ahead of time, so I can prepare and contribute." B. Meetings make me anxious. I just need you to slow down or skip me." C. I don't do well in meetings, so maybe I can skip them or get someone to go for me." D. "Could I maybe get the agenda ahead of time? No worries if not!" The correct answer is A. It's clear, respectful, and action-focused. B points only to discomfort without a plan. C avoids the situation instead of addressing it. D asks nicely but lacks context or urgency. Now that you've seen two examples in action, try practicing one that fits your own situation. Say it out loud, tweak it, try again. Clear, confident self-advocacy isn't about being pushy- it's about being real. You are explaining what helps you show up and succeed.

title: Meet Claudia - Asset Manager
transcript: Claudia, thank you so much for joining us today. No problem. Thank you for having me. In your words, what is an asset manager? So an asset manager takes care of an owner's investments in a property, whether it be residential, commercial. The goal of an asset, asset managers to improve the value of the property by managing the property managers, and the leasing agents, make sure that the operating expenses are low as possible and all of the rents are as high as possible by negotiating leases at a higher rental price. You were the first person in your family to go to college, right? Yes. Amazing. So without those connections, how did you get your foot in the door and how did you navigate that process? So when, when I was still an undergrad, I applied for a file clerk position just in order to get exposure to the business world because I knew I was gonna need a full-time job once I graduated and that was my first exposure to the professional world. And especially on the commercial real estate side The, the firm specializes in retail and office. And I got my foot in the door by remaining focused, being a hard worker, and speaking out. So when I realized I had the capabilities of becoming an asset manager, I approach, approached my, my boss and asked him to be considered for the position. So it was a matter of taking control of my own hands and moving forward with that and really going after what I wanted. You've been in this industry for a while now. Correct. I've been in the industry for 18 years as an asset manager, and two of those years was as a file clerk, doing it part-time while I was still an undergrad. Okay. So what is your favorite part of this job? Oh man. Favorite part of this job is running the numbers. I love math. So it does require quite a bit of number crunching and I love interacting with people. So in managing the property managers, I have a lot of different people, different personalities, managing the leasing agents, same deal, and of course, controllers, and other folks that go into it. And you, you interact with your tenants as well, so. So as one of the few women in this industry, do you have any advice for young women who are hoping to pursue a career in asset management? Don't be shy. Go out there. Ask questions. Contact folks that, that you may be interested to speak with. Ask them to have coffee and ask a few questions. Good. That's incredible. Well, thank you so much for your time today and sharing a little bit about your wonderful career and yourself. Of course. Thank you so much. You're very welcome.

title: Knowledge Check Instructions Video
transcript: ﻿1 Connecting Passions and Interests: Knowledge Check. Now that we've explored how passions and interests guide us toward meaningful careers, let's see how your passions and interests align by answering a few key questions. Think carefully about what excites and motivates you, and how your values and goals connect to your interests. When you're ready, start the knowledge check.
