Janay, 함께해 주셔서 감사합니다.

title: How to Write a Great LinkedIn Experience Section
transcript: How to write a great LinkedIn Experience section. Just as important as your LinkedIn profile's About section is the Experience section under it. Though it doesn't specifically say so, this section is about your work experiences and it functions similarly to a resume. Let's go over some tips for using this section effectively.

title: Tips for "Experience" Section
transcript: Bonus tips. Before you dive into writing your experience section, let's review a few bonus tips. First, add skills. For every employment period you add, LinkedIn lets you select five skills you use for that job. Add media. LinkedIn also lets you add media such as images, presentations, or documents. You can even drop a link to an external website. Review other profiles. Look up a few successful professionals to see how they phrase their LinkedIn profiles. These individuals don't necessarily have to be in the same industry as you. Add keywords. Incorporate relevant keywords into your profile to make it more search friendly. If you're not sure which keywords to use, scan some job descriptions or job posts related to your position. Highlight the keywords that stand out. Use powerful action verbs. Your profile needs to be stocked with plenty of action verbs. Some examples of this include: Achieved, as in achieved a 100% success rate. Strategized, as in strategized effective plans. Optimized, as in optimized conversations. Generated, as in generated 5 million in sales. Quantify achievements include quantifiable data such as dollar amounts, percentages, or statistics. For example, if you manage an inventory, list the number of items or the dollar value of the inventory as a whole. Now that we've discussed what goes on in the experience section, it's time to take the knowledge check before we move on.

title: Examples
transcript: ﻿1 Real-World Examples About Learning From Failure. Sometimes the best way to see how failure fuels growth is by looking at those who've stumbled, learned, and forged a new path forward. Let's take a look at three Gen Z icons who turned setbacks into stepping stones. Chappell Roan. Dropped by her record label, Chappell Roan refused to quit. She kept creating music independently, eventually building a passionate fan base and reigniting her career on her own terms. Most recently, she won her first Grammy in 2025 for "Best New Artist," solidifying her place in the pop music pantheon. Gitanjali Rao. Named "Kid of the Year" by time in 2020, this teen inventor faced skepticism and trial-and-error while developing "Tethys," a device that detects lead in water. By refining her prototypes and learning from each hurdle, she inspired other young innovators worldwide. Jack Andraka. In high school, Andraka endured repeated rejections while working on an early test for pancreatic cancer. Undeterred, he refined his research, found a supportive lab, and ultimately created a breakthrough test proving that resilience can overcome even the steepest obstacles. These stories underscore the fact that failure doesn't have to be final. Often, it's the trial run that reveals what you need to adjust to make your next attempt successful. By normalizing failure as part of the path, you remove much of its sting and discover that it can actually be a powerful catalyst for achievement.

title: How to Make the Most of Your Internship
transcript: How to make the most of your internship To get the most out of your internship, keep these tips in mind. Engage in meaningful work. Today's internships often involve exciting project-based tasks. Take ownership of your project and show that you can handle responsibility. Seek learning opportunities. Be proactive, whether it's asking to sit in on meetings or requesting more tasks, the more you learn, the more you'll grow. Build your network. Make connections, whether it's with coworkers or mentors. These relationships can support you long after your internship ends. Document your work. Keep a record of your achievements. This will come in handy when updating your resume and discussing your experience in future job interviews. Reflect on your experience. Take time to think about what you've learned. What did you enjoy? What skills did you develop? Use these insights to guide your next steps. Speaking of which, internships are no longer just a stepping stone. They're a critical component of career development that provides invaluable experience, skills, and connections. By approaching your internship with the right mindset and a proactive attitude, you can maximize the opportunities it presents and set yourself up for long-term success.

title: Ways to Support Yourself During an Internship
transcript: Ways to Support Yourself During an Internship If you're taking on an unpaid internship, you're not alone. There are plenty of ways to ease the financial strain: scholarships and grants. Many organizations offer scholarships and grants specifically for students taking on unpaid internships. Sites like Fastweb and Scholarships.com can help you get started. University Support Some schools offer stipends or academic credit to help students with internship costs. Be sure to ask your career services office what support they provide. Part-time Work Juggling a part-time job can help cover expenses without cutting too much into your internship time. Flexible work like tutoring or freelancing can be a great option. Crowdfunding and sponsorships. Some students use platforms like GoFundMe to raise money for their internships, especially if they're tied to a cause that others want to support. Living Arrangements Staying with family or friends can help you save on rent, or you can find a roommate or two to rent a space and save money together. Facebook groups, Nextdoor and Zillow are all platforms that can help you find a good fit. Just be sure to do your due diligence with credit checks and living vibes. And don't forget, some companies offer housing stipends, so don't be shy about asking when you're applying. But for most, the optimal path is to find unpaid internships while you're still in high school and have fewer living expenses, and then focus on paid internships when you're living on your own.

title: Compensations, Pros, and Cons
transcript: Pros and cons of going to community college. The pros of attending a community college depend on your goals and circumstances. Common benefits include affordability. Community college can be a good option for students who want to save money. Lower tuition reduces overall education costs and potential debt. Keep in mind, students who plan on transferring should make sure their credits will actually transfer. It doesn't save money if you have to retake courses. Flexibility. Community college students are often busy with work, family, or other obligations, so the flexibility of part-time and online classes is convenient. Also, community colleges offer a range of courses that allow students to explore different fields before committing to a specific area of study. Admissions Four year colleges and universities can be tough to get into, especially if your high school GPA isn't super competitive. It should be easier to get into a community college. Academic Improvement Classes at four year colleges can be academically challenging. Community college offers an easier transition. This helps students maintain better grades, making their GPA more competitive by the time they apply to transfer. Small Classes Four year college classes are often jam packed with students while community college classrooms are smaller. This allows for more personalized attention from faculty, which can help raise grades. Community Feel Community colleges often feel more inclusive because students tend to be from the local area, whereas universities may have students from other states or countries. Focused Support Services Many community colleges feature focused support in tutoring, academic counseling, and job seeking skills. Career Training Several programs are tailored to thoroughly prepare students for careers after graduation. They may also grant industry recognized credentials or prepare students for industry related exams required to work in a profession or to gain state licensure. Potential Downsides to Community College The potential cons to community college depend on the individual. For some students, there are no cons at all, but possible downsides include Class Availability Smaller classes fill up faster, so students may have to register early to get the classes they want. Limited Campus Life Community colleges don't offer the range of activities, clubs, and events that larger campuses might, but that's not always a negative thing, since it can help students focus on their academics. Fewer Degree Options Most community colleges don't offer degrees above the associates level. Some states have a few community colleges with career oriented bachelor's degree programs, but this is relatively rare and the major options are limited. In addition, no community colleges offer graduate programs. Post-Graduation Career Options Degree requirements vary by career and by employer. Many employers want applicants to hold a bachelor's even if the job duties don't necessarily require it. That said, many community college grads start working, gain some experience, then return to college later to finish a higher level degree. Transfer Issues If a community college student knows they plan to transfer, it is important to get the timing right. The process of transferring to a four year university could potentially prolong the time it takes to finish a bachelor's degree. Credit Transfer Issues Certain credits may not transfer, thus affecting degree completion time. Competitive schools may be less likely to accept a transfer application, so it's important to do your research ahead of time. Less Research Community colleges offer varying degrees of undergraduate research opportunities, but they are typically less funded or emphasized. Fewer Resources Compared to larger institutions, community colleges tend to have fewer facilities, IT resources and student services in general.

title: When Internships Start
transcript: When internships start and how long they last. Good news, internships are available year round. Whether you're looking for something during the fall, spring, or even all year, there's usually an option that fits your schedule. Most internships last 8 to 12 weeks. Giving you plenty of time to dive into meaningful projects. When choosing an internship, make sure to factor in how long it lasts and how it fits with your education and career plans.

title: Ways to Find Internships
transcript: Ways to Find Internships Internships are available in just about every industry, from tech and finance, to the arts, healthcare, non-profits, and more. If you're ready to find your next big opportunity, here's some helpful places to start. Online Platforms Sites like LinkedIn, Indeed and Glassdoor, make it easy to search for internships by industry, location, and more. Company websites. Got a dream company in mind? Head straight to their careers page to see if they're offering internships. University Career Services. If you're a student, your school's career center likely has exclusive internship listings and connections with alumni who can help you land an opportunity. Plus, career fairs are a great place to meet potential employers in person. Industry-Specific Job Boards Some fields have dedicated job boards for internships. For instance, check out Media Bistro for media roles or Idealists for nonprofits and social impact jobs. Networking. Never underestimate the power of talking to people, whether it's attending industry events or reaching out on LinkedIn, your network can be a treasure trove of opportunities.

title: Why Internships?
transcript: Why internships? In a classroom, you learn the theory, but an internship lets you dive into the real thing. By working alongside professionals, you'll pick up essential workplace skills like communication, teamwork, and problem solving. Plus, you'll gain a clearer understanding of how things work in a professional environment, which can be a game changer when it's time to apply for jobs. Here's why internships are so valuable. Skill development. You'll get to practice what you've learned in school and sharpen skills that are essential in your field. Career exploration. Not sure what you want to do yet? Internships let you try out different roles and industries so you can figure out what excites you most. Networking opportunities. Internships are an opportunity to meet people in your industry. These connections can help with mentorship, job references, or even future job offers. Increased employability. Employers prefer candidates who already have some experience, and an internship can help you stand out in a competitive job market. It's often the key that opens doors after graduation.

title: Personal Analytics
transcript: Personal Analytics Everyone looks for ways to be physically and mentally healthier. We also all look for ways to do our work better so we can be happier and make more money. AI helps with these efforts thanks to personal data analytics apps. These apps dig into our data to find our strengths and areas where we can improve. They give us insights into ourselves that we can use to boost our career performance and gain competitive advantages. Some examples include Breathhh a Google Chrome extension that tracks our online activity to suggest stress reducing breathing exercises, Growth Mindset AI Coach, a virtual coach that helps users reach their goals. And Wysa, an anonymous app that offers mental wellness support.

title: Intro to Internships
transcript: ﻿1 Developing Career Skills The goal of seeking advanced education and training is to prepare for a career. A career is not the same as a job. A job is a specific position of employment. While a career is a long-term professional journey with progressive development and growth. Many workers switch jobs over the course of their careers. As they grow professionally and develop new skills. As they progress on their career trajectories, they can qualify for higher earnings, better opportunities and options with increased job satisfaction and security. Education and training are the keys to career success. They provide the foundational knowledge and skills needed to grow and adapt in an ever changing job market. Employers of today and tomorrow are looking for applicants with a mix of hard and soft skills, practical experience, and academic knowledge. Unlike hard technical skills, soft skills like effective communication and attention to detail are developed over time through personal experiences, social interactions, and both formal and informal education. It's time to explore opportunities in high school.

title: Building Work Experience For Getting a Job
transcript: Building Work Experience for Getting a Job Starting your career is one of the biggest challenges you'll ever face. A big reason why is because employers want entry-level candidates to have some relevant work experience. But how can you get experience before actually getting your first job? That's the classic paradox job seekers often find themselves in. In fact, it's such a common problem, we've devoted this chapter to helping you overcome it. In the next modules, we'll review how you can build relevant work experience through internships, freelancing, volunteerism, and online work-based projects. These experiences will empower you to develop marketable skills and boost your resume so it'll stand out from the crowd. We suggest starting as early as possible. Ideally, this means getting involved in high school, if you can. Why so early? Because these experiences can affect your career interests and passions. That, in turn, will impact your career choice and the education and training you'll need for it. And in chapter 9, we'll talk more about opportunities available for high school students. Starting early will also enable you to begin making valuable connections, building social capital that'll come in handy later. Having a large well-established network can be an incredibly powerful resource. But no matter when you decide to start building your work experience, the key is to take the initiative. Don't wait for things to just happen. Be proactive, find opportunities, and make the most of them. Go out and make great things happen in your career. Now, let's dig in and explore exciting ways you can gain experience and give your career a jump start.

title: Keep Going One Step at a Time
transcript: ﻿1 Keep Going One Step At A Time. Remember, setting realistic goals isn't about holding yourself back. It's about giving yourself achievable steps that build momentum. Once you prove to yourself you can hit your smaller targets, your self-confidence grows. That confidence helps you take on bigger challenges in the future. It's also okay to adjust your goals as you learn more. Maybe you discover a new interest or realize the timeline you set is too tight. That's all part of the process. The important thing is to keep moving forward in a way that feels doable and motivating, rather than overwhelming. So go ahead - dream big, but don't forget to create those stepping-stone goals that show you a clear path forward. Each small win is like a puzzle piece that eventually completes the whole picture. And when that picture finally comes together, you'll see how every little milestone helped you become more resilient, more determined, and more capable than ever before. By breaking larger goals into smaller, attainable milestones and celebrating each step, you'll develop the resilience and confidence to tackle anything life throws your way - now and throughout your career journey!

title: Adding Endorsements
transcript: Adding Endorsements Alright, let's say you added some incredible skills to your profile. How does anyone verify that you actually have these skills? One way to offer proof is through third party endorsements. LinkedIn allows your connections to endorse skills listed on your profile, which is a great way to show employers that you've been vetted. As the platform itself notes, when a connection endorses your skills, it contributes to the strength of your profile and increases the likelihood that you'll be discovered for opportunities related to the skills you possess. Ideally, you should only ask for endorsements from people with firsthand experience working with you as an employer, supervisor, coworker, or professor. If it's a peer, you might offer to return the favor and endorse some of their skills as well. To request a skill endorsement, all you have to do is click the more icon on your profile, then select request a recommendation and follow the instructions.

title: Virtual Assistants
transcript: Virtual Assistants Virtual AI assistants keep us organized and on track. They manage our schedules, prioritize our things to-do lists, and remind us of appointments and due dates. With AI's help, we're able to save time and energy that we can use for career readiness and development. Popular AI assistants include Amazon's Alexa, Apple Siri, Google Assistant, Microsoft's Cortana. There are also specialized programs such as DataBot, a virtual talking "protocol droid". Elsa Speak for English language learning. Fireflies for meeting note taking. Fyle, for expense management. Hound, a voice assistant. Socratic, for homework help. And Youper, an emotional health assistant.

title: Welcome to FutureReady
transcript: ﻿1 Introduction to Self-Awareness. As students, you're doing your best to get through school so you can be prepared for your future. But what do you want to do in your future" And who do you want to be? Sometimes that's hard to figure out.. especially when we don't fully understand who we are now. So before you start exploring career paths and the required education and training for them, in this chapter, let's explore YOU! This chapter is all about you getting to know yourself better. Discovering what you truly want in life begins with exploring who you are, what you believe, and what matters most to you. Self-awareness is a lifelong journey, and each step helps you understand yourself a little better. To do that, you'll have to deep dive and explore your core values, motivations, and priorities. And also your interests, skills, and aptitude. Being self-aware also involves knowing your goals in life, your own definition of success, and how you learn from experiences. All these unique variables weave together to make you the fascinating person you currently are. And like the rest of us, "who you are" changes and evolves as you get older. Getting to know yourself better will help you align your future career choices with who you truly are. The more you understand yourself, the more confidently you can explore the world of career possibilities. But remember, exploring your own self-awareness isn't a one-time process; it's a lifelong journey.

title: Let Technology Help You and Not Hurt You
transcript: Let technology help you and not hurt you Technology is everywhere. It connects us with friends, helps us learn new things. It can even open doors to future opportunities like finding a job or getting into college. But while technology offers many benefits, it can also cause problems if we're not careful. For example, spending too much time online can distract you from important things and even affect your mental health. That's why it's so important to learn how to use technology wisely. In this chapter, we'll explore some tips on how to make the most out of the tools you have while avoiding the pitfalls. But we also need to think about another aspect of our digital lives. Every time you go online, you leave behind a trail of information. This digital footprint includes everything from your social media posts to the websites you visit, and it can have a big impact on your future. Employers and college admissions officers may look at your online presence to learn more about you, so it's important to make sure your digital footprint shows the best version of you. This chapter will also touch on protecting your digital footprint, so you can stay safe and make sure that your online presence reflects who you really are. Let's get started on learning how to use technology in a smart way and protect yourself online.

title: College vs. Work Quick Comparison
transcript: ﻿1 College versus Work. Quick Comparison. The steps for requesting accommodations are similar wherever you go. But the process feels different in college versus at work. Here's a quick breakdown: In college, you go through the Disability Services Office- usually called the DSO. You'll need documentation like a psycho-educational evaluation or a letter from a doctor. Once you're approved, the DSO helps coordinate support like extra test time, note taking tools, or text-to-speech software. They also help you navigate issues with faculty if something doesn't go as planned. In the workplace, you are in charge of your request! You'll reach out to your manager or HR department directly. They might ask for a short note from your doctor, but usually not a full evaluation. Common supports at work include things like modified schedules, noise-reducing equipment, or a more accessible workspace. You and HR work together to find a solution that fits. The key difference? In college, the DSO is your built-in advocate. At work, you start the conversation- but HR is there to help make it happen. Both settings are backed by the ADA- different parts apply depending on where you are. Title I of the ADA applies to employment. It protects you when you're requesting accommodations at work and requires employers with 15 or more staff to provide reasonable adjustments, unless doing so causes significant difficulty or cost. For education, Title II covers state and local public colleges, while Title III covers private colleges and universities, ensuring both must provide equal access in their programs and services.

title: Real-World Scenario: Work
transcript: ﻿1 Real World Scenario. Brianna at Work. Brianna just started her first full-time job at a busy media company. She has ADHD, and the noisy open office makes it hard to focus. By week two, she's falling behind on deadlines and feeling overwhelmed. Instead of powering through, Brianna emails HR: Subject: Accommodation Request. "Hi, I'm reaching out to request reasonable accommodation under the ADA. I have a documented disability, ADHD, that affects my focus in loud environments. I'd like to explore a quieter workspace option or the possibility of working remotely one day a week. I'm happy to provide documentation if needed. Thanks for your time. Brianna." HR replies within a few days. They ask for a short letter from her doctor, then schedule a meeting to discuss options. Together, they decide Brianna will move to a desk in a quieter area and work from home every Wednesday. One email started a conversation and made her job sustainable.

title: Work: Step by Step Accommodations Request
transcript: ﻿1 Step by Step. College Accommodation Request. Getting accommodations in college takes a few clear steps- but you don't have to figure it out alone. Almost every college has a Disability Services Office, or DSO, whose job is to make sure students with documented disabilities get equal access. Here's how to get started: One. Find and register with the DSO. Look on your college's website. Search for Disability Services or Accessibility Office. Every school calls it something slightly different, but it's usually part of Student Affairs or Academic Support. Reach out early, ideally before classes begin. Two. Submit your documentation. You'll usually need to provide official paperwork that explains your disability. This might be a psychoeducational evaluation, which is a detailed report from a licensed psychologist that explores how you process information and learn or it could be a medical letter from a healthcare provider that outlines your diagnosis and how it affects your daily activities. Whatever the format, the documentation should clearly describe how your disability impacts your learning or daily life, and includes specific recommendations for support. Such as extended test time, access to assistive technology, or flexible deadlines. Three. Attend an intake meeting. This is a conversation- not a test. You'll discuss what barriers you've faced such as: note-taking, test anxiety, or sensory issues, and explore what might help. Be honest and specific. The DSO staff knows how to translate challenges into legally supported accommodations. Four. Receive your accommodation letter. Once approved, you'll get a letter listing your accommodations, like extended time, lecture recordings, or captioning. You are in charge of sharing this letter with your professors, typically by email or in person. Follow up early and often. Before each exam, project or new class, remind instructors what you need. If something slips through- like a professor for getting to upload slides- loop in the DSO. Their job is to back you up.

transcript: ﻿1 Real World Scenario. Brianna at Work. Brianna just started her first full-time job at a busy media company. She has ADHD, and the noisy open office makes it hard to focus. HR replies within a few days. They ask for a short letter from her doctor, then schedule a meeting to discuss options. title: Follow-Up Etiquette — How to Nudge Without Nagging
transcript: ﻿1 Follow Up Etiquette - How to Nudge Without Nagging. Give the inbox a little room first. Academic support sites such as Duke University's follow up and reminder emails guide. Tell students to wait at least 48 hours - and a full week at the start of term - before tapping out a second message. Because faculty inboxes triple when classes launch. In the workplace, career advisors at Indeed recommend three to five business days so managers can consult HR or budgets before they answer. That pause says, "I'm organized, not impatient." But if the timeframe has passed, and you need to follow-up, keep your reminder short. "Hi, Professor Lopez - just checking on the request below. Let me know if you need anything else. Thank you!" Polite brevity works because people scan follow-ups; anything longer can feel like pressure.

title: Keeping Your Paper Trail Tidy & Safety Nets
transcript: ﻿1 Keeping Your Paper Trail Tidy and Safety Nets. For important email threads, it's a good idea to save every sent message, attachment, and reply in one folder. If silence continues, you forward the same thread and loop in an ally: CC or carbon copy. Lets all addresses see who's in the loop - perfect for a disability- services coordinator or HR partner. BCC or blind carbon copy. Hides addresses. Use it sparingly, for example to send yourself a time-stamped copy without cluttering the visible list. Escalating with a CC gently reminds the original reader and shows your following formal channels, not jumping straight to a complaint. And before you hit send lock in two last safeguards, smart attachments and the "undo-send" safety net. If you need to attach a file, check that you: Picked an accessible format. PDF or a clean Word doc opens on any device and works with screen readers; smartphone photos of paperwork often don't. Named it clearly. "Patel_AccommodationLetter_Fall2025. Pdf" beats "scan123.jpeg." A clear label tells the reader- and future you- what the file is at a glance. And mentioned it once in the body, "My accommodation letter is attached." That single line cues the reader to open the file and reminds you to double-check it's really attached. And if you accidentally click send before you intend to, it's okay. Breathe. You have some potential options. Most mail services let you snatch a message back moments after you sent it, but you may have to alter your settings. Here's an overview for some popular platforms. Gmail. Open settings, then general, then undo send. Slide the timer up to 30 seconds. That's a half a minute of breathing room. Outlook. In outlook on the web, open settings, then mail, then compose and reply. And then undo send and select 10 seconds. Using the desktop app? Create a "delay delivery" rule so every message sits in your Outbox for the grace period you choose. Apple Mail. On iPhone, iOS 16 plus or above, or Mac, MacOs Ventura plus or above. A blue undo send link sits at the bottom of the mail window for 10 seconds after you hit send. No setup required. If you spot the slip - wrong name, missing file - tap Undo, add what you left out, and resend with confidence. And if you missed the countdown. Just send a fast, no-drama fix: "Sorry for the extra email. My accommodation letter is attached here." A quick save, a clear attachment, and your professionalism stays intact. A specific ask, a courteous follow up, clear attachments, and a clean email trail work together to move your accommodation or workplace support from "request" to "approved."

title: Tone, Clarity & Accessibility — Five Quick Rules
transcript: ﻿1 Tone, Clarity and Accessibility - Five Quick Rules. You've built the skeleton of your message with the five-part blueprint. Now give a polish so a busy professor or HR rep can approve your request on the first read. Use these five finishing touches to sound professional, stay clear, and keep your support team in the loop. Plain words beat jargon. Say extra time on quizzes instead of assessment time modification. Clear language shows confidence and keeps the reader from guessing. One idea per paragraph. Limit each paragraph to two or three short sentences - easy to skim on a phone and less overwhelming. If the reader processes text slowly, say the attachment out loud. Write: "I've attached my accommodation letter" and then the name of the file - a clear file name and InBody mention, prevent lost paperwork, and follow up delays. Loop in allies. CC your disability services counselor or HR partner. Having the right support person on the thread adds credibility and saves you from "he-said-she-said" later. Proof two ways. Reading aloud catches missing words; a quick on-screen scan catches typos and extra spaces. A clean email shows you respect the reader's time - and your own request. Send only after this final checklist: Does the subject make sense on its own? The first line states the ask? Does the whole note fit on one screen? Attachment named and mentioned? Thank-you included? With structure plus these tone rules, your email lands focused, respectful, and ready for a quick "approved."

title: The Five-Part Email Blueprint
transcript: ﻿1 The Five-Part Email Blueprint. Picture this, it's Sunday night, you've created the syllabus and realized the first quiz is in two days - but your accommodation letter hasn't reached the professor yet. You could wait and hope or you could send one short email that starts the official process, proves you planned ahead and gives the instructor everything needed to say yes. That's the power of writing - a clear message in your own words, delivered before the clock runs out! So let's learn a five-part blueprint, subject line to sign off - that turns any request into a concise, professional email your teachers, bosses, and disability-services staff can act on right away. Number one. Nail the subject line. Think of it as the headline on a news story. Six to ten words that tell the reader exactly what's inside! A clear subject gets your email opened instead of lost in a professor's 200-message pile. For example, Accommodation Request - Extra Time for Bio 201 Quiz (October 10) Number two. Greet and identify yourself. Lead with a friendly, "Hello, Dr. White," then remind them who you are, "I'm Sam Lee from your 9 AM Bio 201 lecture." Most instructors teach hundreds of students. One quick identifier earns instant context. Number three. State your purpose in a single sentence. "I'm writing to request 50% extended time on quizzes, as approved by Disability Services." That one line answers the reader's top question: "Why am I getting this email?" Number four. Add only the key context. Two short sentences is the sweet spot. Give the facts they need to say yes - attachments, dates, or equipment. For example, "My accommodation letter is attached. The first quiz is next Tuesday, October 10." Number five. Close with gratitude and a full signature. A brief thank you builds rapport. "Thank you for helping me demonstrate my knowledge under equal conditions." Then sign off with name, role, and any ID number so they can file or forward without guessing. Now, let's stitch it together. Subject: Accommodation Request - Extra Time for Bio 201 Quiz (October 10) Hello, Dr. White. I'm Sam Lee from your 9 AM Bio 201 lecture. I'm writing to request 50% extended time on quizzes, as approved by Disability Services. My accommodation letter is attached, and the first quiz is next Tuesday, October 10. Thank you for helping me access the material fully. Best regards, Sam Lee Student ID 123456.

title: Why Written Communication Matters
transcript: ﻿1 Why Written Communication Matters. When you send an email, what you write and communicate becomes permanent. A paper trail is formed! And what you said can be referenced by the recipient. So if your email is worded poorly, this could affect your professional image at work or if it's successfully worded, it will come across as confident, professional, and respectful. Earning you credibility and improving your chances of getting your request.

title: Writing Emails & Requests That Get Results
transcript: ﻿1 Tone, Clarity and Accessibility - Five Quick Rules. You've built the skeleton of your message with the five-part blueprint. Now give a polish so a busy professor or HR rep can approve your request on the first read. Use these five finishing touches to sound professional, stay clear, and keep your support team in the loop. Plain words beat jargon. Say extra time on quizzes instead of assessment time modification. Clear language shows confidence and keeps the reader from guessing. One idea per paragraph. Limit each paragraph to two or three short sentences - easy to skim on a phone and less overwhelming. If the reader processes text slowly, say the attachment out loud. Write: "I've attached my accommodation letter" and then the name of the file - a clear file name and InBody mention, prevent lost paperwork, and follow up delays. Loop in allies. CC your disability services counselor or HR partner. Having the right support person on the thread adds credibility and saves you from "he-said-she-said" later. Proof two ways. Reading aloud catches missing words; a quick on-screen scan catches typos and extra spaces. A clean email shows you respect the reader's time - and your own request. Send only after this final checklist: Does the subject make sense on its own? The first line states the ask? Does the whole note fit on one screen? Attachment named and mentioned? Thank-you included? With structure plus these tone rules, your email lands focused, respectful, and ready for a quick "approved."

title: Why Practice This
transcript: ﻿1 Why Practice This? When you choose the confident and clear style- you make your needs unmistakable, show respect for the other person, and open the door to a win-win solution. Repeat the activity until spotting the patterns feels automatic. The faster you label the style, the faster "Confident and Clear" becomes your default.

title: Finding the Balanced Voice
transcript: ﻿1 Passive, Aggressive, or Clear and Confident. Finding the Balanced Voice. Let's decode four communication styles. Passive, Aggressive, Passive-Aggressive, and Clear and Confident. And practice the one that respects both you and the person you're talking to. Decode your communication style. Passive. When you communicate something passively, you shrink back. What you're communicating or asking for is not direct. This can lead to frustration for both parties. On your end, you may not receive what you want or need. And whoever you're talking to may be confused by how indirectly you communicated something. Aggressive. When you communicate something too aggressively, you tend to be too direct In your approach. Someone could think you are bulldozing your way into the situation. As volume goes up, tension increases, and cooperation goes down. Passive-Aggressive. When you slip into passive aggressive mode, you appear agreeable on the surface. But show frustration through sarcasm, silence, or foot dragging. The mix signals create confusion, irritate the other person, and can damage your professional reputation. Confident and clear. When you speak in a confident and clear way- you state your need calmly, link it to a shared success, and invite solutions. The tone is respectful, the request is unmistakable, and you're far more likely to get a quick yes!

title: Turning Rights into Results
transcript: ﻿1 Turning Rights into Results. Plan what to say. Choose your moment! State needs and solutions and enlist allies. Speaking up is your bridge from legal rights to real support! In our next lesson, we'll fine-tune how you speak - finding the assertive voice that gets results without stepping on toes.

title: Real-Life Inspiration: Temple Grandin
transcript: ﻿1 Real-Life Inspiration: Temple Grandin. Temple Grandin was diagnosed with autism at age 2 and didn't begin to speak until she was about 3 and a half years old; intensive early speech therapy and a few visionary teachers opened that door. Fast forward: she earned a PhD, became a professor of animal science at Colorado State University and applied her "thinking in pictures" to redesign cattle-handling equipment. Today, more than half of all cattle in the U.S. and Canada move through facilities she created, making meat plants safer and far more humane. Grandin now fills lecture halls worldwide and advises Fortune-500 food companies. Her story proves that with practice, support, and courage, communication skills can grow from total silence to global influence - and if Temple can make that leap, you can move from a nervous whisper to a confident advocate.

title: Confident, Respectful Requests
transcript: ﻿1 Confident Respectful Requests. One. Know your rights and the exact support you need! Federal laws like Section 504 and the Americans with Disabilities Act or (ADA) protect your right to accommodations, changes that help you fully participate in school or work. When you clearly mention these laws and name a specific accommodation, your request is much more likely to be approved quickly. For example, instead of saying, "Can you help me?", you can say, "My 504 plan includes accommodations for my ADHD, such as extended time on tests and assignments, which I'd like to make sure are in place. Thanks." Two. Plan and practice in a low stake space. Before you communicate something, it can help to get yourself in the right mindset by writing what you want to communicate down and then rehearse saying it a few times. You can even use an app or a journal to write them as bullet points notes or role-play with a friend. Rehearsing in a safe space calms nerves! In a communication Quarterly experiment with 220 under-graduates, students who practice their speech in front of even a small audience earned 7.5% higher performance scores than classmates who rehearsed alone. Three. Choose the right moment. Timing matters when speaking up. Instead of waiting to bump into someone in a crowded hallway or catching your professor as they're rushing out after class, aim for moments when you'll have their full attention. Office hours, a scheduled one-on-one Zoom call, or a brief meeting with HR are ideal opportunities to clearly express your needs without distractions. For example, imagine you need extra software installed on your classroom computer, rather than asking your instructor right before class when they're busy setting up, schedule a quick check-in during office hours or send a brief email requesting a short meeting. Picking the right moment increases your chances of having a focused conversation and getting the support you need. Four. State your needs clearly and how they will link to success! For example, you can say, "Using text-to-speech lets me finish the readings and participate fully in class instead of just asking for text to speech because it's solution focused. Five. Seek allies. If your first request stalls, talk to a disability services coordinator, counselor, or a trusted coworker. Most employers get on board once they see the benefits: According to the 2024 Job Accommodation Network survey, 67% of managers say accommodations are vary or extremely effective and 56% cost nothing at all.

title: Common Roadblocks—and Why They’re Normal
transcript: ﻿1 Common Roadblocks and why They're Normal. Maybe you have a voice inside that tells you not to be such a burden on people, or don't be a bother. You're definitely not alone in feeling that way. Your feelings are valid and recognizing them is a powerful first step. From here, our goal is to equip you with the tools and support you need to work through it and keep moving forward. Here are five steps you can take to be more assertive in communicating your requests.

title: Why Speaking Up Matters
transcript: ﻿1 Why Speaking Up Matters. When people around you understand your needs, they can help remove barriers. Staying silent stacks up stress. In NAMI's 2024 Workplace Mental Health Poll, 62% of employees who felt uncomfortable speaking about their mental health also reported feeling burned out. Meanwhile, a Salesforce survey of 1,500 professionals found that workers who feel heard are 4.6 times more likely to feel empowered to do their best work. Each successful conversation makes the next one easier, so think of self-advocacy as a muscle. The more you use it, the stronger it gets!

title: Speaking Up for Yourself in School, College & Work
transcript: ﻿1 Speaking Up for Yourself in School, College, and Work. Knowing your rights is powerful, but putting them into action takes your voice. Speaking up can feel intimidating, but it's also how you transform legal protections into real-world support. Whether at school, college, or work, confidently expressing what you need is the key to unlocking the support you deserve.

title: Staying Organized and Speaking Up
transcript: ﻿1 Staying Organized and Speaking Up. Keep a paper trail, save emails, letters, meeting notes, and any documentation you submit or receive. Having a record makes it easier to follow up or escalate if needed. Know when to escalate. If your request isn't being addressed, don't give up. Reach out to Disability Services, HR, or an ADA coordinator- whoever can help move things forward. Celebrate small wins. Got extra time on a test? Received lecture slides in advance? Every win- big or small- builds confidence and helps create a more accessible environment for everyone. Self-advocacy doesn't mean solving everything alone. It means using the tools you have, asking for what you need, and staying steady even when the process is frustrating. Speak clearly, follow up, and trust that your voice matters.

title: Success Story: Helena's Graduation Victory
transcript: ﻿1 Success Story. Helena's Graduation Victory. Helena is a senior at public college. She's deaf, and so is her grandmother- who's flying in from Puerto Rico to watch her graduate. But a few weeks before commencement, Helena finds out the school isn't planning to provide a Spanish Sign Language interpreter. Instead of giving up, Helena contacts her regional ADA Center, looks up the relevant part of the law and decides to speak up. What follows is a simple email thread that changed not just her experience, but the school's entire accessibility policy. Subject: Interpreter Access at Commencement. From: Helena.Rivera@college.edu To: Dean.Carter@college.edu. "Hi Dean Carter, I'm a graduating senior and I'm deaf. My grandmother, who is also Deaf and speaks Spanish Sign Language or LSE, is attending commencement. I've learned there won't be a Spanish interpreter provided. Under ADA Title II, public colleges are required to provide effective communication for guests with disabilities. Would the college be able to provide an LSE interpreter for the event? Thank you for your time. Helena Rivera." Subject: Re: Interpreter Access at Commencement From: Dean.Carter@college.edu To: HelenaR.ivera@college.edu. "Hi Helena, Thank you for reaching out and for the helpful reference. We hadn't received a request like this before, but we're now working with an interpreting agency to provide LSE interpretation at the ceremony. We're also reviewing our accessibility policies to make sure future events automatically include interpreter options when needed. Thanks again for your advocacy and congratulations on your graduation. Best, Dean Carter." Helena's voice made a difference- not just for her grandmother, but for every family attending future ceremonies. That's the power of knowing your rights, doing your research, and speaking up respectfully. Next, we'll wrap up this lesson by giving you a few tools to stay organized, escalate when needed, and celebrate the wins- big or small- along the way.

title: Phrases that Make Self Advocacy Easier
transcript: ﻿1 Phrases That Make Self-Advocacy Easier. Knowing what to say and how to say it can make all the difference when you're asking for support. These phrases help you stay respectful, clear, and confident, whether you are emailing a professor or speaking to your manager. Try one of these: "Because of [disability or need], it helps me when [support/ accommodation]." For example: "Because of ADHD, it helps me when I wear noise-canceling headphones to stay focused." "I'm requesting [accommodation] so I can meet the same expectations as my peers." For example: "I'm requesting captions on training videos so I can understand everything being said and meet deadlines." Under Section 504/the ADA, this [support] ensures equal access." For example: "Under the ADA, this flexible schedule helps ensure I can complete my work without burnout." "What's the best way to move forward with this request?" A great way to invite collaboration and keep the conversation going. "Let me know if you need documentation or next steps from me." Shows initiative while reinforcing your rights. Speak calmly. Repeat if needed. Always follow up in writing- emails or notes create a paper trail that protects you. Self-advocacy is a skill, and every time you practice, it gets easier.

title: In the Workplace: Know Your Rights Around Disclosure
transcript: ﻿1 In the Workplace: Know your rights around disclosure. At work, support is still available- but it looks different, and you are in charge of asking for it. Under the Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA) you have the right to request reasonable accommodations at any point in the hiring process or on the job- but you're never required to disclose your disability unless you're asking for an accommodation. Here's what that means for you: You decide if and when to disclose. You can share during the interview, after a job offer, or once you've started the role- whatever makes the most sense for you! Your employer can't ask. It's illegal for an employer to ask about your disability or medical history before making a job offer. After that, they can only ask questions related to the accommodation request or job functions. Requests must be tied to job needs. You'll need to explain how your disability affects your ability to do the job and what adjustments would help. For example, you might say, "I have a condition that affects my stamina and focus. To perform my job effectively, I'm requesting noise-reducing headphones and the option to take short breaks as a reasonable accommodation under the ADA." Put your request in writing, and if possible, include one or two options so you're showing flexibility. This starts what the ADA calls "an interactive process"- a respectful back-and-forth where you and your employer work together to find a solution that supports your work without causing undue hardship on the business. If the process stalls or you face pushback, organizations like your Regional ADA Center or the Job Accommodation Network can help you understand your rights and suggest next steps.

title: Navigating the DSO and HR
transcript: ﻿1 Best Practices When Working with HR. Even though the ADA has your back, navigating workplace accommodations can feel tricky. Here are five strategies to make the process smoother and more effective: One. Come prepared. Before your meeting, think through what kind of support would genuinely help you succeed. Be ready to explain the barrier and suggest specific solutions. If you have documentation from a provider, bring it- especially if HR requests it. Two. Use clear confident language. You don't need to share every detail of your condition. Just focus on how it impacts your ability to do the job and what adjustments would help. Use phrases like "I'm requesting reasonable accommodations under the ADA." "This support would allow me to meet expectations and perform at my best." Three. Follow-up in writing. After any conversation, send a quick email to confirm what was discussed. It creates a helpful paper trail and reduces the chance of misunderstandings later. For example: "Thanks for taking the time to meet. Just to recap, we discuss trying a quieter workspace and checking back in after two weeks." Four. Know your resources. You don't have to figure it all out alone. The Job Accommodation Network, or "JAN," offers free, expert advice on common accommodations and how to request them. In a 2019 to 2022 survey, 68.4% of employers said accommodations were vary or extremely effective, and another 18.3% rated them as somewhat effective after consulting the network. Some workplaces also have Employee Resource Groups, or ERGs, for people with disabilities, and joining one can give you extra community and guidance. Five. Stay open and flexible. The first solution may not be perfect. That's okay! Keep the conversation going until you land on something that works. The goal isn't perfection- it's functionality and access.

title: Using Your Rights - The Self Advocacy Toolkit
transcript: ﻿1 Success Story. Helena's Graduation Victory. Helena is a senior at public college. She's deaf, and so is her grandmother- who's flying in from Puerto Rico to watch her graduate. But a few weeks before commencement, Helena finds out the school isn't planning to provide a Spanish Sign Language interpreter. Instead of giving up, Helena contacts her regional ADA Center, looks up the relevant part of the law and decides to speak up. What follows is a simple email thread that changed not just her experience, but the school's entire accessibility policy. Subject: Interpreter Access at Commencement. From: Helena.Rivera@college.edu To: Dean.Carter@college.edu. "Hi Dean Carter, I'm a graduating senior and I'm deaf. My grandmother, who is also Deaf and speaks Spanish Sign Language or LSE, is attending commencement. I've learned there won't be a Spanish interpreter provided. Under ADA Title II, public colleges are required to provide effective communication for guests with disabilities. Would the college be able to provide an LSE interpreter for the event? Thank you for your time. Helena Rivera." Subject: Re: Interpreter Access at Commencement From: Dean.Carter@college.edu To: HelenaR.ivera@college.edu. "Hi Helena, Thank you for reaching out and for the helpful reference. We hadn't received a request like this before, but we're now working with an interpreting agency to provide LSE interpretation at the ceremony. We're also reviewing our accessibility policies to make sure future events automatically include interpreter options when needed. Thanks again for your advocacy and congratulations on your graduation. Best, Dean Carter." Helena's voice made a difference- not just for her grandmother, but for every family attending future ceremonies. That's the power of knowing your rights, doing your research, and speaking up respectfully. Next, we'll wrap up this lesson by giving you a few tools to stay organized, escalate when needed, and celebrate the wins- big or small- along the way.

title: HR Policies and Workplace Interactive Process
transcript: ﻿1 HR Policies and the Workplace Interactive Process. In the workplace, there's no central office like the DSO- but there is a system. It's called the interactive process and it's how accommodations are handled under the ADA. HR's Role. Your company's Human Resources department is typically responsible for handling accommodation requests. Their duties include: receiving your request, whether written or verbally, keeping your medical and disability information confidential, initiating the interactive process to explore solutions, and documenting and tracking the accommodations provided. What the Process Looks Like. Once you submit a request, HR starts a dialogue with you- and possibly your manager- to figure out what support might work. This isn't a one-size-fits-all checklist. It's a conversation designed to match your needs with your job's requirements. They may ask for limited documentation, such as a brief note from a healthcare provider stating that you have a disability and need accommodation. But they can't ask for your full medical history or access to private records. No Retaliation Allowed. It's illegal for your employer to retaliate against you for asking for an accommodation. That means no demotions, firings, or penalties for speaking up about your needs. "Undue Hardship" Is Rare. Employers aren't required to provide accommodations that would cause significant difficulty or expense- but in practice, most requests are low-cost and easy to implement. Most accommodation requests cost little to nothing and over half cost employers nothing at all! Things like flexible scheduling, software, or noise reducing tools are common and reasonable. At its best, the interactive process is collaborative. You bring clarity about your needs; they bring flexibility and a legal obligation to respond in good faith. When both sides stay open and proactive, accommodations become part of the workplace culture- not a burden.

title: Anticipate & Map Resources
transcript: ﻿1 Anticipate and Map Resources. Even the best plans can hit a snag, but that doesn't mean you have to face it alone or unprepared. One of the smartest ways to stay ahead of setbacks is to anticipate where they might happen and map out your support system in advance. Start by asking yourself: Where do things tend to go off track for me- transportation? technology? communication? health? What types of situations make me feel stuck or anxious? What's helped me in the past? Who can I count on for help- and how do I reach them quickly? Now, build your personal hiccup map. Make a short list of real-life challenges that could pop up. The bus is late- or doesn't come at all. A class is suddenly moved online. Your laptop crashes the night before a big paper is due. You have a pain flare or a sensory overload during your shift. A group project partner drops out last minute. Next, match each challenge with at least one potential support: disability services office or HR representative who can offer formal accommodations, a friend, classmate, or mentor who's good at troubleshooting or calming you down, tools and apps like public transit trackers, screen readers, calendar reminders, or breathing timers, backup materials- a phone charger, medication, extra earbuds, snacks, headphones, fidget tools. Some students make a plan B folder on their phone with emergency contacts, support scripts, campus maps, or screenshots of accommodation letters. Others carry a physical kit like a small pouch with earplugs, a snack, a printed schedule, or a note card with calming strategies. The key is to keep support tools visible and easy to access, not buried in an email box or the bottom of a bag. When stress spikes or your mind goes blank, this kind of preparation helps you stay steady. You're not scrambling, you've already laid out your options. That small shift can make a big difference when things go sideways.

title: Plan B Mindset
transcript: ﻿1 Practice Activating Plan B. Knowing your backup plan is one thing. Using it under pressure is another. That's why practice matters. When you rehearse small setbacks ahead of time, you train your brain to respond with action instead of shutting down. This isn't about pretending the worst will happen- it's about proving to yourself that even if it does, you've got options. Start with everyday scenarios and work your way up. Think of it like building a muscle. The more you lift, the stronger it gets! Here are some simple ways to practice: Your printer jams before a deadline, email the file to yourself and print it at school at library or a local shop. Your lab partner is out sick, practice how you'd ask your instructor to pair you with someone new or adjust the assignment. Your headphones are missing before a noisy commute. Use a grounding tool like a fidget, a focus app, or try reading a familiar book to stay calm. Try this next: Write down five common hiccups that throw you off. Missed alarms. Tech failures. Sudden schedule changes. For each one, come up with at least two backup options. Then rehearse them, say them out loud. Walk through them with a friend or teacher. The more familiar these responses become, the easier it'll be to activate them under stress. And don't be afraid to make it collaborative or even fun. Challenge a classmate or peer mentor, "Let's each come up with one plot twist today and role-play how we'd handle it." Take turns, tossing curve balls, and problem-solving on the fly. It's not about getting it right the first time. It's about getting used to thinking on your feet and staying calm when things shift. If you're working with a job coach, teacher, or family member, ask them to throw in a surprise scenario once in a while. "The elevator's out." "There's a loud fire drill during your quiz." "Your wifi crashes during an online class." You'll be surprised how quickly your brain starts scanning for solutions- not just problems. Each time you put a backup plan into action, you build trust in yourself. Not just that you can bounce back- but that you will. Because flexibility isn't just a skill, it's a superpower. Expect setbacks. Prepare backups. Keep going. Use "if-then" plans to handle disruptions before they happen. Map your resources- people, tools, and calming strategies. Build multi-layer options for transportation, tech, and health. Practice pivoting through small hiccups so you're ready for bigger ones. Confidence grows when you know you've got options! You've got the skills, you've got the mindset. Now you've got the backup plan.

title: Always Have a Plan B...and C!
transcript: ﻿1 Practice Activating Plan B. Knowing your backup plan is one thing. Using it under pressure is another. That's why practice matters. When you rehearse small setbacks ahead of time, you train your brain to respond with action instead of shutting down. This isn't about pretending the worst will happen- it's about proving to yourself that even if it does, you've got options. Start with everyday scenarios and work your way up. Your lab partner is out sick, practice how you'd ask your instructor to pair you with someone new or adjust the assignment. Your headphones are missing before a noisy commute.
