"Your wifi crashes during an online class." You'll be surprised how quickly your brain starts scanning for solutions- not just problems. Each time you put a backup plan into action, you build trust in yourself. Not just that you can bounce back- but that you will. Because flexibility isn't just a skill, it's a superpower. Expect setbacks. Prepare backups. Keep going. Use "if-then" plans to handle disruptions before they happen. Map your resources- people, tools, and calming strategies. Build multi-layer options for transportation, tech, and health. Practice pivoting through small hiccups so you're ready for bigger ones. Confidence grows when you know you've got options! You've got the skills, you've got the mindset. Now you've got the backup plan.

title: Bringing it All Together
transcript: ﻿1 Bringing It All Together. Reflecting and journaling are powerful tools for building resilience because they help you connect the dots between your experiences and your personal growth. The simple act of writing down your challenges and the steps you took to overcome them creates a narrative of strength. You can revisit whenever you feel stuck or uncertain. By tracking your progress over time, you begin to see how each setback teaches you something valuable, and how each success story adds another brick to your foundation of confidence. It doesn't matter if your entries are polished or messy, long-winded, or just a few bullet points. What matters is the habit of pausing, thinking about what happened, and noting what you can learn from it. Keep at it, and you'll discover that reflection isn't just an end-of-the-day exercise, it's a mindset that turns everyday ups and downs into building blocks for future success. So grab a notebook or open a fresh document and give journaling a try. You'll likely find that putting your experiences into words helps you process them more fully, and reminds you in real time that you're always learning, always growing, and always capable of bouncing back!

title: Conflict & Help-Seeking Skills
transcript: ﻿1 Conflict and Help-Seeking Skills. Even with strong problem-solving skills, there are times when you'll need to speak up, clarify expectations, or ask for support. That's not a failure- it's a strength. Knowing when and how to ask for help is a key part of handling unexpected challenges and building independence. But asking for help doesn't always feel easy. You might worry about being annoying, looking incapable, or getting brushed off. And those fears can be even stronger- especially if past requests were misunderstood or ignored. The good news? There are simple ways to frame your request so that it's clear, respectful, and focused on collaboration. Here's a real-world script that works in many situations: Whether you're speaking to a teacher, supervisor, or peer. "I have ADHD, which makes it hard for me to concentrate when there's a lot of noise or movement around me. Having access to a quiet testing room really helps me focus. That way I can show what I've learned and do best on my exams." This script does three things: One. It briefly names the need or disability (if you feel comfortable doing so). Two. It explains how it affects your learning or work. Three. It shares what support helps- and why it matters. You can adjust it for different situations. For example, if you're stuck in class and need clarification: "I've tried rereading the instructions and checking my notes, but I'm still not sure what's expected for the second part of the assignment. Could you help me understand what the next step should be?" This isn't about complaining- it's about problem- solving together. It shows that you've already taken action, and now you're seeking guidance to keep moving forward. If you are in a workplace or internship, you might say: "I followed the steps in the training manual, but the software isn't letting me move to the next screen. I'm not sure if I missed something- can you take a quick look with me?" Being proactive like this builds trust and respect. It also shows resilience: you're not giving up, you're finding another way through.

title: Self-Talk & Inner Coaching
transcript: ﻿1 Self-talk and Inner Coaching. When you're stuck, stressed, or starting to spiral, your internal voice matters. What you say to yourself- whether out loud or silently- can shape how you handle problems. Negative self-talk often sounds like this: "I always mess up.", "Everyone else can do it, Why can't I?" "I'm going to fail." These thoughts aren't just unhelpful, they actually make it harder to take action. Studies show that rumination repeatedly going over negative thoughts- undermines problem-solving ability and reduces the use of active coping strategies. When your mind loops through what went wrong instead of what you can do next, motivation and clarity drop fast. Try using short, encouraging phrases to interrupt negative spirals. Think of them as inner scripts that help you reset: "One step at a time." "I've handled worse before." "This is hard, but I can figure it out." Some people write these on sticky notes and post them near their workspace. Others record them as voice memos or make them part of their daily routine- repeating a phrase before class or when facing a challenge. Here's an example: Peter's self-coaching moment- workplace. Peter works part-time at a grocery store. A customer is upset and raises their voice. Peter's face tightens. His breathing speeds up. He excuses himself and then steps away to the back door for a second. "Stay calm. This isn't about you. You've got this." He returns and handles the situation with quiet confidence. Self-talk isn't about pretending everything's okay- it's about reminding yourself that you can keep going, even when things are hard. Step-by-step Problem Solving. Not every problem can be solved in the moment. But when you've got a framework to follow, even tough challenges become easier to face. Remember that 5-step problem-solving strategy we covered earlier? Let's walk through it again with more detail- because having a calm, clear process your brain can return to is especially important when you're overwhelmed or unsure of what to do next. Here's that simple five-step strategy that works for school, work, or daily life: One, what's the actual problem? When you're stressed, everything can feel like a crisis. So start by identifying the real issue. Not "I'm a failure," but "I forgot to submit the assignment." Keep it specific and neutral- no name calling or drama. Two, what are my options? List as many ideas as you can- even if some feel silly or imperfect. The goal here is creativity, not judgment. If you're stuck, ask: Can I fix it? Can I get help? Can I adjust the timeline or expectations? You might be surprised how many possible paths forward exist once your brain is in problem-solving mode instead of panic mode. Three, what's the best option now? This is about making a choice that's helpful, realistic, and doable, not perfect. Ask yourself: What can I act on immediately? What has the least risk and the most benefit? Pick one strategy and commit to it. Four, try it out. Take that first step, even if your heart's racing. Action interrupts spirals. Whether it's sending an email, starting the task, or asking for help, momentum matters. Five, did it work? What's next? Reflect on the outcome. Did your plan help? If yes- amazing! If not, go back to your list and try something else. Problem solving isn't always linear, but having a plan gives you something to return to when emotions are high. This framework is especially helpful for students who may face unpredictable challenges, like sudden schedule changes, inaccessible instructions, or unexpected sensory triggers. When your environment feels chaotic, a steady routine like this becomes a mental anchor. Here's an example of how this works in real life: Cadie's locker lock meltdown- high school hallway. Meet Cadie, a new student from Northshore High. She stands in front of her new locker. The hallway is noisy and crowded. She's late for class and her lock won't open. Let's examine the situation. Step one, what's the problem? "Ugh, My lock won't open, and I'm going to be late!" Step two, what are my options? "What am I going to do!" Cadie pauses for a second, thinking again, again, slowly. She could try to ask a nearby student for help. Or she could go to the front office and ask if she can borrow some tools. Or, she could email her teacher from her phone for a heads up. Step three, what's the best option right now? She tries again, but slower focusing on the combination and it clicks open! Step four, try it out. "Yes! Got it!" She grabs her folder and hurries to class. Step five, did it work? Yes. Cadie made it in time. Next time she plans to practice opening the lock during lunch when the hallway is quiet- and she writes the combo in a safe place. In just a few minutes, Cadie goes from panic to problem-solving. Not because the stress disappeared- but because she had a script to follow. These steps can become second nature with practice. You can even keep a copy of the five-step list in your phone, notebook, or locker. Somewhere easy to access when your brain's too overwhelmed to think clearly.

title: Role Play & "What-If" Rehearsals
transcript: ﻿1 Role-Play and "What-If" Rehearsals It's one thing to know how to solve a problem. It's another thing to stay calm and act quickly in the moment. That's why rehearsing "what-if" situations ahead of time is such a powerful tool. It trains your brain to expect the unexpected- and respond with confidence instead of panic. Role-play and scenario rehearsals give you a low-stakes way to prepare. You can do them with a friend, teacher, job coach, or even on your own by talking out loud or journaling your responses. The idea is to mentally walk through common challenges before they happen so your brain has a plan ready to go. Here are three examples of everyday problems- and how students prepare to handle them: Scenario One. Jinoo's Wi-Fi Meltdown. Meet Jinoo. He's a high school senior finishing up a history essay on his laptop. It's late at night- 10:42 PM. Jinoo is working hard at his desk, typing on his computer. On the screen, we see a file called "Final Draft." Suddenly, something happens. The internet stops working. Jinoo sees an error: "No internet connection." His face shows disappointment. He stops typing. Then, he picks up his phone. Jinoo decides to text his friend Rumi for help. "Hey, Rumi, WiFi's out. Can I finish working on my essay at your place?" "Hey, dude! Of course. And I made some late night cookies too! Let's make sure you submit that on time." "Hi, Mr. Han, sorry I submitted my essay late last night- I lost WiFi at my place but finished using a backup plan. Thanks for understanding!" "No problem, thank you for explaining." Scenario Two. Zoey's Ride Dilemma. Meet Zoey. She's getting ready for her morning class at the local community college. Zoey takes a sip of her coffee and looks at her phone. A new notification appears- onscreen text: "Your driver has canceled the trip." "Ugh." Zoey stops. She stares at the screen. She quickly opens her transit app. "Sorry for the interruption- the bus is running late." She takes a deep breath, then thinks for a second- she should call someone for help. "Hi, Bobby-would you be heading downtown this morning by any chance? My ride was canceled and the bus is running late." "Of course, on my way!" A friend arrives to help. As they drive, she takes out her phone. She starts texting her professor to let them know she's running late. "Hi Professor Saja. My ride fell through, but I'm on my way now, so sorry I'm late. Thanks for your patience." Scenario Three. Mira's Group Project Hiccup. This is Mira. She's a sophomore at a university. Right now, she's in class about to present a group project. Two teammates were supposed to help. But Mira looks around- one of them isn't there. "Where's Abby?" "Alright, let's start presentations starting with Abby's team." Mira starts getting nervous and then an idea pops in her head. It's time to improvise a bit. "I'll do the intro. You cover Abby's part?" Mira and Hunter do the presentation together, covering Abby's parts as needed. Although it required some modification and revisions, the slides move smoothly and they wrap it up with confidence. The whole class applauds and they get a good grade! They adapted, adjusted, and delivered- no panic necessary. Rehearsing "What if" situations builds mental muscle memory. The more you walk through how you'd react, the less likely you are to freeze or spiral if it actually happens. You can also create your own "What-If" List. Write down five situations that stress you out- missed alarm, class presentation nerves, forgetting an assignment, a schedule change, sensory overload- and jot down two or three ways you might handle each one. Even this short exercise can help lower anxiety by giving you a sense of control. Try rehearsing with someone you trust. Take turns acting out situations and brainstorming solutions. Or, if that feels awkward, write out scripts or record voice memos to yourself. The format doesn't matter- The habit of practicing does. The more you rehearse, the faster your brain learns that unexpected doesn't have to mean unmanageable. You've got tools. You've got options. And you're not winging it- you're prepared.

title: 5 Step Problem Solving Framework
transcript: ﻿1 Five Step Problem-Solving Framework. When something unexpected happens, it's easy to freeze or fall into negative thoughts like, "I can't deal with this" or "Everything's ruined." But you don't need a perfect plan. You just need a place to start. That's where a step-by-step problem-solving framework can help. It gives your brain something to focus on when emotions are high or plans fall apart. Here's how to walk yourself through it: Step one, identify the problem. Get specific. What actually went wrong? Instead of saying, "I messed up," try "I forgot to set an alarm and missed my class." The clearer the problem, the easier it is to solve. Step two, brainstorm solutions. Write down every possible way forward, even if it seems silly or unlikely. Could you email the teacher?, borrow a friend's notes?, reschedule your appointment?. This part is about quantity not perfection. The more ideas, the better. Step three, choose the best option. Look over your ideas and ask: What's realistic right now? What solves the biggest part of the problem? It's okay if the option you pick isn't perfect- go for the one that gets you unstuck. Step four, try it. Take action. Even a small step counts. Making a move- any move- can help reduce stress and give you momentum to keep going. Step five, reflect and adjust. Once you've tried something, check in with yourself. Did it work? If yes, great. If not, what could you do differently next time? This step turns every setback into a learning opportunity. You can write these steps down and keep them somewhere visible: on a notebook, phone screen, or sticky note by your desk. When your brain wants to panic, this framework helps you pause, get focused, and problem solve with more confidence and less stress.

title: Recognizing & Regulating Emotions
transcript: Recognizing and Countering Misinformation The entertainment industry has long used technology such as CGI for movies. Now, thanks to generative AI tools like Stable Diffusion and Unreal Engine, almost anyone can produce highly realistic images or video content. Some content is so realistic, it's hard to tell the difference anymore. That's fine for certain purposes, but many people exploit this technology for malicious purposes. For instance, they may manipulate existing content to create deepfake videos, photos, or audio tracks designed to deceive audiences. This unfortunate trend will continue to grow, adding to the rapid rise of online disinformation, such as false information spread for the purpose of misleading others. Students should be aware of and avoid such unethical AI practices and learn how to critically evaluate content and sources.

title: Flexible vs. Rigid Thinking
transcript: ﻿1 Flexible Thinking Versus Rigid Thinking. Some people feel more comfortable when everything goes exactly as planned. Having structure can make daily life feel predictable and safe- but when something changes, rigid thinking can make it hard to adapt. Flexible thinking is the skill of adjusting your mindset when things don't go the way you expected. It means finding another way to solve a problem, trying a new strategy, or being willing to shift your plan. Without this skill, even small changes- like a teacher changing the order of a lesson- can feel like a major disruption. In one study, school-aged students on the autism spectrum who participated in a shortterm flexibility training program improved their ability to switch between tasks and reduced repetitive errors. You can start building flexible thinking with small, low stress challenges. Try changing the order of your morning routine or switching where you sit to study. These tiny changes train your brain to stay calm and adjust. When something unexpected happens. Over time, your brain learns that change doesn't always mean danger and that you can handle it. Flexible thinking isn't about giving up control. It's about having more than one way forward when life throws you a curve ball.

title: When Plans Changed: Handling the Unexpected
transcript: ﻿1 Bringing It All Together. Solving problems and becoming more independent isn't about having all the answers. It's about building habits that help you move forward, even when things get hard. Here's a quick recap of what you've learned in this lesson: One. Break Big Tasks Into Smaller Steps. Task analysis helps you turn overwhelming assignments into bite-sized actions you can actually start- and finish. Two. Estimate and Schedule Time. Tools like Task-Time sheets and the Pomodoro Technique help you plan ahead, stay on track, and avoid last-minute panic. Three. Use Structure and Routines. Visual schedules and consistent anchor points during your day can lower stress and boost focus. Four. Prioritize and Front-Load. Learn to separate the must-do tasks from the nice-to-do ones. Handle important things early, when you have the most energy. Five. Build Flexibility. Practice adjusting when plans change. Small daily shifts can help you stay calm and problem-solve on the fly. Six. Regulate Emotions. When stress builds up, use grounding strategies- like 4-7-8 breathing or positive self-talk to get back to your center. Seven. Practice With "What-If" Scenarios. Role-playing real-life challenges builds mental muscle memory, so you're not caught off guard when problems pop up. Eight. Coach Yourself. Inner scripts like "I've got this" or "One step at a time" can turn down the volume on negative self-talk and help you reset when you feel stuck. You don't have to master everything all at once. Even one new strategy can make a big difference. The more you use these tools, the more confident and capable you'll feel. Next up, we'll dive into setting personal goals that actually make sense for you- not just goals that sound good, but ones that feel doable, motivating, and aligned with your strengths. You're not starting from scratch. You're building something. Let's keep going.

title: Scenario Walk Through
transcript: ﻿1 Scenario Walk-Through. Let's look at what all of these strategies can look like in real life. Jamal is a college student with ADHD. He has a group presentation coming up next week, and he's been putting it off. He knows he needs to get started, but the project feels huge. Instead of avoiding it, Jamal breaks the task into smaller pieces. He lists each step, choose a topic, meet with the group, divide responsibilities, research his section, create slides, and rehearse. He writes each task on a separate sticky note and puts them on his wall. Then he uses a task app to set deadlines for each step. He blocks off time in his calendar to work and sets alarms on his phone to help him stay focused using the Pomodoro method. 25 minutes of work, followed by a 5-minute break. He also builds a visual routine for his day. After class, he gives himself time to rest, then sets a timer for his first work session. Every time he completes a step, he moves the sticky note to a "done" section on his wall. This helps him feel motivated by the progress he's making. By using the tools that work for him- breaking things down, setting reminders, and building structure- Jamal finishes his part of the presentation on time and feels confident presenting with his group. Big tasks and tight schedules don't have to be overwhelming. Once you learn how to break things down, plan your time, and use the right tools, it gets easier to stay focused and follow through. Start by making tasks smaller and clearer. Then find out how much time each step will take and build a schedule that actually works for you. Use visual reminders, set alarms, and build routines that help your brain stay on track. Add tools like timers, checklists, and task apps when you need extra support. You don't need to change everything overnight. Just start with one strategy- like creating a checklist or blocking out a study session- and build from there. Every small step you take is a step toward more independence and less stress. Break it, plan it, time it, check it off.

title: AT Toolbox
transcript: ﻿1 Assistive Tech Toolbox. Technology can be a powerful support when it comes to staying organized and getting things done. Here are some tools that can help: Timers like "Time Timer" give you a visual sense of how much time is passing, which can make it easier to stay focused during work blocks. Digital Calendars and Reminders help you plan ahead and keep track of deadlines without relying on memory alone. Task Management Apps such as Trello, Monday.com, or Todoist let you break big assignments into smaller steps, set deadlines and check off progress. Text-to-speech tools read written material out loud, which can be especially useful if reading is slow or tiring. Research has found that using external tools like these improves time awareness, planning skills, and task follow-through. Try setting up three simple alarms. One goes off 10 minutes before you need to start your next task. The second lasts for a 25-minute work session. The third reminds you to take a 5-minute break. That's a full productivity routine in less than a minute of setup. At Gladeo, our founder uses Todoist to plan projects and phone alarms to stay on track throughout the day. When the right tech supports the way your brain works, it becomes easier to take control of your time.

title: Prioritizing, Chunking, Frontloading
transcript: ﻿1 Prioritizing, Chunking and Front Loading. When everything feels urgent, it's hard to know where to start. That's why learning to prioritize tasks is key to getting things done without feeling overwhelmed. This is especially true for students with executive functioning challenges who may have trouble organizing, initiating, or sequencing tasks in the right order. Start by sorting tasks into two categories. Must-do and Nice-to-do. For example: Finishing your science project that's due tomorrow, Must-do. Organizing your desk, Nice-to-do. It doesn't mean you'll never do the second one- but it can wait. Once you know what to tackle first, chunk the work into smaller parts. Say you're writing a history essay, one chunk might be gathering sources. Another could be outlining your argument. Then, writing a single paragraph. Breaking it up this way makes it easier to focus and track progress. Setting micro-deadlines is another useful strategy. Instead of telling yourself, "Finish the essay by Friday." Try, "Finish the outline by Tuesday, and the first draft by Thursday." This gives you short term wins and keeps you moving forward. Finally, front load anything you can. Pack your bag the night before, lay out clothes, charge your laptop, even prepping a snack ahead of time can save mental energy during busy mornings. The more you do in calm moments, the smoother your day runs later. Prioritizing isn't about doing everything- it's about doing the right things at the right time.

title: Visual Schedules & Structured Routines
transcript: ﻿1 Visual Schedules and Structured Routines. Some students thrive when there's a clear structure for the day. A visual schedule, or a consistent routine can make a huge difference in reducing anxiety, improving time management, and supporting independence. A visual schedule is exactly what it sounds like- a chart or list that lays out your day in pictures, colors, or words. It can be a whiteboard with magnets, a printed schedule with icons, or even a digital version on your phone. What matters is that it helps you see what's coming next. Research shows that using visual activity schedules can significantly reduce off-task behavior, improve transitions, and lower anxiety. For example, your after-school schedule might look like this: 3:30 to 3:50 Snack, 3:50 to 4:30 Homework Block Number 1, 4:30 to 4:45 Short walk and Movement break, 4:45 to 5:30 Homework Block Number 2, 5:30 to 6:15 Free time and Hobbies, 6:15 to 7 Dinner, 7 to 7:45 Screen time and Social time, 7:45 to 8:15 Chores and Prep for the next day, 8:15 to 9 Wind down (with no screens), 9 to 9:30 In bed by 9:30. Following a predictable routine like this builds confidence and reduces stress about what's next. Over time, routines become automatic and that's the goal! It means you're building habits that don't rely on reminders or outside help. One tip, check off each task as you go. Whether you cross it out with a pen, move a magnet, or tap complete on an app- that small action helps you stay focused and feel accomplished. You don't have to structure every minute of your day, but creating a few anchor points- like set times for meals, work, and breaks- can make your whole schedule feel more manageable.

title: Time Estimation & Scheduling
transcript: ﻿1 Time Estimation and Scheduling. Once you've broken a task into smaller steps, the next challenge is figuring out how long each step will take and actually making time for it. Studies show that many students have difficulty estimating time and monitoring how long tasks are taking. Which makes meeting deadlines and shifting between tasks especially tough. A helpful tool is the task-time sheet where you list each step, estimate how long it should take, then track how long it actually takes. Over time, this helps build a more accurate internal clock, which is something many students struggle with. It's not about being perfect- it's about learning from patterns and improving with practice. Another effective strategy is the Pomodoro Technique. The Pomodoro technique was developed in the late 1980s by Francesco Cirillo, an Italian university student who struggled with focus. He started timing his study sessions using a kitchen timer shaped like a tomato- "pomodoro" in Italian. The method he created was simple: work for 25 minutes, take a 5-minute break, and repeat. That short burst of focus, followed by regular breaks, helped him stay on track without burning out. Today, the technique is used by students, professionals, and productivity coaches all over the world. Research has shown that this method significantly boosts focus and reduces burnout for students. This can help maintain focus, especially for tasks that feel too long or boring. If 25 minutes feels too hard, start with 10 or 15. The key is to get going! You can also try the 2-Minute and 5-Minute Rules- popular with neurodivergent creators and professionals. If a task will take 2 minutes or less, do it immediately. If it takes 5 minutes, try setting a timer and see how much progress you can make. Even five minutes of progress can break the cycle of procrastination and give you a sense of accomplishment. At Gladeo, our founder uses this exact method to manage her ADHD. It's a simple way to trick your brain into starting. "As someone with ADHD, I rely on the 2-Minute and 5-Minute Rules. If it takes 2 minutes, I just knock it out. If it feels overwhelming, I set a timer for 5 minutes and just start- usually that's all it takes to get going. It's a simple trick, but it really helps me stay on track." Once you've estimated time, plug each step into your calendar or planner. Use alarms, reminders, or notifications to nudge yourself. Block out time in advance- even 30 minutes a day for a week can help you finish a big project without the last-minute panic. The goal isn't to be perfect with time. It's to be realistic and prepared. The more you practice estimating and scheduling, the better you'll get- and the more in control you'll feel.

title: Task Analysis: From Big to Bite-Size
transcript: ﻿1 Task Analysis. From Big to Bite-Size. When a task feels overwhelming, it's often because we're looking at the whole mountain instead of the individual steps it takes to climb it. That's where task analysis comes in. It's the skill of breaking a big task into smaller, manageable actions, and it's a game-changer for people who struggle with executive functioning. Studies show that when students learn how to plan, organize, and break tasks into smaller steps, they're more likely to succeed in school as especially students who have challenges with executive functioning. Let's say your assignment is to write a research paper. That might sound like one giant task, but it's actually a series of steps. One, pick a topic. Two, write an outline. Three, find three sources. Four, draft the paper, five, edit and revise, and six, turn it in! Each of those can become its own line on a checklist or a sticky note on your desk. Suddenly, instead of "write a paper," your brain sees a clear path forward. Smaller steps mean smaller decisions and fewer chances to get overwhelmed or stuck. Writing these steps down also helps you estimate how long things will take, which is crucial for planning your schedule and meeting deadlines. Students can often have trouble visualizing how much time something really takes. So identifying each step improves both focus and time awareness.

title: Breaking Down Tasks & Managing Time
transcript: ﻿1 Task Analysis. From Big to Bite-Size. When a task feels overwhelming, it's often because we're looking at the whole mountain instead of the individual steps it takes to climb it. That's where task analysis comes in. It's the skill of breaking a big task into smaller, manageable actions, and it's a game-changer for people who struggle with executive functioning. Studies show that when students learn how to plan, organize, and break tasks into smaller steps, they're more likely to succeed in school as especially students who have challenges with executive functioning. Let's say your assignment is to write a research paper. That might sound like one giant task, but it's actually a series of steps. One, pick a topic. Two, write an outline. Three, find three sources. Four, draft the paper, five, edit and revise, and six, turn it in! Each of those can become its own line on a checklist or a sticky note on your desk. Suddenly, instead of "write a paper," your brain sees a clear path forward. Smaller steps mean smaller decisions and fewer chances to get overwhelmed or stuck. Writing these steps down also helps you estimate how long things will take, which is crucial for planning your schedule and meeting deadlines. Students can often have trouble visualizing how much time something really takes. So identifying each step improves both focus and time awareness.

title: Choosing & Integrating AT
transcript: ﻿1 Choosing and Integrating AT. Finding the right assistive tech isn't about grabbing the flashiest tool- it's about discovering what works for you, your brain, and your environment! And just like learning a new app or a piece of software, it takes a little trial, error, and patience. Start with tryouts. Many colleges have assistive tech labs or lending programs where you can test tools before committing. Online resources like the Job Accommodation Network also break down tools by need, with real-world examples and comparisons. Once you've found a tool you like, invest a little time in learning it! Tutorials, shortcut guides, and help forums can turn something clunky into second nature. Some tools even have training programs or communities built in- take advantage! Don't forget backup plans. Save your settings to the cloud, keep spare batteries or chargers handy, and know what to do if your go-to tool glitches mid-exam or workday. That safety net matters. And here's a pro tip: You might already have powerful accessibility tools on your device! Apple, Android, and Window systems all include built-in features like voice control, zoom, focus modes, screen readers, and more. Explore your settings. You might be surprised by what's there, and what's free!

title: Assistive Technology (AT) for Learning & Productivity
transcript: ﻿1 Tech in Action. Let's bring assistive tech to life with a few real-world snapshots- where the right tools help break down barriers and boost independence. College. Screen reader for a research paper. Maya is blind and needs to review dozens of journal articles for a final project. She uses a screen reader, JAWS, to navigate online databases and have articles read aloud. With keyboard shortcuts and a text-to-speech setting at 1.8 times speed, she's able to keep pace with her classmates- and stay ahead of deadlines. College. Smart pen and text-to-speech for lecture notes. Luis has dyslexia and finds it hard to write notes and follow lectures at the same time. He uses a smart pen that records audio while he writes, syncing spoken words to his notes. After class, he plugs his notes into a text-to-speech tool to review them aloud. It helps him catch key concepts and prepare for tests more confidently! Workplace. Managing focus in an open office. Amira is autistic and also has ADHD. Her workplace is full of distractions: phones ringing, people chatting, constant movement. She combines a few tools- noise-canceling headphones, a visual Pomodoro timer, and a task management app- to create a focused workflow. Her productivity increases, and so does her confidence. Workplace. Voice dictation and eye-tracking for accessibility. Darren is an accountant with limited mobility due to a spinal cord injury. He uses voice dictation software for emails and reports, and an eye-tracking device to control his mouse. With a few customized settings, he's able to complete spreadsheets, manage deadlines, and lead client calls- all from his adaptive workstation. These stories are just a glimpse of what's possible. Assistive tech isn't one-size-fits-all- but when the right tool meets the right need, it opens up new ways to learn, work, and thrive.

title: Real Life Snapshot
transcript: ﻿1 Real-Life Snapshot. Let's see how this plays out in real life- on both campus and on the job. College. Jordan uses the DSO to fix a mic issue. Jordan is a sophomore with moderate hearing loss. At the start of the semester, he requests real-time captions or CART for his large lecture class. The DSO arranges it! But after the first session, Jordan notices he can't hear the instructor clearly when they turn away from the mic. Instead of dropping the class, Jordan emails the DSO! "Hi, I really appreciate the CART support in my biology lecture. I've noticed that when the professor turns away from the mic, I lose some of the live captioning context. Could we explore solution?" The DSO contacts the instructor and provides a lapel mic, which stays clipped on no matter where the professor moves. Problem solved! And Jordan stays on track! Work. Marco works with HR to improve office access. Marco recently started a new job at a tech company. He uses a wheelchair and notices that while most of the building is accessible, the main entrance has a heavy manual door that's hard to open solo. He emails HR, "Hi, I've noticed the front door isn't easy to navigate independently with my wheelchair. Would it be possible to install an automatic opener as a reasonable accommodation under the ADA?" HR responds within a few days, confirms the request and fast tracks the installation. They also connect Marco with facilities to adjust the height of his desk and install pull-out shelves. A few simple modifications- and Marco's workday just got a lot easier! The DSO and HR aren't just paperwork processors- they're your access partners! Their job is to support your learning and work environment, while keeping your information private and protected! When you communicate clearly and stay in touch, you make it easier for them to do their job- and for you to do yours. Next, we'll dive into assistive technology that can help you stay organized, focused, and fully engaged in school or on the job!

title: Best Practices When Working with HR
transcript: ﻿1 Best Practices When Working with HR. Even though the ADA has your back, navigating workplace accommodations can feel tricky. Here are five strategies to make the process smoother and more effective: One. Come prepared. Before your meeting, think through what kind of support would genuinely help you succeed. Be ready to explain the barrier and suggest specific solutions. If you have documentation from a provider, bring it- especially if HR requests it. Two. Use clear confident language. You don't need to share every detail of your condition. Just focus on how it impacts your ability to do the job and what adjustments would help. Use phrases like "I'm requesting reasonable accommodations under the ADA." "This support would allow me to meet expectations and perform at my best." Three. Follow-up in writing. After any conversation, send a quick email to confirm what was discussed. It creates a helpful paper trail and reduces the chance of misunderstandings later. For example: "Thanks for taking the time to meet. Just to recap, we discuss trying a quieter workspace and checking back in after two weeks." Four. Know your resources. You don't have to figure it all out alone. The Job Accommodation Network, or "JAN," offers free, expert advice on common accommodations and how to request them. In a 2019 to 2022 survey, 68.4% of employers said accommodations were vary or extremely effective, and another 18.3% rated them as somewhat effective after consulting the network. Some workplaces also have Employee Resource Groups, or ERGs, for people with disabilities, and joining one can give you extra community and guidance. Five. Stay open and flexible. The first solution may not be perfect. That's okay! Keep the conversation going until you land on something that works. The goal isn't perfection- it's functionality and access.

title: Tips on Working with your DSO
transcript: ﻿1 Tips on Working with Your DSO. Once you've registered and received your accommodations, the DSO remains a key ally throughout your time in school. But like any support system, it works best when you stay connected. Here are four tips on how to efficiently work with your DSO. Tip one. Build the relationship. Introduce yourself early, not just when there's a problem. If the office host orientation sessions, drop by. If you get assigned a specific staff contact, learn their name and let them know how things are going once classes begin. Tip two. Keep them updated. If an accommodation isn't working- maybe the assistive tech is glitchy or a professor keeps forgetting to upload materials- tell the DSO. They can help adjust your plan or step in to fix issues before they snowball. Tip three. Ask about extras. Some DSOs offer more than just letters. You might find an assistive technology lab, access to peer mentors, workshops on study skills, or even quiet study spaces during finals. Ask what's available- you might be surprised! Tip four. Use the appeal process if needed. If an accommodation is denied, you're allowed to appeal the decision. The DSO can walk you through how it works. You don't have to accept "no" without understanding your options. When you treat the DSO like a partner- not just a paperwork stop- you're more likely to get the access and flexibility you need to thrive.

title: Free Tools
transcript: Free Tools There are a few free tools to experiment with for fun. Once you try a few, you'll understand how far reaching AI's impacts are going to be in the near future. Bard, Google's free chatbot. Bing Chat, Microsoft's free chatbot. Brain.fm, free music to focus better. Canva AI, free creative image generator. Character.AI, free fictional character dialogue generator. ChatGPT, free Chatbot from OpenAI. Chat YouTube, free YouTube video chatbot. Copy.ai, free copywriting tool. Craiyon, free image maker. Dall-E, free AI generated images. Jukebox, free audio generator. Lalal, free music track isolator. MuseNet, free music composer. Opus, free video clip maker. Quizalize, free quiz maker. Soundraw, free music composer. Stable Diffusion, free text to image program. Supermeme, free meme generator. Synthesia, free video making software. and Tableau free data analysis.

title: Scenario: Julia’s Journey — High School to College to Career
transcript: ﻿1 Scenario: Julia's journey High school to College to Career. Meet Julia. She's a high school junior with a reading disability and ADHD. One day she gets an email: "IEP meeting Tuesday at 3 o'clock. See you there!" At the meeting, Julia shares that dense reading passages are slowing her down. The team listens. By the end of the meeting, they update her IEP and install reading support software on her school laptop. Fast forward to college. Julia's on campus for move in day, suitcase in one hand, paperwork in the other. She's ready. She submits her documentation, signs a release, and picks up her accommodation letters. "Hi, Dr. Torres. I have approved accommodations for audio versions of assigned readings. Attached is my letter. Thank you!" After graduation, Julia lands her first job at a fast-paced marketing agency. It's exciting- but loud. The noise makes it hard to concentrate. "I'm requesting noise- canceling headphones as a reasonable accommodation under ADA Title I. This will help me stay focused and meet deadlines." Request Approved! Julia chooses the headphones and gets back to work. In every setting, Julia's needs changed- but so did her confidence. By knowing her rights and advocating clearly, she kept moving forward. Understanding how support shifts over time helps you stay one step ahead. In high school, the system usually finds you. In college and at work, it's your move. But when you know what to expect, you're ready to speak up, plan ahead, and get what you need. Up next: how to make those conversations easier- with language that's clear, confident, and built to get results.

title: The Transition Roadmap
transcript: ﻿1 The Transition Roadmap. High School support finds you. In K to 12 public schools, your support system is built in. If you're eligible under IDEA or Section 504, your school is required to evaluate your needs, create a plan, and provide accommodations without you needing to ask. Your parents or guardians are included in meetings, and if something isn't working, it's the school's job to address it. For example: If your IEP says you get extra time on tests, teachers are expected to know that and provide it - no reminders needed! College. You have to ask. Once you graduate IDEA ends. From here on out, you need to take the lead. In college Section 504 and ADA still protect your right to accommodations - but only if you register with the Disability Services Office and request them. Professors won't get notified unless you provide documentation, and they're not required to accommodate you without it. For example: If you need captioned videos or a note-taker, you need to ask for that before classes begin, usually in writing. Workplace. You negotiate. At work, your protections come from the ADA's Title I. But again, support doesn't show up automatically. You decide when-or if-to disclose your disability. If you want accommodations like a flexible schedule, screen-reader software, or acquired or workspace, you'll need to start a respectful conversation with your manager or HR. That means explaining the barrier, proposing a solution, and being open to alternatives. For example: "I have a condition that affects stamina. To do my job effectively, I'm requesting a sit-stand desk as a reasonable accommodation under the ADA."

title: Building Strong Relationships
transcript: ﻿1 Building Strong Relationships. Building strong relationships is one of the most important but most overlooked parts of career planning. So in this lesson, let's talk about how your relationships can affect your future. Think of any successful person, whether you know them in real life or not. It can be a family member, a celebrity, or just someone you admire. Now, try to imagine the path that person followed to get from where they started to where they are now. What steps did they take? What education and training did they pursue? What sort of challenges did they have to overcome? And very importantly, who helped them along the way? On their path to success, those people had others who supported them on their rise to the top. They didn't do it alone. In one way or another, they had assistance. Because for anyone to succeed in today's hectic world, it requires us helping each other. That help can come in a variety of ways. Maybe it's a parent who encourages you to get good grades in a subject that's hard. Or a life-changing bit of career advice from a school guidance counselor, or perhaps it's your first employer who takes a chance on you even if you don't have a ton of experience. We all need an occasional boost on our climb up the ladder. In fact, many successful people can trace back to exactly who helped them and how and when. For example, during her best supporting actress acceptance speech at the 96th Academy Awards, Da'Vine Joy Randolph expressed deep gratitude to her college acting professor, Ron Van Lieu. As the only black woman in her acting class, she struggled to see herself in the industry, but Van Lieu's support and encouraging words, "We're going to forge our own path. You'll lay a trail for yourself" - gave her the confidence to succeed! During a live TV special, acclaimed singer Adele shared a heartfelt moment about her former English teacher, Ms. McDonald, who sparked her love for literature and storytelling. "She was so cool, so engaging, she really made us care and we knew that she cared about us." Adele said, explaining how Ms. McDonald's belief in her talent inspired her. After her words, the hosts surprised Adele by bringing Ms. McDonald on stage for an emotional reunion that moved the singer to tears, showcasing the profound impact a dedicated teacher can have on a student's life! Lin-Manuel Miranda, creator and star of the hit Broadway musical, Hamilton, cites his former 8th grade English teacher for setting him on the path to success. One day in English class, Miranda decided to work on a musical he was writing - instead of doing his assigned homework. Instead of getting upset, his teacher, Dr. Rembert Herbert, recognized his young student's emerging talent. "You could be good at this"' he said, "and you should stop hibernating in my class, and you should be doing this." Dr. Herbert then nudged Miranda to join the student theater club, a push that "changed my life forever," Miranda acknowledged in a heartfelt interview with CBS News. Miranda's remarks are a great example of how one person's support and belief in you can shape your entire destiny. The message brought Miranda to tears, underscoring the profound impact a teacher's belief can have on a student's life. Some people may never fully recognize the impact others had in their lives. Some might even say they "made it on their own," but that's rarely the case. Somewhere in their past, somebody supported those people, too, whether they realized it or not. That's why we all need to build strong relationships, and support one another as we pursue our dreams, whatever those may be. And the more help you have, the better! As author and activist, Helen Keller once said, "Alone we can do so little; together we can do so much." Keller was the first deafblind person in the US to earn a bachelor's degree. She also piloted a plane for 20 minutes over the Mediterranean Sea from Paris to Rome!

title: Informational Interview Tips
transcript: Nailing your Job Interview Getting a job interview is exciting. It's the culmination of hours of hard work and a chance to hopefully land a great job. It can be stressful though because you're competing with other applicants who want the same job. You may also feel nervous about sitting in a room with people asking you questions, but don't worry, there are things you can do to make a fantastic impression on those people. So let's review the steps you can take to nail your next interview.

title: Example of a First Message
transcript: Được rồi, vậy thì chuyển sang giai đoạn chuyển tiếp tiếp theo, tìm được công việc đầu tiên sau khi tốt nghiệp đại học, bạn làm điều đó như thế nào? Tôi nghĩ điều quan trọng nhất là phải có một tâm trí cởi mở. Hãy có một tâm trí cởi mở để lựa chọn, để có thể nhảy vào một lĩnh vực nghề nghiệp đang tuyển dụng, ít nhất là tương tự như ngành của bạn. Hãy đến hội chợ việc làm. Các hội chợ việc làm thật tuyệt vời, đặc biệt là nếu bạn chỉ tìm thấy một số hội chợ chỉ liên quan đến lĩnh vực của bạn. Chắc chắn hãy nghiên cứu về những người sẽ ở đó. Bởi vì nếu bạn có thể tra cứu một chút về một công ty và tìm ra một vài công ty mà bạn thực sự thích cụ thể, hãy nghiên cứu một chút về những gì họ làm và mục tiêu của họ là gì. Tìm hiểu tuyên bố sứ mệnh của họ là gì. Bạn hãy đến và nói chuyện với họ về hội chợ việc làm đó với thông tin đó trong tay. Họ sẽ ấn tượng và họ sẽ quan tâm vì họ sẽ nói, Này, bạn đã nghiên cứu về họ và thể hiện nỗ lực để biết họ làm gì và họ muốn làm gì. Họ sẽ thể hiện sự quan tâm đến bạn vì họ nhận ra rằng bạn là kiểu người chủ động một chút và, và cũng học hỏi nữa, đó là chủ đề mà tôi đã nói trước đó. Dành thời gian để học, dành thời gian để nghiên cứu. Bạn cho thấy rằng bạn sẵn sàng làm điều đó. Nó đã cho họ thấy rằng bạn có một trong những kỹ năng quan trọng mà họ đang tìm kiếm.

title: Bringing it All Together
transcript: ﻿1 Bringing It All Together. Reflecting and journaling are powerful tools for building resilience because they help you connect the dots between your experiences and your personal growth. The simple act of writing down your challenges and the steps you took to overcome them creates a narrative of strength. You can revisit whenever you feel stuck or uncertain. By tracking your progress over time, you begin to see how each setback teaches you something valuable, and how each success story adds another brick to your foundation of confidence. It doesn't matter if your entries are polished or messy, long-winded, or just a few bullet points. What matters is the habit of pausing, thinking about what happened, and noting what you can learn from it. Keep at it, and you'll discover that reflection isn't just an end-of-the-day exercise, it's a mindset that turns everyday ups and downs into building blocks for future success. So grab a notebook or open a fresh document and give journaling a try. You'll likely find that putting your experiences into words helps you process them more fully, and reminds you in real time that you're always learning, always growing, and always capable of bouncing back!

title: Reflecting on What You've Learned
transcript: ﻿1 Reflecting on What You've Learned. Along with logging events and feelings, take time to consider what each experience taught you. Sometimes the lesson might be practical, like learning a new study technique or discovering a better way to manage your time. Other times, it might be about emotional growth: maybe you learned how to communicate more effectively with a friend, or how to set healthier boundaries in a relationship. Try asking yourself questions that spark deeper thinking: "What did I do well this week and why did it work?" "What was harder than I expected and how can I approach it differently next time?" "Who helped me, and what can I learn from their support?" These prompts guide you toward constructive reflection, ensuring you're not just recording what happened but also uncovering the meaning behind it.

title: Reminding Yourself of Your Resilience
transcript: ﻿1 Reminding Yourself of Your Resilience. When life throws curveballs - like an unexpected bad grade or a personal conflict. It's easy to get discouraged and think, "I can't handle this." Journaling can counteract that negativity. You can literally flip back {or scroll up} to an entry where you faced something similar and came out okay, or even stronger. Seeing proof of your past victories helps you realize you're more capable than you might feel in the moment. Every time you note how you dealt with a challenge, you are adding to a collection of success stories. Even failures become valuable lessons once you write down what went wrong and how you adapted.

title: Finding Your Own Approach
transcript: ﻿1 Finding Your Own Approach. Everyone's style of reflection is a little different, so experiment to see what works for you. Some people like a traditional diary format - writing a few paragraphs each night before bed. Others prefer bullet journaling, listing key points about their day, including obstacles and triumphs. You could also try recording voice memos if you're not a fan of writing or vlogging to capture your thoughts in the moment. The goal is to capture your experiences and insights in a way that resonates with you. It doesn't have to be daily or even weekly, as long as you're consistent enough to see a pattern over time. Let yourself be creative. Doodle, write poetry, or add photos and mementos. The important thing is that you're pausing to reflect and giving yourself credit for the challenges you've overcome.

title: How Journaling Helps
transcript: ﻿1 How Journaling Helps. Journaling makes reflection tangible thoughts that bounce around in your mind become real, visible words on a page or a screen. This simple act of writing can reduce stress and anxiety because you're getting your worries out of your head. It also creates a safe space to process complex emotions, whether you're excited about a new opportunity or disappointed about a setback. Over time, your journal entries build a personal map of your journey. Maybe you'll notice how a math concept that once terrified you, now feels easier, or how you handled a conflict with a friend more calmly than you did a year ago. Looking back at your old entries can offer proof of how resilient you are. If you ever doubt your ability to bounce back, all you have to do is re-read a few entries and see the progress for yourself. A study from Montana State University found that middle school students who engaged in regular nature journaling developed more positive attitudes towards science and the natural world. And you can apply this to any subject matter! No matter your age, journaling can enhance your connection to your personal goals.

title: Why Reflecting Matters
transcript: ﻿1 Why Reflecting Matters? Reflection is more than just thinking about what happened. It's a deliberate pause to sift through your experiences and figure out which parts hold lessons you can carry forward. When you reflect, you transform life's events into meaningful insights. Instead of just saying, "That test was hard". You might realize, "I struggled with that test because I didn't practice time management. Next time, I'll start studying earlier." According to the California Learning Resource Network, reflection can help you solidify your understanding of new concepts and retain information more effectively. How does it work? Well, the process of looking back helps you see patterns - like recurring obstacles or repeated successes - that you might otherwise miss. By identifying those patterns, you can celebrate your strengths and come up with new strategies to tackle your weaknesses. Ultimately, reflection is like shining a flashlight on your path. It helps you see what's behind you so you can walk forward with more clarity and self-awareness.

title: Mindfulness Break: Capture Your Journey
transcript: ﻿1 Capture Your Journey. The Power of Reflection and Journaling. Life can get busy. We move from one activity to the next - classes, part-time jobs, family obligations - without pausing to take stock of everything we've gone through. That's where reflection and journaling come in. When you've set aside time to process your experiences, you gain valuable insights into who you are, what you've learned, and how far you've come. It's like putting a bookmark in your life story so you can flip back later to see just how much you've grown. Journaling doesn't have to be fancy or time-consuming. It can be as simple as opening a notebook or a notes app on your phone and writing down your thoughts about the day. Maybe you know the challenge you faced, how you felt about it, and what you learned from it. Over time, these entries form a record of your personal and academic journey - a track record of resilience you can look back on whenever you need a boost of confidence.

title: Style, Content, Formatting, and Format
transcript: ﻿1 Style, Content, Formatting and Formats. You may hear terms like resume style, format, and formatting used interchangeably. Resume style refers to the overall visual design, such as colors and graphics. Formatting refers to the technical aspects such as margins and font sizes. Format refers to how the information is structured. All of these things need to be simple and organized so ATS programs can read the content. Plain resumes are easier to read, but can seem a bit boring. Many job seekers want to add a little flare by using attention getting designs, graphics, and interesting fonts on their resumes. This approach may be suitable in some cases such as creative design jobs. The downside is that ATS might have trouble reviewing a resume if there are graphics in the way, the font isn't recognized or the color scheme interferes. When in doubt, keep it simple and let your portfolio serve as your creative showcase if needed. Photos should also be avoided unless the job ad calls for one due to the nature of the work. Many employers prohibit resumes that feature the applicant's photograph because it could lead to unintentional bias, or allegations of bias if the person isn't hired. Content. Your resume's content will include a Header listing your name, address, city, state, zip code, email, phone number, LinkedIn profile, and the URL to your online portfolio. You can also include your preferred pronouns and relevant social media if it is professional and necessary to demonstrate your skills or experience. The next sections should feature standard headings to ensure the ATS correctly categorizes your information. If using the most common format, these sections include: a summary or statement, which is just a brief one or two line summary of your work experience or a statement about your objective. For example: "Experienced digital marketer with five plus years in campaign management, seeking to drive results in a dynamic marketing team." Your Work Experience, listing previous and current jobs in reverse chronological order with the most recent first. Include the job title, company name, location, I.e., the city and state abbreviation and dates of employment in months and years. Under each job listing, write bullet points describing your responsibilities and achievements. Bullet points should use the "APR" format, listing your Achievement, the Purpose behind it, and the measurable Result. For example: "Reduce project completion time by 15% by streamlining workflow processes, decreasing costs, and increasing efficiency." List quantifiable data, such as numbers, percentages, dollar amounts, et cetera., when possible. If you haven't held a job yet, there are ways to beef up this section such as volunteering, internships, and freelance work. Your Education, listing your credentials, certifications, and degrees in reverse chronological order. For example: For credentials or certifications, list the issuer and name, e.g., Microsoft Certified - Cybersecurity Architect Expert. For degrees, include the degree type, major school name, and graduation date, or expected date. An optional additional information section to list relevant skills, e.g., software you're proficient in, second languages you know, or technical or soft skills you possess. You can also include awards, honors, and recognitions you've received, volunteer experiences, and community service activities. Formatting. In terms of formatting, resume should be able to fit on a standard letter-size eight and a half by 11 inch piece of paper. For margins and fonts, we suggest the following: Margins should be approximately one inch on all sides. Fonts should be professional, such as Arial, Calibri, Times New Roman, or Georgia. Font sizes should be 10 to 12 points, except for elements such as your name and headings. These can be slightly larger or put in bold or italics. Line spacing should be roughly 1.15, so sentences are easy to read. Formats. As per formats, the most common are chronological, functional, and combination. Chronological. The chronological resume format focuses on your prior work history with your most recent job first. This is a good format when looking for a new job that relates to your previous job. Functional. The functional format focuses more on skills than work history, which may be better for people switching careers or those without much work experience. Combination. The combination format is simply a hybrid of both. For the combination format, you'd list applicable skills, past achievements, and qualifications followed by your work history in reverse chronological order, then your education and professional credentials. Targeted. The phrase "targeted resume" doesn't refer to a different format. It just means the resume is customized to a specific job posting. This is important because it shows the employer you took extra time to prepare it just for them. That's why we recommend always doing a targeted resume. You can use any format for a targeted resume. Every employer has their own particular format preference, but remember, the most important factor is the content, not the format.

title: Google Tools & Resources
transcript: Google Tools and Resources Everyone's heard of Google. It's one of the world's leading technology companies known for its powerful search engine and popular Chrome web browser. Google also owns Android, an open source operating system used on countless smartphones and tablets, but that's just the tip of the iceberg. This lesson highlights Google's ever-growing array of free online products and services. Google's wide range of free resources can be especially helpful for students, job seekers, and career development. These include tools such as Gmail, a free email account that can be used for messaging, recruiters, hiring managers, colleges, and others. Google Docs, a free word processing app that's great for writing resumes, cover letters, college essays and other documents. And Google Drive, a free cloud storage service for saving, organizing and sharing files. Most of Google's services also feature upgraded versions you can pay for, but many people just use the free versions. Google also provides educational tools like Google Classroom, which facilitates organized learning and collaboration for students and educators. Google Digital Garage, offering free courses to help job seekers and professionals develop digital skills for career advancement and Google Skill Shop, which provides job seekers with industry recognized certifications that can boost their employability. In addition, Google owns YouTube where users can access millions of free educational videos and tutorials for learning and developing career skills. Other powerful free tools include calendar for scheduling and reminders. Google Books, a vast library of resources for research and continuous learning. Keep for note taking. Meet for video conferencing, virtual job interviews and professional networking. Sheets for spreadsheets and reports, as well as academic and career tasks. Slides, for presentations that can be used for classroom projects and idea pitches and Translate, which gives students and job seekers access to opportunities in multiple languages. In the artificial intelligence space, Google AI helps users with powerful tools like Google Assistant, a voice controlled virtual assistant; Google Lens, a visual search tool for identifying objects and translating text, and Gemini, Google's AI model for natural human-like text interactions, which can answer questions, write drafts, analyze images, automate processes, and much more. For teams, there's even Google Workspace, a suite of productivity tools designed for collaboration and efficiency. This overview highlights Google's vast ecosystem and some of its many useful features. While other companies offer similar products and services, Google remains a popular choice due to its commitment to providing a wide range of resources for free. Disclaimer: Gladeo does not endorse or promote the use of Google or any specific products or services mentioned in this content. The information provided is for educational purposes only.

title: Take Charge of Your Career Development!
transcript: Take charge of your career development Whether you are looking to update your current skills, learn something entirely new, or gain credentials that will set you apart in the job market, the resources are at your fingertips, so take charge of your career development today. Explore the wealth of online courses available. Choose the ones that align with your goals and start building the skills that will help you succeed in the future of work.

title: Financial Support
transcript: Financial support for learning If the cost of courses is a concern, there are several ways to reduce or cover these expenses: scholarships and financial aid. Many online learning platforms, universities and specialty schools offer scholarships or financial aid to help cover the cost of courses. It's worth researching what's available, especially for programs that align with your career goals. Employer tuition reimbursement. Some employers offer tuition reimbursement programs where they cover part or all of the cost of courses that will benefit your work. It's a great idea to check with your HR department to see if your company offers this benefit. Company-sponsored learning. In some cases, employers may directly pay for you to take a course if it's relevant to your job or the company's needs. This could include online courses, certifications, or even attending workshops and conferences.

title: How to Get Started
transcript: How to Get Started Getting started with skill building is easier than ever. Begin by identifying the skills that are most relevant to your career goals or the industry you want to enter. Consider what areas you want to improve in or new fields you're interested in exploring. Then, search for courses on the platforms we presented in the previous section. Many courses are self-paced, allowing you to learn at your own speed, and a lot of them are free or low cost making skill building an accessible option for everyone.

title: Types of Skill Building Opportunities
transcript: Types of Skill Building Opportunities There are many online platforms and resources where you can enhance your skills. Here are some top options: 1. Online Learning Platforms edx.org and coursera.org. These platforms offer a wide range of courses taught by professors from top universities around the world. The beauty of edX and Coursera is that they provide the opportunity to try courses for free, meaning you can learn at no cost. From business and technology to humanities and science, there's a course for nearly every interest. Udemy and LinkedIn Learning Udemy provides thousands of affordable courses on almost any topic you can think of, and LinkedIn Learning offers, courses in business, technology, and creative fields, often taught by industry experts. Both platforms are great for learners who want flexibility and a wide range of options. Udacity and Pluralsight, these platforms specialize in tech and professional skills. Udacity offers nano degree programs in areas like data science, AI, and digital marketing. While Pluralsight focuses on technology skills such as software development and IT operations. Skillshare and Masterclass. If you're looking for creative skills like design, photography, writing, or cooking, Skillshare offers a variety of courses and Masterclass provides lessons from celebrities and experts in a wide range of fields, from filmmaking to entrepreneurship. Khan Academy Known for its free educational content, Khan Academy offers courses in math, science, economics and more. It's a great resource for brushing up on foundational skills or exploring new subjects. Two, University-Led and Specialty Schools University-Led Courses Many prestigious universities now offer free online courses directly through their websites. For example, Stanford, Harvard, and MIT provide access to courses that were once available only to enrolled students. These courses can give you insights from leading academic minds and help you gain knowledge that is recognized globally. Community Colleges and Vocational Schools Local community colleges often offer affordable courses in a wide range of subjects. From technical skills to the arts and vocational schools, focus on practical training in specific fields like healthcare, coding, or the trades. These institutions are excellent options, if you prefer in-person learning or need more specialized instruction. Three, Industry-Specific Training Company Provided Learning Many leading companies offer free or affordable courses, sometimes called massive, open online courses that are directly tied to industry needs. For instance, Google's Grow with Google platform provides training and digital marketing, data analysis and more, with credentials that are highly regarded in the tech industry. Unity, a leading game development platform offers Unity Learn, where you can acquire game development skills, Certification Programs Some platforms also offer certification programs that validate your new skills with industry recognized credentials. These certifications can be a powerful addition to your resume, signaling to employers that you have the knowledge and expertise required in specific areas.

title: Why Focus on Skill Building?
transcript: ﻿1 How "Why" Helps Career Planning. Asking why is especially important when we're talking about career planning, and choosing our education and training pathways. You not only need to know why you're interested in a particular career field, but you should understand the larger purpose behind that interest. That way you can connect to it and use it to keep yourself motivated over the long haul, because most jobs require a lot of time and commitment to learn how to do them properly. Some jobs may only take a few weeks to learn the basics. For example, to become a barista, you can learn how to make different coffee orders pretty quickly. But it may take months of practice to perfect every drink on the menu or to create detailed latte art! If your why behind wanting to be a barista is simply to get a part-time job in college, that's fine because you don't have to invest much time and effort to learn the basics. But there are other jobs that require several years of intense training and education. Such as becoming a licensed commercial pilot, which involves rigorous coursework, hundreds of flight hours, and intense exams. The longer and more difficult the training and education requirements, the more vital it is to have a strong reason why you're doing it. Otherwise, you might wake up one day and realize, "Hey, I don't want to do this for a living!" The bottom line is keep your why, your purpose or reason for pursuing a goal in mind often. Tell yourself regularly why that goal matters to you!

title: Why Skill-Building Courses?
transcript: Financial support for learning If the cost of courses is a concern, there are several ways to reduce or cover these expenses: scholarships and financial aid. title: Cleaning Up Your Footprint
transcript: Cleaning up Your Footprint Keeping your digital footprint professional can help you maintain a positive online reputation. So let's go over tips to clean up your current footprint. First, go online and run a search for your name using different search engines such as Google. Look for any content associated with your name that could be viewed in a negative light. Work on improving or removing it. Review your social media accounts and delete old or unused ones. Do a search of your usernames too, and even keywords you don't wanna be associated with you. If a post exists with something you don't want anyone to see anymore, delete it now before anyone else finds it later. Likewise, review who is interacting with your posts and what kinds of comments they tag you in. If a post exists with interactions or comments you don't wanna associate with, consider deleting the post altogether. Use privacy settings to control who sees your posts and personal information and who can tag you in their posts. Consider setting up a Google alert for your name to monitor new mentions. Keep your footprint clean by doing the following. Think before you post. You don't have to censor yourself, but try to not post things that could come back to haunt you later. When in doubt, wait before posting. Post positive things about yourself, such as news about your achievements and activities. Update your profiles on a regular basis to ensure a professional appearance. Review your old footprint on occasion. Sometimes we post things that seem like a good idea at the time, but if we see them days or weeks later, our attitudes might have changed. Do reviews to see if other people have posted about you. Use tools like Google's URL Removal tool to manage your search results if desired. If necessary, consider deleting social media profiles and creating new ones. Managing your digital footprint is an ongoing process. The more active you are online, the more regular attention it'll take. But in the end, it'll be worth the effort to make sure your footprint reflects well on your real world self.

title: Types of Digital Footprints
transcript: Types of Digital Footprints There are two types of digital footprints, passive and active. Passive footprints are created when data is collected about us, such as our browsing history or data collected by apps. Active footprints are the ones we create through actions like posting on social media. These are the ones colleges and employers may review to access your character and suitability. If they find content in your footprint that they feel is inappropriate or offensive, they may choose not to hire you or accept you into their school or program.

title: Your Digital Footprint
transcript: If you are watching this, you probably grew up with smartphones, laptops, and other cutting edge technology connected to the internet. Virtually everything we do on the web contributes to our digital footprint. A trail of data we leave behind when we search the internet, visit websites, engage on social media, and so on. Our information is floating out there in the ether, offering free insights about who we are to anyone who wants to look. So who's looking? It's hard to know for sure, but we can assume it could be college application review boards, job recruiters and hiring managers. Our digital footprints make it easy for people to find out things about us, but sometimes they might find things we'd rather they didn't see because the information could make them feel biased against us. In other words, the details in our digital footprints can affect our lives and futures.
