title: Surrounding Yourself with Positive Relationships
transcript: ﻿1 Surrounding Yourself with Positive Relationships. In high school, it's probably easy to believe your future success is all on you about you, studying hard, engaging in extracurricular activities, and choosing the "right" education and training path. And that's true to a large extent. But that's only part of the equation. We all rely on the resources of others, whether it's their knowledge, their wisdom, their mentorship, or discipline, or the life experience they share with us. On the opposite side of that coin, what if our relations are only with people who hold us back or pull us away from our goals? When that happens, it's all too easy to get on the wrong track. That's why it's important to intentionally find and surround ourselves with positive people who inspire us, motivate us, believe in us, and push us to be our best selves. Then we can really go after our dreams. How do you do that? One way to start is by paying attention to the people already in your life. Who is always there when you want to talk about your future? Who do you go to when you need advice? Who helps you figure things out when you're feeling stuck? Who shows patience and empathy when they're showing you something? Who's really got your back and will tell you the truth, even if maybe it's not what you want to hear? Those are the types of constructive relationships worth nurturing! Maybe it's a teacher who just gets you, who understands what you're trying to say, even when you're not even sure how to say it. Maybe it's a spiritual mentor who keeps you connected to your beliefs or values, or maybe it's that friend who pushes you to step out of your comfort zone because they believe in you even when you feel a little scared. These types of beneficial connections are on your home team. They're the ones holding the ladder for you so you can climb high and reach your potential.

title: What Stays the Same, What Changes
transcript: ﻿1 What Stays the Same, What Changes. Even as you move from high school to college or from college to the workplace, some rights stay the same. You're still protected from discrimination. You still have the right to ask for reasonable support when you need it. But other things change. One of the biggest shifts, you become your own spokesperson. In high school, parents or teachers might have handled the paperwork or spoken up on your behalf. In college and at work, that role is yours. The focus of support also changes. Instead of modifying your assignments or job tasks, the goal is to ensure access- giving you the tools or flexibility to complete the same work as everyone else. And here's the part that surprises many people. Timing Matters! If you wait too long to register with disability services or delay making a request, you might have to finish that class- or that big work project- without the support you could've had.

title: Expanding Your Circle
transcript: ﻿1 Expanding Your Circle. Next, look for ways to connect with more people, like ones who can introduce you to new ideas or open a door to new opportunities. Just ask around for help - you'll find it's everywhere! It could be someone from your class that you haven't talked to before, a relative you haven't connected with in a while, a new teacher, or someone else in your community. The possibilities are endless! Just remember that these relationships aren't all about you and what you get out of them. You have to be ready to give back, too - even if it's just by doing your best so that the other person's investment of their time and energy in you is worth it. The relationships you build, today and for the rest of your life can benefit you - or keep you from moving forward in the direction you want to go. Not just with your career, but in all aspects of your life. In some way, maybe big or maybe small, they'll shape the person you become and the life you lead. That's how powerful our relationships are. And that's why it's important to choose wisely when you build your network. So be intentional about who you spend time with, who you let into your personal inner circle, your professional network of power. Then keep adding meaningful connections to that growing network. We'll dive more into the idea of networking in a later lesson. For now, remember, the relationships you create today could lead to the opportunities of tomorrow. And that's definitely worth investing your time and energy in. So think about someone you've always wanted to be friends with, and make a point to reach out and try to get to know them!

title: Before the Interview
transcript: Before the Interview Analyze the job description When you see an ad for a job, you want copy and paste the job description into a document. Study every word until you completely understand all the duties and requirements. Remember what we learned in chapter four about keywords. You'll wanna use as many of those as possible because the interviewer is likely listening for those. This will also demonstrate that you did your homework on having a full understanding of the job description. Keep these things in mind during interviews so you can speak about how you meet their needs. Research the organization and interviewers. Don't just look at the job ad. Look at the organization's website too. Get familiar with who they are, what they do, and what their values and mission are. Before the interview, set some time aside to read up on the organization. What is their mission? What have been some milestones in the company? What is the latest news or project they shared? These are some questions to ask yourself while browsing through their website. Make sure to take notes. If you can, check out the organization's social media and the LinkedIn profiles of whoever will be interviewing you too. Practice your elevator pitch. An elevator pitch is a speech that's short enough to make during an elevator ride. Imagine you jump in an elevator with someone you want to impress. You only have a few seconds to sell an idea to them. It needs to be concise and compelling. It's best to always be ready. If an interviewer asks about your background, have your pitch down. Practice it out loud, and focus on experiences directly relevant to the job and why you'd be perfect for it. Review standard interview questions and do mock interviews. It's easy to search online and find common interview questions. You should anticipate questions like, why should we hire you? As well as more detailed job specific questions. There may be questions such as, can you tell us about a time you were faced with a challenge at work and how you overcame it. For these, plan your response using the STAR method. Explain the situation you were in, the task you needed to do, the action you took and the results. You can also practice your responses via mock interviews with a friend, family member, or someone at your school's career center. At the end of this chapter, we will have a mock interview for you too. Prepare questions to ask them. At the end of the interview, interviewers often ask if you have any questions for them. Just remember, that's still part of the interview. Make a very short list of questions you can ask them to demonstrate that you understand the role and are curious to learn more about the organization. Just make sure to ask questions that haven't already been answered during the interview. Some examples of what you can ask are: Can you describe the working culture of the company? Do you provide professional development opportunities? If so, what do those look like or What are some of the challenges I might face in this position? Remember, the only wrong response when the recruiter asks if you have any questions is to say no. Why? Because studies show that nearly half of all interviewers say they won't hire a candidate who shows little knowledge of the employing organization. Know where To Go Research the route to get to the interview location. We recommend you arrive as early as you can, but always pay attention to details given by the recruiter. Before the day of the interview, check how long it takes based on the time of day you'll be traveling. You don't want being late to be your first impression on them. Be ready for remote interviews. These days many interviews are done virtually over the internet, so ensure you'll have the right software downloaded and updated. Some popular methods have been through Zoom or Google Meets. Also, check that your video and audio devices are working properly. Pick a quiet location with good lighting and a professional uncluttered background. If you don't have access to such a space, consider using a virtual background picture you can find on the internet or on the video calling service. Be sure there won't be any interruptions or background noise. Lastly, dress the same as if you were going to an in-person interview.

title: From K-12 to College to Work: How Support Changes
transcript: ﻿1 Success Stories. Let's meet three people who turned their self-knowledge into real world results. You'll hear how they paired their strengths with the right supports- and how it changed the game. Jordan, a high school student with ADHD. Jordan is a high school junior who loves building things- especially robots. But her ADHD made it hard to stay organized. She'd lose track of assignments and missed deadlines, even when she cared about the work. After learning about self-advocacy, Jordan talked with her teachers. She asked if she could record lectures and started using phone alerts to break homework into smaller chunks. With those changes, she started turning things in on time- and even joined the robotics club, where she now helps design competition bots. Lena, a college student who is deaf. Lena is studying biology in college and has been deaf since early childhood. In her lab classes, following fast-moving instructions from professors and lab partners was a constant challenge. So Lena advocated for real-time captioning software. Now she follows everything on her tablet as it happens- and no longer worries about missing critical steps. With that support, she's not just keeping up- she's thriving. This semester, she's leading her project team and preparing to apply to grad school. Arman, a new software tester on the Autism Spectrum. Arman recently got hired as a software tester at a growing tech company. He's autistic, with a strong eye for detail- but struggled when his supervisor gave vague, verbal-only feedback. Rather than stay silent, Arman sent an email asking for feedback in writing. His supervisor agreed- and that simple switch made a big difference. With clear, concrete notes, Arman became the team's top bug-catcher, spotting tiny issues others missed. These stories aren't about "overcoming" disability. They're about using strengths and tools together- so you can succeed on your own terms.

title: Turning Reflection Into Action
transcript: ﻿1 Turning Reflection Into Action. You've explored how disability can come with powerful strengths- and how speaking up can unlock the support you need to thrive. Now it's time to apply what you've learned. Pick one challenge you face regularly- something from class, work, or daily life. Then name one strength you bring to that situation. Finally, choose one small action you can take this week to advocate for yourself. That action might be sending a quick email to request lecture slides in advance, practicing your 15 seconds script in your notes app, or setting a reminder to ask for a quieter space or clear instructions. Save it somewhere that works for you- on a device like the notes app on your phone or even as a reminder on a digital calendar app, or as something physical, like an entry in a journal or on a sticky note by your desk. The format doesn't matter. What matters is showing up for yourself! Because every time you speak up, you're not just asking for support- you're shaping a world that works better for you!

title: Linking Challenges to Supports
transcript: ﻿1 Linking Challenges to Supports. Let's go back to the challenge you thought about earlier- the one that makes school or work feel harder than it should. Now ask yourself: What's one tool, strategy, or change that could make it easier? If you've noticed that reading takes you longer than your classmates, that doesn't mean you're falling behind- it means you might benefit from support. Maybe that's using text-to-speech software that reads the material aloud. Or maybe you request extra time on tests so you're not racing the clock. If you deal with sensory overload- loud noises, fluorescent lights, crowded rooms- you're not just "being dramatic." Your nervous system is asking for a different setup. That could mean bringing your own noise-canceling headphones, asking to sit near a window or away from high-traffic areas, or requesting a quieter room for testing. If you live with chronic pain or fatigue, you might need physical accommodations like a sit-stand desk, a more ergonomic chair, or built-in breaks to stretch and reset. These aren't "special" perks- they're are ways to level the playing field so you can focus on your work, not just on managing discomfort. Sometimes supports are simple. Other times they take a conversation or a formal request. But the first step is the same! Identify what's hard and imagine what would help. And remember if something works for you, even if it's not a formal accommodation, that's still valuable! Finding your own tools is part of the journey. Ready to take it further? In the next section, we'll help you turn this reflection in into a concrete goal!

title: Success Stories
transcript: ﻿1 Success Stories. Let's meet three people who turned their self-knowledge into real world results. You'll hear how they paired their strengths with the right supports- and how it changed the game. Jordan, a high school student with ADHD. Jordan is a high school junior who loves building things- especially robots. But her ADHD made it hard to stay organized. She'd lose track of assignments and missed deadlines, even when she cared about the work. After learning about self-advocacy, Jordan talked with her teachers. She asked if she could record lectures and started using phone alerts to break homework into smaller chunks. With those changes, she started turning things in on time- and even joined the robotics club, where she now helps design competition bots. Lena, a college student who is deaf. Lena is studying biology in college and has been deaf since early childhood. In her lab classes, following fast-moving instructions from professors and lab partners was a constant challenge. So Lena advocated for real-time captioning software. Now she follows everything on her tablet as it happens- and no longer worries about missing critical steps. With that support, she's not just keeping up- she's thriving. This semester, she's leading her project team and preparing to apply to grad school. Arman, a new software tester on the Autism Spectrum. Arman recently got hired as a software tester at a growing tech company. He's autistic, with a strong eye for detail- but struggled when his supervisor gave vague, verbal-only feedback. Rather than stay silent, Arman sent an email asking for feedback in writing. His supervisor agreed- and that simple switch made a big difference. With clear, concrete notes, Arman became the team's top bug-catcher, spotting tiny issues others missed. These stories aren't about "overcoming" disability. They're about using strengths and tools together- so you can succeed on your own terms.

title: What is Self Advocacy
transcript: ﻿1 What is Self-Advocacy? Self-advocacy means being able to explain what you need- clearly, calmly, and with confidence. It's not about demanding special treatment. It's about sharing facts and solutions that help you thrive. There are three core pieces. Know yourself. Understand how your disability shows up for you- and what you are great at. Know your rights. Laws like IDEA, Section 50 , and the ADA protect your access to fair support. Speak up. The way you ask matters. Being clear and confident helps people understand and take action. One way to speak up clearly is by using a simple, goal-oriented script. Here's how to build one: One. Start with your disability. For example: "I have ADHD." Two. Explain how it affects your learning or work, which means it's hard for me to focus when taking quizzes in a loud environment. Three. Describe what support helps. Having access to a quiet testing room makes a big difference. Four. Add the goal that support helps you reach, "so I can stay focused and do my best during exams." Let's put it all together. "I have ADHD, which means it's hard for me to focus when taking quizzes in a loud environment. Having access to a quiet testing room makes a big difference, so I can stay focused and do my best during exams." This is a goal-oriented, clear, and concise statement because it tells the listener what your disability is, how it impacts you, what support you need, and how that support helps you succeed. Let's look at a few more examples. For example, Camilla has dyslexia and has trouble keeping up with lecture slides being presented quickly during class. She needs to bring this up to her professors so she can fully participate in discussions. What's the clearest way to communicate that? A. "I have dyslexia, which means I read slowly. It helps when I get lecture slides in advance, so I can participate fully in class discussions." B. "I can't keep up with your slides. You need to slow down or give me the materials ahead of time." C. "I have a disability, so you're supposed to give me accommodations like slides or something." D. "Hi, just wondering if I could maybe get the slide sometime? If not, that's okay too." The correct answer is A. It clearly explains the barrier and offers a practical solution in a respectful, goal-oriented way. B is too accusatory. C is vague and entitled. D is polite but doesn't explain the need or here's another example. Jordan has anxiety, especially during fast-paced meetings. He wants to ask his supervisor for something that will help him prepare and participate more fully. What's the best way to say it? A. "I have anxiety, which makes it harder to speak up in fast-paced meetings. It helps when I get the agenda ahead of time, so I can prepare and contribute." B. Meetings make me anxious. I just need you to slow down or skip me." C. I don't do well in meetings, so maybe I can skip them or get someone to go for me." D. "Could I maybe get the agenda ahead of time? No worries if not!" The correct answer is A. It's clear, respectful, and action-focused. B points only to discomfort without a plan. C avoids the situation instead of addressing it. D asks nicely but lacks context or urgency. Now that you've seen two examples in action, try practicing one that fits your own situation. Say it out loud, tweak it, try again. Clear, confident self-advocacy isn't about being pushy- it's about being real. You are explaining what helps you show up and succeed.

title: Turning the Lens Towards Strengths
transcript: ﻿1 Turning the Lens Towards Strengths. Disability isn't solely about overcoming obstacles; it often brings unique strengths that can be powerful assets in various aspects of life. Recognizing and embracing these strengths is a crucial step toward effective self-advocacy. Dyslexia. People with dyslexia often excel in big-picture thinking and three-dimensional visualization. Research from the University of Cambridge suggests these abilities are linked to strengths in exploration and creativity. These abilities can be advantageous in fields like design, architecture, and engineering. ADHD. Many people with ADHD are highly creative and experience hyperfocus- deep, sustained concentration on tasks that interest them. These traits can boost performance in dynamic or idea-driven environments, especially in fields such as entrepreneurship, marketing, software development, design, and the performing arts. Autism Spectrum. Autistic individuals often show strong attention to detail and the ability to build deep expertise in subjects they care about. Studies have shown these qualities are especially valuable in technical, analytical, and artistic work, especially in fields such as data science, engineering, visual arts, archival work, cybersecurity, and game design. Physical Disabilities. Living with a physical disability often means navigating a world that isn't always built with you in mind. That daily experience builds resilience, adaptability, and creative problem-solving. Whether it's finding new ways to complete tasks, troubleshoot inaccessible systems, or advocate for your needs- those skills are real strengths. In team settings, people with physical disabilities often bring a unique perspective, logistical insight, and strong planning abilities- especially valuable in fields like project management, policy, design, and advocacy. Reflecting on your own experiences, consider the activities where you feel most confident and engaged. These areas often highlight your inherent strengths. Whether it's artistic expression, analytical thinking, or interpersonal connection, acknowledging these abilities empowers you to advocate for the supports that match how you work best. Understanding and valuing your strengths not only boosts self-confidence- it also lays the foundation for independence, fulfillment, and long-term success.

title: Why Self Knowledge is Power
transcript: ﻿1 Why Self-Knowledge is Power. When you understand both your challenges and your strengths, three great things happen. First, you can choose supports that actually work for you. Second, your confidence grows- because you know what you bring to the table. And third, you move closer to real independence, whether that's in college, at work, or in daily life.

title: From Challenges to Strengths: Becoming a Self Advocate
transcript: ﻿1 From Challenges to Strengths, Becoming a Self-Advocate. You've already started connecting the dots- how your disability shows up, what helps, and where you might need support. Now, let's flip the focus. This section is all about strengths, the unique skills, talents, and perspectives that often come with disability. We'll also dive into a powerful lifelong skill: self-advocacy. Knowing what works for you is the first step. Speaking up for it is the next.

title: How Does it Apply To You
transcript: ﻿1 How Does It Apply to You? Let's take a moment to make this real. Think of one activity - at school or at work - that feels harder because of your disability. Maybe it's keeping up with class notes, focusing during meetings, or managing last-minute changes. Now think of one tool or idea that already helps - or that you think might. It could be a planner app, noise-canceling headphones, voice memos, a text-to-speech reader, or simply asking for instructions in writing. You can write it down, record a voice note, make a quick sketch, or talk it out with someone you trust. However you do it, pause and name that connection: "Here's what's hard - here's one thing that helps." That insight is the foundation of self-advocacy. The more clearly you can name your needs and your strengths, the more power you'll have to shape an environment that works for you. And that's where we're headed next: taking what you've learned about your disability and using it to uncover what makes you strong - so you can speak up with confidence!

title: Your Legal Rights
transcript: ﻿1 Your Legal Rights. In the United States, several federal laws protect the rights of students and workers with disabilities. Understanding these laws empowers you to advocate for the support and accommodations you need. Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA). The Individuals with Disabilities Education Act or (IDEA) ensures that eligible children with disabilities receive a Free Appropriate Public Education, or FAPE, tailored to their individual needs. Under IDEA, public schools must provide an Individual Education Program, or IEP, for each qualifying student. The IEP outlines specific educational goals and the services the school will provide to help the student achieve them. This law applies to children from birth through age 21. Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act of 1973. Section 504 is a civil rights law that prohibits discrimination against individuals with disabilities in programs receiving federal funding, including public schools and colleges. Students who do not qualify for an IEP under IDEA may still receive accommodations through a 504 plan. These accommodations can include extended time on tests, preferential seating, and the use of assistive technology, ensuring that students have equal access to education. Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA). The Americans with Disabilities Act or (ADA) is a comprehensive civil rights law that prohibits discrimination based on disability in various areas, including employment, public accommodations, transportation, and education. In the workplace, The ADA requires employers with 15 or more employees to provide reasonable accommodations to qualified individuals with disabilities, unless doing so would cause undue hardship. These accommodations might include flexible work schedules, modified equipment, or accessible workspaces. Understanding these laws helps you recognize that requesting support isn't asking for special treatment - it's exercising your legal rights. When you're informed about these protections, you can confidently advocate for the accommodations and services you need to succeed in school, work, and life.

title: How It Might Show Up at Work
transcript: ﻿1 How Your Disability Might Show Up at Work. Now picture a job site. Andre uses a wheelchair and just started an office job. On day one, he realized the conference room door was too narrow and the break room counters were out of reach. After he spoke up, his manager quickly arranged for adjustments - wider door access, a sit-stand desk, and a more accessible workspace setup. Now Andre can navigate his day without extra stress! Nia has ADHD and works in a marketing firm. Keeping track of shifting deadlines and last-minute requests used to feel like chaos! After talking with her supervisor, she got approval to use a digital task board and a flexible schedule that matches her high-focus hours. Since then, her projects have been on point - and her stress has gone way down. Luis is Deaf and recently joined a team that relies on big weekly meetings. At first, it was hard to follow the rapid-fire discussion. But after requesting live captions and a remote interpreter, Lewis now fully participates - and even leads updates during team check-ins. Tara has social anxiety and sensory sensitivities. Loud, open office spaces and constant interruptions made it hard to focus. With a quieter cubicle, written instructions instead of sudden verbal asks, and permission to use noise-canceling headphones, she now gets more done and feels way more at ease. And then there's Imani who lives with a chronic illness that causes fatigue and joint pain. Sitting or standing for long periods made her job difficult. After disclosing her condition, she worked with Human Resources to adjust her schedule, include rest breaks, and switch to hybrid work - changes that made it possible to stay productive without pushing her body past its limits. Most employers are not only willing but legally required to provide reasonable accommodations once they understand what you need - so speaking up isn't just helpful, it's essential!

title: How It Might Show Up at School
transcript: ﻿1 How Your Disability Might Show Up at School. Let's set up a scenario. Jacob has Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder or ADHD, which makes it hard for him to focus during long lectures - especially when there's a lot of visual information on the screen. By the 10-minute mark, his mind is already somewhere else. To stay on track, Jacob uses a planner to break assignments into smaller chunks and sets phone reminders to keep himself moving. It's not about working harder - it's about working with his brain, not against it. Now, imagine Maya who has dyslexia, which affects her reading and writing abilities. Heavy reading used to make her feel overwhelmed and frustrated. But when she started using text- to-speech software, things changed. Now she listens to assignments while walking or drawing, and for the first time, she actually enjoys what she's learning. For Aiden, who is autistic, group projects used to be the hardest part of school. Sudden schedule changes or unclear instructions would throw him off. But after asking teachers for written step-by-step instructions and advance notice of any changes, group work became more manageable - and sometimes even fun. And then there's Leila, who has anxiety. Presenting in front of the class made her so nervous she'd lose her train of thought or freeze up. Once she started requesting alternative formats - like pre-recorded videos or one-on-one presentations with her teacher - her confidence and grades both improved.

title: Different Types
transcript: ﻿1 Imagine our personalities are like puzzle pieces that fit into specific jobs and activity types. The Holland Codes framework helps us solve our puzzles by categorizing our different personality types and explaining which careers or occupational themes they match up to. This unique system was developed by American psychologist, Dr. John Holland, who conceived of six personality groups based on specific interests and preferences. These groups are labeled as follows: Realistic, also known as builders or doers. Investigative, also known as problem solvers or thinkers. Artistic, also known as creators. Social, also known as people persons or helpers. Enterprising, also known as persuaders. And Conventional, also known as organizers. Most of us fall into more than one of these categories, which is normal. The Holland system wasn't designed to pigeonhole us or limit our options, but understanding your Holland Code types will help you identify careers that align with your interests and capabilities. So think of it as a thought provoking starting point for your career exploration journey. Now, let's review each type a bit more.

title: What is a Disability?
transcript: ﻿1 What Is My Disability and How Does It Affect Me? In this lesson, we'll explore what a disability really is and how it can shape your everyday life and school and on the job. We'll also discover why understanding these differences - your challenges and your talents - is the very first step toward independence.

title: What is my Disability & How Does it Affect Me?
transcript: ﻿1 What Is My Disability and How Does It Affect Me? In this lesson, we'll explore what a disability really is and how it can shape your everyday life and school and on the job. We'll also discover why understanding these differences - your challenges and your talents - is the very first step toward independence.

title: Question 5
transcript: Wow. That sounds like a really cool book. I can totally imagine myself getting really into reading that. We're nearing the end of this interview. I've been having a really great time getting to know you. Do you have any questions for me?

title: Question 4
transcript: Impressive. Thank you for sharing that story. I have one more question. I wanna get to know you and what drives you a little bit more. Here at Geolad, our internship program is really about investing in people's talents and turning their passions into the beginning of their careers. Imagine that you can write a book about any topic you're passionate about. What would that book be about and why?

title: Question 3
transcript: Great. Good to know. Next question. We have a really great work culture here at Geolad, but sometimes it can be challenging working with local businesses. For example, some business owners are tough to work with because they just don't get how important social media marketing is for today's audiences, so we try to break it down for them. Can you describe a time where you faced a significant challenge at work or in school? How did you handle that and what was the outcome?

title: Question 2
transcript: Awesome! Thanks for sharing that. I'm looking forward to getting to know you more today. Let's start with our questions. As an intern here, you will be spending a lot of time learning. Can you describe a project or task where you had to learn something completely new? What was that process like for you?

title: Question 1
transcript: Great. Good to know. Next question. We have a really great work culture here at Geolad, but sometimes it can be challenging working with local businesses. For example, some business owners are tough to work with because they just don't get how important social media marketing is for today's audiences, so we try to break it down for them. Can you describe a time where you faced a significant challenge at work or in school? How did you handle that and what was the outcome?

title: Mock Interview Activities Instructions
transcript: Interview Activity Instructions Now that you've learned about job interview best practices, it's time to put those skills to the test. For this scenario-based activity, you'll be role-playing as a candidate, interviewing for an internship at Geolad, the fictional social media agency from our previous lessons. Before you start, you'll have a chance to review the internship description. Then Katelyn, Geolad's Internship program director, will do a prerecorded mock interview with you through a series of videos to simulate the different phases of a real interview. In each video, she'll ask you a question you could expect in an actual interview. After each video, there'll be a popup screen where you can enter your response. Take your time to think about your answer. Remember the tips you learned in this chapter, such as the STAR Method, your self presentation, and asking thoughtful questions. If needed, feel free to refer back to the internship description or previous videos in this chapter. After you type your response, be sure to review it and make any edits. When you're confident with your answer, submit it. Last note. Even though this is a mock interview, it's an important opportunity to practice your skills, so do your research, be confident and do your best. Here's the internship description for you to read through before starting.

title: After the Interview
transcript: After the Interview Send a thank you message. Within 24 hours, send a short email expressing your gratitude for the opportunity to do the interview. If your personal email account name is too casual, make a new account for use for job applications and interview follow ups. Be sure to include your name in the body of the email as well. Restate your interest in the position and touch on any specific topics discussed to personalize the message. Follow up on questions or assignments, if needed. If you were asked any questions you couldn't address during the interview, follow up by sending that information promptly. By the same token, if they gave you any post-interview tasks, complete them to the best of your ability and send those right away too. When to follow up Waiting for the company's hiring decision may cause anxiety. Remember, you've done your best and the rest is in their hands. Most of the time ater the interview, the interviewer or recruiter will give you an idea of when you can expect to hear back. It's hard to say for sure when you'll hear back, but you should wait five business days before following up. So if you were interviewed on a Tuesday, wait until the next Tuesday before following up. Make sure you stay respectful. Thank them for their time, and express how excited you are about the opportunity. Don't just ask them directly when you'll hear back. Be prepared for more. While a lot of jobs may only require one interview, some may have multiple rounds. Companies and teams do this to get to know a person as best as they can. You might meet with the hiring manager, someone from human resources, your future coworkers and team members, your future boss, or even the leaders of the company depending on the job. The person who reached out to you will usually let you know if the interview will go through a few rounds. If this is one of many interviews, start from the top of this list and do your best to prepare. Now, are you ready to test your interviewing skills? In the final activity of this chapter, we'll be using everything you learned for a mock interview.

title: During the Interview
transcript: During the Interview Make a strong first impression. Use positive body language such as a firm handshake, direct eye contact, and a warm smile as you introduce yourself. Remember the pre-interview preparation you did. Some applicants get so nervous, they draw a blank when the questions begin. If you prepared ahead, think back to the preparation you did. Stay relaxed, friendly, and flexible. When appropriate, engage in small talk to show your enthusiasm and interpersonal skills. Otherwise, keep your answers brief, honest, and focused on specific achievements and experiences that could relate to the position. Remember the STAR method Exude confidence and be personal. Employers don't expect you to know everything, especially when you're early in your career. They want workers who are confident in their abilities, but are also trainable and open to learning new things. Make sure you phrase things in a way that makes you sound confident, but still humble. Remember, they're looking for someone who has the skills for the job, but they're also looking for someone with the right personality. Take notes. You should always keep your attention on the person asking you questions, but if you need to, you can write down notes so you don't forget details that may come up during the interview. This shows that you're engaged and also helps you remember points to reference for any follow ups. Ask thoughtful questions During your interview preparation, hopefully you thought of some good questions to ask at the end of the interview. This demonstrates that you were proactive and did some research ahead of time, but if new questions occurred to you during the course of the interview, feel free to ask those. It shows you paid attention and gave some thought to the conversation.

title: Going to the Interview
transcript: Going to the Interview Dress appropriately. Choose a professional outfit that matches the company's dress code and makes you feel confident and comfortable. Always do your research on the company's dress code, and when in doubt, wear a suit or other dress apparel. Bring what you need. Take copies of your resume, a list of your professional references or letters of reference, a pen and a notepad. If they ask you to bring any other materials, make sure you prepare those the night before too. Be on time. Give yourself plenty of time to get to the interview site. Take into consideration things such as traffic, detours, parking, entryway check-ins, or weather conditions. Plan to arrive early, so you can freshen up and won't feel stressed out. Be hygienic. Remember, this is your first impression on the company interviewing you, so it's best to look as presentable as possible. Consider your personal hygiene and how you want to present yourself. Be prepared for some last minute touch ups too. Think about bringing a comb, some gum or breath mints, and something to dry off your sweat, just in case. Stay calm. Get a good night's rest the night before. Try to avoid caffeine on the day of the interview. You want to be alert, but not jittery. It's okay to fill on edge, but this is when the soft skill stress management comes in handy. Find healthy ways to be calm and collected during the interview. Stay positive and don't dwell on whether you'll get the job or not. Treat every interview as a learning experience and a great way to practice your people skills.

title: Nailing Your Job Interview
transcript: Before the Interview Analyze the job description When you see an ad for a job, you want copy and paste the job description into a document. Study every word until you completely understand all the duties and requirements. Remember what we learned in chapter four about keywords. You'll wanna use as many of those as possible because the interviewer is likely listening for those. This will also demonstrate that you did your homework on having a full understanding of the job description. Keep these things in mind during interviews so you can speak about how you meet their needs. Research the organization and interviewers. Don't just look at the job ad. Look at the organization's website too. Get familiar with who they are, what they do, and what their values and mission are. Before the interview, set some time aside to read up on the organization. What is their mission? What have been some milestones in the company? What is the latest news or project they shared? These are some questions to ask yourself while browsing through their website. Make sure to take notes. If you can, check out the organization's social media and the LinkedIn profiles of whoever will be interviewing you too. Practice your elevator pitch. An elevator pitch is a speech that's short enough to make during an elevator ride. Imagine you jump in an elevator with someone you want to impress. You only have a few seconds to sell an idea to them. It needs to be concise and compelling. It's best to always be ready. If an interviewer asks about your background, have your pitch down. Practice it out loud, and focus on experiences directly relevant to the job and why you'd be perfect for it. Review standard interview questions and do mock interviews. It's easy to search online and find common interview questions. You should anticipate questions like, why should we hire you? As well as more detailed job specific questions. There may be questions such as, can you tell us about a time you were faced with a challenge at work and how you overcame it. For these, plan your response using the STAR method. Explain the situation you were in, the task you needed to do, the action you took and the results. You can also practice your responses via mock interviews with a friend, family member, or someone at your school's career center. At the end of this chapter, we will have a mock interview for you too. Prepare questions to ask them. At the end of the interview, interviewers often ask if you have any questions for them. Just remember, that's still part of the interview. Make a very short list of questions you can ask them to demonstrate that you understand the role and are curious to learn more about the organization. Just make sure to ask questions that haven't already been answered during the interview. Some examples of what you can ask are: Can you describe the working culture of the company? Do you provide professional development opportunities? If so, what do those look like or What are some of the challenges I might face in this position? Remember, the only wrong response when the recruiter asks if you have any questions is to say no. Why? Because studies show that nearly half of all interviewers say they won't hire a candidate who shows little knowledge of the employing organization. Know where To Go Research the route to get to the interview location. We recommend you arrive as early as you can, but always pay attention to details given by the recruiter. Before the day of the interview, check how long it takes based on the time of day you'll be traveling. You don't want being late to be your first impression on them. Be ready for remote interviews. These days many interviews are done virtually over the internet, so ensure you'll have the right software downloaded and updated. Some popular methods have been through Zoom or Google Meets. Also, check that your video and audio devices are working properly. Pick a quiet location with good lighting and a professional uncluttered background. If you don't have access to such a space, consider using a virtual background picture you can find on the internet or on the video calling service. Be sure there won't be any interruptions or background noise. Lastly, dress the same as if you were going to an in-person interview.

title: Writing Bullet Points
transcript: ﻿1 Writing bullet points. Now let's discuss the second task of your resume - to convince the hiring manager that you had the required skills and experience needed for the job. A human reader is going to spend a little more time reviewing your resume than a software program, but employers get a ton of applications so they skim through them pretty fast. And like the ATS programs, they're looking for specific indicators that tell them if you're a good candidate or not. If they don't spot those indicators quickly, they'll throw your resume into the "do not contact" pile. That's why it's vital to make your resume easy to read by using "bullet points." Bullet points are brief, clear, and impactful statements that highlight key information in a list format. This makes the text easier for ATS programs to scan and for humans to read! Here's how to write strong bullet points: Start with action verbs. Begin each bullet point with a powerful action verb explaining what you did. Examples includes words like: achieved, created, designed, developed, executed, led, managed, and optimized. An example of a bullet point with a strong action verb is: "Managed a team of five developers to complete software projects on time and within budget." Use quantifiable results. When possible, include numbers, statistics, or other quantifiable results to show the impact of your work. This makes your achievements measurable and more impressive. Example: "Increased website traffic by 30% through SEO strategies over a six month period." Write about tasks but focus on achievements. You'll want to list your job duties but focus on what you accomplished by doing them. Show how you added value or made a difference. Example: "Redesigned the customer service training program, resulting in a 15% increase in satisfaction ratings." Tailor your bullet points for the job. Remember to use keywords and phrases relevant to the specific position you're applying. Example: You see a job post that lists cybersecurity as a required skill. A good bullet point example might be: "Implemented strong cybersecurity protocols to protect data and prevent unauthorized access to networks." Keep the reader in mind. You don't always know who will be reviewing your resume. Use caution when making assumptions about what they will understand. Try to avoid using unnecessarily technical jargon unless you're sure the reader will know what you mean. As for acronyms, it's a good idea to include them but also spell them out unless they're common or the job posting also uses those acronyms. For example, if the job requires knowledge of Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation, they'll probably just write "CPR" - so you can, too! Keep it concise. Stick to short, clear statements. Hiring managers may get a stack of a hundred or more resumes to review. They won't review every word, so keep it short and to the point. Ideally, each bullet point should be one sentence around one to two lines. In fact, your whole resume should ideally be one page or two at the most. If it's longer than that, you'll need to do some editing. Note, Curriculum Vitae, or CVs, are a bit different. These are like resumes but much more in-depth. They're only used for certain professions, to provide extra information such as details about the applicant's educational history, published works, research experience, fellowships completed, grants received, honors, professional organization memberships, and so on.

title: Introduction
transcript: ﻿1 Introduction to Self-Awareness. As students, you're doing your best to get through school so you can be prepared for your future. But what do you want to do in your future" And who do you want to be? Sometimes that's hard to figure out.. especially when we don't fully understand who we are now. So before you start exploring career paths and the required education and training for them, in this chapter, let's explore YOU! This chapter is all about you getting to know yourself better. Discovering what you truly want in life begins with exploring who you are, what you believe, and what matters most to you. Self-awareness is a lifelong journey, and each step helps you understand yourself a little better. To do that, you'll have to deep dive and explore your core values, motivations, and priorities. And also your interests, skills, and aptitude. Being self-aware also involves knowing your goals in life, your own definition of success, and how you learn from experiences. All these unique variables weave together to make you the fascinating person you currently are. And like the rest of us, "who you are" changes and evolves as you get older. Getting to know yourself better will help you align your future career choices with who you truly are. The more you understand yourself, the more confidently you can explore the world of career possibilities. But remember, exploring your own self-awareness isn't a one-time process; it's a lifelong journey.

title: How to Get Past the Automated Tracking Systems!
transcript: ﻿1 Automated Tracking System. The only way your resume can get you an interview is if a person sees it, right? But many companies use programs called Automated Tracking Systems or ATS, that screen and filter the resumes that come in. The larger the employer, the greater the odds they use ATS. You'll never know if they use ATS or not, so just assume they do. And if your resume doesn't get past the ATS filter, no one will see it. As we said before, your resume only has one job - to get you interviews. So let's break that one job down into two separate tasks. The first task, get past the ATS! Here's how ATS basically works. It starts with the organization's job post! When an organization wants to hire someone, they post about the opening on sites like Indeed, ZipRecruiter and other job portals or on their own websites. Job posts contain elements like the job title, a job summary, describing the position and its main duties and responsibilities, minimum qualifications to apply such as education, skills and experience needed, preferred qualifications, which are extra skills or experience they'd like the applicants to have, and information about the company culture, which is shared so job seekers can decide how well they might fit into the work environment. Job posts contain other information, too, but let's focus on these elements because they deal directly with the ATS screening process. Each of these elements will have specific keywords and phrases sprinkled throughout the text. The job posts won't tell you exactly what those keywords and phrases are, but it's easy to spot them.

title: Industry Trends
transcript: ﻿1 Industry Trends. The world of business evolves constantly with changes coming even faster, thanks to globalization and the rise of E-commerce. Companies must keep up with the latest trends to stay competitive. For example, Web3, the next generation of the internet is built on blockchain technology and promises better security, increase transparency, and more decentralization. Business managers must learn to use the capabilities that Web3 offers, such as smart contracts, decentralized finance models, and decentralized autonomous organizations. Another trend involves data analytics. The process of collecting, cleaning, and analyzing data to extract insights used for decision-making. Businesses that maximize this powerful tool can gain significant advantages over the competition. Curious about which pathways might match your personality and interest in this sector? title: Different Pathways in the Sector
transcript: ﻿1 Different Pathways in the Sector. The five main career pathways for this sector are General Management, Business Information Management, Human Resources Management, Operations Management, and Administrative Support. What is the General Management Pathway? Workers in this pathway are responsible for planning, organizing, directing, and evaluating business processes. They also oversee the proper use of financial, human, and material resources. Careers in this pathway include Entrepreneur, Finance Director, Investment Executive, Public Relations Manager. What is the Business Information Management Pathway? Business information management professionals ensure accurate and timely information is available to support business decisions. These jobs require knowledge of both business processes and IT systems. Careers include Auditor, Business Consultant, Certified Public Accountant, E-commerce Analyst, Marketing Analyst. What is the Human Resources Management Pathway? Businesses rely on human resources for staffing activities such as recruitment, hiring, training, compensation, and other employee-related matters. HR professionals play a vital role in creating positive work environments, leading to better morale, productivity, and customer satisfaction rates. Careers include Corporate Trainer, Employer Relations Representative, Equal Employment Opportunity Specialist, Human Resources Manager, Payroll Professional, Personnel Recruiter. What is the Operations Management Pathway? Operations teams meticulously plan, organize, coordinate, and control the resources used in the production of a business's goods and services. They may also be involved in sales and marketing. Careers include Advertising Salesperson, Facilities Manager, Sales Representative, Wholesale and Retail Buyer. What is the Administrative Support Pathway? Administrative professionals perform a variety of office tasks to support employees, managers, and executives. They are the essential behind-the-scenes team that enables businesses to operate efficiently. Careers include Customer Service Assistant, Data Entry Specialist, Executive Assistant, Office Manager, Receptionist, Shipping and Receiving Clerk.

title: Top Skills Needed
transcript: ﻿1 Top Skills Needed. The Business Management and Administration sector covers a lot of different career types. There are many different skills required depending on the job. Let's review a few of the general skills needed. Analytical. Business professionals have to acquire and analyze data to identify trends and extrapolate information they can use. Attention to detail. This is crucial because businesses have to comply with many policies and must avoid making small errors that could have big consequences. Communication. It's vital to be able to discuss business and financial matters calmly and clearly, especially when the topics are complex and there are high risks involved. Decision-Making. Business managers make decisions all the time, often under pressure. They weigh the pros and cons of their options and try to make the best choices for the company or client. Leadership. In this line of work, it's often necessary to lead teams to complete projects. Leaders have to motivate and inspire others, set clear goals, and delegate tasks. Problem-Solving. Workers must objectively identify problems methodically, look for underlying root causes, and come up with innovative solutions. Teamwork. Being able to collaborate effectively is a key aspect of almost every job in this sector. Technical. Several jobs in this sector require technical skills such as proficiency in using databases or analytical tools.

title: Who enjoys working in this sector?
transcript: ﻿1 Types of People Who Enjoy Working in this Sector. Business managers often desire to drive innovation while generating revenue. They're outgoing and persuasive communicators who want to create products and services that make life better for consumers. Many are visionaries with strong ethics, who take pride in contributing to a more sustainable future. Administrative workers are usually customer service-oriented, with a high degree of attention to detail. Though they may work "behind the scenes", they know their roles are essential for the smooth operations of their organizations.

transcript: ﻿1 Why is it important? Business is a cornerstone of modern society because, like it or not, societies run on money. The entire global economy is built on businesses offering citizens jobs where we can create products and offer valuable services to each other. Business drives innovation such as technological advancements, and improves our quality of life. It creates brands that cater to our cultural identities and lifestyles and gives our country competitive advantages in the global market. But it takes dedicated leaders, managers, and administrative teams to help businesses succeed and grow.

transcript: ﻿1 Different Pathways in the Sector. The six main career pathways in this sector are A/V Technology and Film, Printing Technology, Visual Arts, Performing Arts, Journalism and Broadcasting, and Telecommunications. What is the A/V Technology and Film Career Pathway? AV is a broad field that encompasses designing, making, selling, and operating audiovisual equipment. Work environments range from theme parks and stadiums to convention centers, classrooms, and more. Careers in this pathway include Audio Systems Technician, Audio-Video Designer and Engineer, Audio-Video System Service Technician, Technical Computer Support Technician: Film, Video, and DVD, Videographer, Special Effects and Animation, and Video Systems Technician. What is the Printing Technology Career Pathway? Printing presses are still used for books, magazines, and papers, but the industry increasingly relies on high-tech digital tools for imaging, layouts, and proofing. Careers include Computer Typography and Composition Operator, Desktop Publishing Specialist, Graphics Equipment Operator, Lithographer, Paper Sales Salesperson, and Plate Maker. What is the Visual Arts Career Pathway? Visual artists communicate ideas, thoughts, and feelings through painting, sculpting, illustrating, and other methods. Their works may be realistic, stylized, or abstract. Careers include Art Director, Artist, Commercial Artist, Fashion Designer, Fashion Illustrator, and Graphic Designer. What is the Performing Arts Career Pathway? Many businesses and groups are involved in the performing arts, such as production companies, agents, costume designers, lighting and stage crews, dance studios, schools, bands, and more. Careers include Actor, Composer, Costume Designer, Makeup Artist, Musician, and Scenic Designer. What is the Journalism and Broadcasting Career Pathway? News analysts and reporters gather information and share stories to tell us about events and current issues. Broadcast and sound technicians set up and operate the recording equipment. While engineers and field supervisors ensure things run smoothly. Careers include Journalist, Light Director, Producer, Publisher, Radio and Television Announcer, and Reporter. What is the Telecommunications Career Pathway? The jobs of telecommunications workers focus on the interaction between users, computers, and electronic communications equipment that processes data, graphics, audio and video. Careers include Customer Service Representative, Network Designer, Sales Representative, Systems Designer, Telecommunication Computer Programmer and Systems Analyst, and Telecommunication Technician.

title: Industry Trends

title: Industry Trends
transcript: ﻿1 Industry Trends. The occupations in this sector are affected by ever-evolving industry trends, changing climate conditions, and updated policies and priorities. For instance, agricultural workers are always developing ways to make crops more resilient, to withstand changing temperatures and rainfall amounts. Workers also look for ways to reduce pollution and mitigate the effects of deforestation to protect resources for future generations. Trends in food production are focused on sustainability, automation, and personalized nutrition to meet consumer demands. Natural resources trends are also shifting towards sustainable practices, technological innovation, and circular economy models to meet growing global demands. Supply chain problems have disrupted the process of getting food from farms to tables impacting availability and prices. Meanwhile, technology like drones and sensors are helping to make processes more efficient. These are just a few of the trends happening in these dynamic fields. The Agricultural, Food, and Natural Resources sector is crucial to our society. Without it, we wouldn't have most of the foods we eat, fibers for the clothes we wear, or renewable energy sources we use every day. title: Different Pathways in the Sector
transcript: ﻿1 Different Pathways in the Sector. Here are the primary pathways to enter the Agricultural and Natural Resources sector. Agricultural Business, Agricultural Mechanics, Agriscience, Animal Science, Forestry and Natural Resources, Ornamental Horticulture, and Plant and Soil Science. What is Agricultural Business? Agricultural business involves growing and selling crops and other agricultural products. It includes everything from farming and ranching to food processing and distribution. Careers in this pathway include Agricultural Credit Manager, Agriculture Inspector, Business Controller, Farm and Ranch Manager, Sales Representative. What is Agricultural Mechanics? Agricultural mechanics is the study of machines and equipment used in agriculture. This covers tasks such as driving tractors and combine harvesters to working with irrigation systems or inside greenhouses. Careers in this pathway include Agricultural Engineer, Agriculture Equipment Operator, Equipment Fabricator, Farm Equipment Mechanic and Service Technician, Welder. What is Agriscience? Agriscience is the study of agriculture using scientific principles, from plant and animal science to soil science and water management. Here are some careers that exist in this pathway. Agriscience Teacher, Entomologist, Plant Scientist, Research Assistant or Associate, Research Scientist, Water Quality Specialist. What is Animal Science? Animal science is the study of animals and how they're raised and used by humans. It includes everything from animal nutrition and breeding to animal health and welfare. Careers in this pathway are Animal Breeder, Animal Caretaker or Kennel Operator, Feed Nutritionist, Ranch Manager, Veterinarian Technician. What is Forestry and Natural Resources? The career pathway of forestry and natural resources refers to managing, protecting, and conserving forests and resources such as water, soil, and even wildlife. Careers in this pathway include Biological Science Technician, Fish Hatchery Technician, Forestry Technician, Logging Operation Inspector, Park Ranger. What is Ornamental Horticulture? Ornamental horticulture is the science and art of growing and using plants for their beauty. It includes everything from growing flowers to decorative shrubs to trees. Careers that exist in this pathway are Botanical Specialist, Florist or Floral Designer, Hydroponics Grower, Landscape Design or Architect, Nursery or Greenhouse Manager. What is Plant and Soil Science? As you might have guessed, plant and soil science is the study of plants and the soil they grow in. It's a broad field that covers topics like plant growth, soil formation, and nutrient cycling. Careers that exist in this pathway are Crop Consultant, Environmental Analyst, Pest Control Advisor, Plant and Soil Scientist, Soil Conservationist.

title: Top Skills Needed
transcript: ﻿1 Top Skills Needed. Agriculture, Food, and Natural Resources jobs require a diverse set of skills and attributes. Including problem-solving, critical thinking, communication, teamwork, leadership, and a variety of technical skills. Adaptability. To work in this business, you've got to be ready to adapt to ever-changing situations in markets, climate conditions, and policies. Business and Financial Acumen. Some workers must plan and manage budgets, analyze costs, establish prices, and study market trends. Communication and Interpersonal Skills. All workers in this sector must be able to work effectively with others and explain their thoughts and ideas clearly to get things done right. Continuous Learning. To solve problems, workers have to stay updated on trends, new technologies and discoveries in the field. Environmental Stewardship. This means using practices that are good for the soil, utilize water wisely, promote biodiversity, and help maintain the natural balance of things. Problem-Solving. Workers must apply problem-solving techniques to identify issues and generate smart, effective solutions that are good for their companies as well as consumers, crops, animals, and the environment. Technical and Operational Skills. Many careers in the Agriculture, Food, and Natural Resources sector require knowledge of highly specialized equipment or programs.

title: Why is it important?
transcript: ﻿1 Why is it important? The Agriculture, Food, and Natural Resources sector is vital to our survival. It ensures our society has plenty of food to eat and sufficient materials and energy to use. Let's take a look at each industry within this broad cluster of career pathways. Agriculture. Agriculture is the practice of cultivating crops like fruits, vegetables, and grains, and raising livestock such as chickens, sheep, cattle, pigs, goats, fish, and more. These animals provide us with resources like milk, eggs, and meat, plus non-food items like wool, leather, and byproducts for medicines, cosmetics, and other things. Food. The food industry is crucial for ensuring the production, distribution, and accessibility of safe and nutritious food to sustain global populations. Safe food production is vital for protecting public health by preventing contamination, ensuring nutritional quality, and safeguarding the supply chain. Natural Resources. Humans use or tap into natural resources such as crops we harvest for food and oil we use for fuel, or wind, rivers and tides, which can generate electricity. Other examples of natural resources include air, sunlight, soil, coal, natural gas, metals, and stones. Some resources like wind are renewable. Others such as fossil fuels are non-renewable or finite.
