title: Industry Trends
transcript: ﻿1 Industry Trends. The health science sector is undergoing rapid changes such as the introduction of artificial intelligence to automate tasks and improve diagnoses and decision-making. Personalized treatment plans are also on the rise. These offer tailored therapies based on a patient's unique genetic makeup and lifestyle factors. Telemedicine has become increasingly popular, allowing patients to receive medical consultations remotely so they don't have to travel to a provider's office. Lastly, patients are being more proactive in their own healthcare by using wearable devices that monitor their vital statistics in real time. Curious about which pathways might match your personality and interest in this sector? Let's take the Sector Quiz!

title: Different Pathways in the Sector
transcript: ﻿1 Different Pathways in the Sector. The five career pathways into the health science sector are Therapeutic Services, Diagnostic Services, Health Informatics, Support Services, and Biotechnology Research and Development. What are Therapeutic Services? Therapeutic service professionals provide patients with direct healthcare treatment, counseling, or education. Careers in this pathway include Anesthesiologist Assistant, Certified Nursing Assistant, Clinical Medical Assistant, Dentist, Dietician, Physician (MD or DO). What are Diagnostic Services? Diagnostic service workers use equipment such as X-rays, sonography machines, and MRI scanners to help detect, diagnose, and treat diseases, injuries, or other health conditions. Careers in this pathway include Cardiovascular Technologist, Central Supply Technician, Clinical Lab Technician, Nutritionist, Pathologist, Radiologist. What is Health Informatics? Health informatics is the use of technology to gather and analyze data, then use the findings to improve healthcare processes and patient outcomes. Careers include Epidemiologist, Ethicist, Health Educator, Health information Coder, Medical Biller, Transcriptionist. What are Support Services? Support service staff offer various types of support ranging from cooking and building maintenance to helping busy nurses and doctors. Careers include Biomedical or Clinical Technician, Central Services Manager, Dietary Aide, Facilities Manager, Food Service Administrator, Industrial Hygienist. What is Biotechnology Research and Development? Bioscience research scientists study how organisms behave and interact in their environments. Then use findings to develop better treatments and equipment. Careers include Lab Assistant Genetics, Lab Technician, Maintenance and Instrument Technician, Microbiologist, Molecular Biologist, Pharmaceutical Scientist.

title: Top Skills Needed
transcript: ﻿1 Top Skills Needed. Common skills for this sector include: Attention to detail. When patient's health is at stake, it's vital to pay attention to details. Mistakes can carry serious consequences. Ability to work under pressure. Many roles require the ability to stay calm under pressure. There are times when quick decisions have to be made under stressful circumstances. Communication Skills. You'll need to communicate effectively with patients, family members, and fellow healthcare professionals about complicated topics. Empathy. Medical patients and their families are often concerned or even frightened. Empathy and compassion are key traits for anyone working with the sick or injured. Patience. When someone is ill, they and their loved ones may feel anxious and have shorter tempers than usual, patience is crucial in these cases. Problem-Solving Skills. Health and medical problems can be complex. Workers need to think critically so they can address challenging problems. Resilience. It can be tough to work around patients every day. Employees must be able to cope with difficult situations and not let the pressure get to them. Science and Math Skills. Several roles require the application of Science and Math concepts. Knowledge of human biology is typically needed in understanding chemistry and physics may be necessary too.

title: Who enjoys working in this sector?
transcript: ﻿1 Types of People Who Enjoy Working in this Sector. There are many career types in this sector, so it's hard to summarize the skills needed, but health science workers share a desire to contribute to the health and well-being of others. Their work truly makes a difference. Employees in these fields are detail-oriented and interested in learning new things because technology is constantly advancing. Some jobs require direct patient care and healthcare settings while others focus on equipment, devices, software, research, or administrative support.

title: Why is it important?
transcript: ﻿1 Why is it important? The health science sector allows us to understand the human body and find ways to prevent, diagnose, and treat diseases, illnesses, and other health-related issues. From developing life-saving medications to advancing imaging technologies that help doctors see inside the body, the incredible work done in these fields helps people live longer, better lives. This sector also plays a vital role in the nation's economy, providing tens of thousands of great-paying jobs with exciting advancement opportunities.

title: Overview
transcript: ﻿1 Welcome. We will be learning about the National CTE Sector, Health Science. This broad sector deals with the science of human health, which includes developing and applying methods and technologies to improve healthcare and quality of life for patients. Employees in this sector work in fields related to medicine, biology, chemistry, engineering, and more. As these fields evolve, workers can expect to be at the forefront of astounding breakthroughs! The five career pathways into the health science sector are Therapeutic Services, Diagnostic Services, Health Informatics, Support Services, and Biotechnology Research and Development. Common job titles include Biochemist, Dietician, Phlebotomist, Physician, Radiologist, and Veterinarian, to name a few. As Dr. William Mayo once said, "The aim of medicine is to prevent disease and prolong life. The ideal of medicine is to eliminate the need of a physician."

title: Industry Trends
transcript: ﻿1 Industry Trends. Many aspects of the government and public administration sector are driven by political priorities and policy changes. Officials may implement policies to better align with voter demands or to address trending topics. There's a growing movement to offer more "citizen-centric" services based on what the public prioritizes for its communities. In addition, there's a push for better collaboration between public service organizations, nonprofits, private companies, and academic institutions to address current challenges together. Curious about which pathways might match your personality and interests? Then let's take the Gladeo Quiz!

title: Different Pathways in the Sector
transcript: ﻿1 Different Pathways in the Sector. The seven main career pathways are Governance, National Security, Foreign Service, Planning, Revenue and Taxation, Regulation, and Public Management and Administration. Let's explore each. What is the Governance Pathway? Officials and government positions are responsible for making and executing public policies. They also help ensure organizations operate in a responsible, ethical manner that is as transparent and efficient as possible. Careers in this pathway include Congressional Aide, Governor, Mayor, Policy Advisor, President, and Senator, Federal or State. What is the National Security Pathway? National security workers engage in military activities and operations designed to protect American citizens and interests against threats. Careers include Agent/Specialist, Airborne Warning/ Control Specialist, Combat Aircraft Pilot/Crew, Combat Engineer, Cryptographer, and Intelligence Analyst. What is Foreign Service Pathway? Foreign service professionals are dedicated to serving U.S. interests in other countries. They are assigned to work at embassies and consulates to promote good foreign relations, while also helping to safeguard Americans living abroad. Careers include Ambassador, Consular Officer, Development Assistance Officer, Diplomatic Courier, Economic Officer, and Foreign Service Officer. What is the Planning Pathway? Planners develop long-and-short term land use plans to provide for growth and revitalization of urban, suburban, and rural communities. Local officials make decisions about how to use land and resources in ways that benefit the most people. Careers include Business/Enterprise Official, Census Clerk, Census Enumerator, Chief of Vital Statistics, Commissioner, and County Director. What is the Revenue and Taxation Pathway? Workers in this pathway write and implement tax systems to ensure citizens and organizations pay their required tax obligations. That money is then used to fund government services and help maintain America's economic stability. Careers include Assessor, Inspector General, Internal Revenue Investigator, Revenue Agent Officer, Tax Attorney, and Tax Auditor. What is the Regulation Pathway? Regulations are rules or directives set by authorities to govern conduct. Regulation professionals create these rules for organizations to follow, thus helping to ensure the protection of workers, consumers, communities, wildlife, and the environment. Careers include Aviation Safety Officer, Bank Examiner, Border Inspector, Business Regulation Investigator, Cargo Inspector, and Chief Bank Examiner. What is the Public Management and Administration Pathway? Public management professionals are in charge of our shared public resources, ensuring they are used effectively, efficiently, and ethically. Careers include City Council Member, City Manager, City or County Clerk, County Administrator or Clerk, Executive Director/ Officer/Associate, and General Service Officer.

title: Top Skills Needed
transcript: ﻿1 Top Skills Needed. There are so many different career pathways in this sector that job skills can vary greatly. However, all workers need to possess a few common skills, including: Communication. Public sector jobs require the ability to communicate effectively, especially when people's welfare and livelihood are at stake. Critical Thinking. Many jobs in this sector have direct and indirect impacts on people's lives, so it's vital to think critically before taking action. Decision-Making. Government service professionals must be able to analyze information quickly and weigh the benefits and risks of different options. Leadership. Public sector teams require strong, motivational leadership at all levels to get their missions accomplished. Problem-Solving. Workers must apply their training and analytical skills to assess situations quickly and react using the best options available. Resilience. These roles can be mentally and physically challenging, so resilience is needed to bounce back and keep moving forward toward goals. Teamwork. Some government and public administration service jobs require working as part of an effective team, often under pressure.

title: Who enjoys working in this sector?
transcript: ﻿1 Types of People Who Enjoy Working in this Sector. Government and Public Administration service professionals enjoy being part of something bigger, serving in roles where they can make a difference in the lives of families and communities. It takes passion, commitment, and sometimes personal sacrifice to succeed in such challenging career fields. But workers in this sector often get job satisfaction from helping to make the world a better place.

title: Why is it important?
transcript: ﻿1 Why is it important? Because America is such a large nation and each state has its own government. The government and public administration sector employs millions of workers in hundreds of very different career types. Employees in this sector provide important public services ranging from healthcare and education to economics and infrastructure. The sector is crucial for ensuring our society runs as smoothly as possible, even though we don't always recognize the importance and complexity of their jobs. We couldn't get by without our government and public administration workers!

title: Overview
transcript: ﻿1 Welcome. We will be learning about the National CTE Sector, Government and Public Administration. The Government and Public Administration sector provides essential, government-funded public services, enforces laws, manages public resources, and represents citizens' interests at various levels of governance. The seven main career pathways are Governance, National Security, Foreign Service, Planning, Revenue and Taxation, Regulation, and Public Management and Administration. Popular job titles in this sector include Congressional Aide, Combat Engineer, Cryptographer, Consular Officer, County Director, and Cargo Inspector among others. "Government is the great engine of social progress.", stated former President Franklin D. Roosevelt.

title: Industry Trends
transcript: ﻿1 Industry Trends. The finance sector evolves constantly with changes coming even faster, thanks to globalization and the rise of E-commerce. Companies must keep up with the latest trends such as Web3, the next generation of the internet. Web3 is built on blockchain technology and promises more decentralization, better security, and increased transparency. The rise of the gig economy, a labor market characterized by short-term contracts and freelance work is driving a work from home mindset that's never been used before. Another trend involves data analytics. The process of collecting, cleaning, and analyzing data to extract insights used for decision-making. Businesses that maximize this powerful tool can gain significant advantages over the competition. Finance professionals help create wealth, boost economic growth, and protect consumers. Curious about which pathways might match your personality and interest in this sector? Let's take the Sector Quiz!

title: Different Pathways in the Sector
transcript: ﻿1 Different Pathways in the Sector. The five main career pathways in the finance sector are Securities and Investments, Business Finance, Accounting, Insurance, and Banking Services. What is the Securities and Investments Pathway? Workers in the pathway may manage investment funds or offer services like financial planning, asset management, or other monetary services. They ensure people in businesses can access capital and investment opportunities. Careers in this pathway include Commodities Representative, Foreign Exchange Manager, Title Researcher and Examiner, Treasurer, Trust Officer, Underwriter. What is the Business Finance Pathway? Business finance workers help businesses control costs, manage budgets, find suitable resources, and develop strategies to reach objectives. Careers include Actuary, Bill and Account Collector, Economist, Fundraiser, Internal Auditor, Nonprofit Manager. What is the Accounting Pathway? Accounting professionals record, classify, analyze, and interpret financial information. Then use their findings to help businesses perform better. Careers include Accountant, Controller, Debt Counselor, Tax Examiner. What is the Insurance Pathway? Insurance industry workers explain insurance coverage products, sign up clients for new policies, and help them process claims. They ensure individuals and businesses have financial protection in the event of accidents, robberies, fires, weather related damages, and deaths. Careers include Benefits Consultant, Branch Manager, Claim Adjuster, Claims Agent, Claims Clerk, Insurance Agent. What is the Banking Services Pathway? Banks provide several important financial services to individuals and businesses. Such as accepting and safeguarding monetary deposits for checking and savings, extending lines of credit, and making loans for homes, vehicles, new businesses, and more. Careers include Data Processor, Debt Counselor, Financial Manager, Financial Planner, Internal Auditor, Loan Officer.

title: Top Skills Needed
transcript: ﻿1 Top Skills Needed. Finance jobs require a range of hard and soft skills, including analytical skills, critical thinking, objectivity, problem-solving, decision-making, communication, teamwork, technical abilities, and both leadership and managerial qualities. Analytical. Finance professionals have to acquire and thoroughly analyze data to identify trends and extrapolate information they can use. Attention to detail. Attention to detail is crucial for most positions in the world of finance, especially when tracking financial figures in order to avoid errors. Communication. It's vital to be able to discuss financial matters calmly and clearly, especially when the topics are complex and there are high risks involved. Decision-Making. Financial managers make decisions all the time, often under pressure. They weigh the pros and cons of their options and make their best guess for the company or client. Leadership. In this line of work, it's often necessary to lead teams to complete projects. Leaders have to motivate and inspire others, set clear goals, and delegate tasks. Problem-Solving. Workers must impartially identify problems methodically, look for underlying root causes and come up with innovative solutions. Teamwork. Being able to collaborate effectively is a key aspect of almost every job in this sector. Technical. Several jobs in this sector require specific technical skills, such as proficiency in software for risk management, accounting, or financial modeling.

title: Who enjoys working in this sector?
transcript: ﻿1 Types of People Who Enjoy Working in this Sector. Finance jobs require a lot of focus, objectivity, and attention to detail. Having an aptitude for math is very helpful. People who work in these fields often see the "big picture" when it comes to business and financial trends. They tend to be good with numbers, work well under pressure, and are confident about taking calculated risks. They're also persuasive communicators able to explain complex financial concepts to others in a way that motivates listeners to take action.

title: Why is it important?
transcript: ﻿1 Why is it important? The global economy is built on businesses offering products and services. The finance sector helps people invest wisely, empowers families to borrow funds, to buy vehicles, properties, and other necessities, and gives companies a means to start businesses. Workers entering this sector might operate businesses, manage other people's money, evaluate investment risks and rewards, or help companies make better, more informed decisions.

title: Overview
transcript: ﻿1 Welcome. We will be learning about the National CTE Sector, Finance. The finance sector helps to create jobs, grow the economy, and provide essential products and services to consumers. It deals with managing and exchanging funds, which includes activities like investing, banking, and insurance coverage. As Janet Yellen, Former Chair of the Federal Reserve says, "Finance is the lifeblood of our economy. It allows businesses to grow and invest, and it helps individuals save for their future." The five main career pathways in this sector are Securities and Investments, Business Finance, Accounting, Insurance, and Banking Services. Common job titles are Accountant, Bill and Account Collector, Claims Adjuster, Economist, Financial Manager, and Tax Examiner. People who wanna work in the finance sector are often eager to help the economy while driving innovation and generating revenue. They provide invaluable opportunities for other individuals to achieve economic success in their personal and professional lives.

title: Industry Trends
transcript: ﻿1 Industry Trends. The Education and Training sector is evolving with technology being increasingly incorporated into the classroom. In addition, remote learning has become more of an option for students who face barriers to access or just need some extra flexibility. Another trend is that STEM programs are recognizing the value of creativity and artistic elements with many schools using the term STEAM to include the letter A for arts. Meanwhile, there's a push in early childhood education to provide more high-quality education earlier in life in order to boost the odds of academic and career success later! Take the major quiz education and training are essential for student success, which in turn helps build more qualified workforces and enriched communities. Curious about which pathways might match your personality and interest in this sector? Then let's take the Sector Quiz!

title: Different Pathways in the Sector
transcript: ﻿1 Different Pathways in the Sector. The three main career pathways for this sector are Teaching and Training, Administrative Support, and Professional Support Services. What is Administration and Administrative Support? Administrators provide management and leadership for the institutions where they work, from reviewing academic programs to setting long-term goals. Careers in this pathway include Assessment Specialist, College Dean, College President, Curriculum Developer, Principal, and Superintendent. What are Professional Support Services? Professional support roles, assistant areas such as personal and family needs, mental health support, educational goal setting, and career decision-making. Careers include Clinical Psychologist, Counselor, Developmental Psychologist, Parent Educator, Social Psychologist, and Social Worker. What is the Teaching/Training Pathway? Teachers and trainers are the pillars of any educational institution. Their abilities to communicate concepts, inspire trust, and motivate learners are the keys to learner achievement. Careers include Childcare Worker, College or University Faculty, Elementary Teacher, High School Teacher, Middle School Teacher, and Physical Trainer.

title: Who enjoys working in this sector?
transcript: ﻿1 Types of People Who Enjoy Working in this Sector. Jobs in this sector require a commitment to helping others reach their full potential. Many roles demand a deep understanding of specific academic subjects, as well as how to manage learners in academic settings. Other positions require strong administrative, managerial, or organizational leadership skills. And all educators should have a desire to make positive impacts on the lives of children, families, and communities. Jobs in this sector can be frustrating at times. It takes patience, empathy, and emotional resilience to hang in there. But nothing beats the feeling of helping students get ready for the next big phase of their lives!

title: Skills to Develop for an AI Career
transcript: Skills to Develop for an AI Career. Career advice for the future. "Focus on skills you will learn, not job titles." says Gaurav Ashisha from Nevus Security. What does he mean by that? Since AI is changing how things are done, jobs in the future will be different than they are today. When you plan for the education and training you'll need for your future career, think about the skills that will be needed in the future. Keep in mind that AI isn't going to make all jobs disappear, but it will replace some and help with others. So, it won't hurt to study artificial intelligence and other evolving technologies. Some of the AI skills you could study would be related to computing, data science, intelligent user interfaces, machine learning, natural language processing, programming, robotics. As we mentioned, AI is going to automate many jobs, but we'll always need workers with soft skills. What are those? Soft skills are things like communication, flexibility, problem solving, teamwork, the ability to work independently, time management. Remember, the key to finding jobs in the future is to either do things AI can't do or do things better than AI can.

title: Keeping Up with AI and Technology
transcript: Keeping up with AI and Technology. Digital marketing is a perfect case study of the importance of keeping up with ever evolving technologies. Technologies, including artificial intelligence, are rapidly causing careers to change. Most of these changes are highly beneficial, but they're nonetheless disruptive. That's why one of the most important markers of success in the future of work will be one's ability to stay flexible, ready to adapt, grow, and evolve along with the world around us.

title: Jobs That Wont be Impacted
transcript: Jobs That Won't Be Impacted By AI Some jobs aren't going to be affected much by AI either way. For example, Mike Rowe, the host of the TV show Dirty Jobs, says, "I haven't seen any plumbing robots." AI can't replace human workers in certain trade jobs, aka skilled labor, like plumbing, electrical work, carpentry, or other hands-on professions such as mechanic work, truck driving, cooking, et cetera. AI might assist with some parts of the job, but it's too expensive to use AI powered robots for those jobs, except for things like manufacturing and other assembly line processes. Cool trivia. Many trade/ skilled labor jobs don't require a college degree. Workers can learn and get paid at the same time through apprenticeship programs. Trade / skilled labor jobs often pay as much or more than white collar office jobs. The term skilled labor can apply to some hands on white collar jobs, such as doctors, nurses, or engineers.

title: Navigating Your Career: Embracing Non-Linear Paths and Uncertainty
transcript: ﻿1 How to Embrace Career Uncertainty. Of course, navigating change isn't always easy. It can feel intimidating to step into something so new, especially when you're not sure how it'll turn out. But uncertainty isn't something to avoid - it's something to embrace. When Luca transitioned from the film industry to EdTech, he faced plenty of unknowns. Would his skills translate? Could he adapt to a completely different work environment? Instead of letting those questions hold him back, he focused on what he could control. He leaned into his strengths, like project management, and creative problem-solving, which turned out to be incredibly valuable in his new role. He stayed curious, asking questions, and learning from others in the education space. He embraced lifelong learning, Viewing the transition as an opportunity to grow, not just professionally but personally too. Uncertainty can feel overwhelming, but it's also where the magic happens. It's where you discover your new passions, build new skills, and find opportunities you've never imagined.

title: The Organizer
transcript: ﻿1 The Organizer. Organizers are Conventional types who thrive on structure. They have an inherent urge to ensure things are in the right place at the right time. They enjoy scheduling, planning, managing tasks, and following procedures. Their analytical skills and attention to detail make them highly sought after by employers who need to manage projects, maintain records, and get things running efficiently. Organizers make excellent human resources managers, office administrators, property managers, and nurses.

title: The Persuader
transcript: ﻿1 The Persuader. Some people are natural leaders who excel at convincing people to do things. These Enterprising types are known as Persuaders. They can sell anything - including ideas! Persuaders are confident, ambitious, and full of energy. They love a challenge and can be very competitive risk takers. Persuaders gravitate toward careers where they can lead and influence, such as business management, sales, politics, and public relations.

title: The People Person
transcript: ﻿1 The People Person. Those who fall into the Social type, are called People Persons. They're driven to interact with others, usually with the goal of helping or educating in some way. People Persons are outgoing, excellent communicators who naturally empathize with and care about others. They made great counselors, teachers, trainers, customer service representatives, and psychologists.

title: The Creator
transcript: ﻿1 The Creator. Do you know people who love to express themselves through art, music, or writing? These are the Artistic, or Creator, types. They have rich imaginations, enjoy creating beautiful things, and often prefer working independently so they can communicate their ideas the way they want. Creators are happiest in jobs where they can explore their artistic passions, such as graphic or fashion design, writing, film directing, or playing music.

title: The Problem Solver
transcript: ﻿1 The Problem Solver. The investigative type, also known as Problem Solvers, are like detectives who love to solve mysteries. They calmly ask questions, methodically gather information, and figure things out with logical efficiency. Since every organization has problems, there's always a need for Problem Solvers! They make great scientists, doctors, accountants, technical writers, and software developers among numerous other career types.

title: The Builder
transcript: ﻿1 The Builder. Some people love working with their hands! They love to see the tangible results of their hard work and precision. These are Realistic, or Builder types. Builders enjoy making or maintaining things in the physical world. They are practical, like being outdoors and don't mind physical activity. Builder type titles include: Carpenter, Mechanic, Electrician, Construction Worker, and Engineer.

title: Taking the Gladeo Quiz
transcript: Este video es presentado por Gladeo On the Go y the Los Angeles Opportunity Youth Collaborative. Mi nombre es Cheyenne. Pero me hago llamar Shy y actualmente me desempeño como Asistente Ejecutiva de programación actual en ITV América. Estaba en mi último trimestre en UCLA cuando llegó la pandemia y tenía a mi jefe. En cualquier momento, podría encontrarse con un correo electrónico o una pasantía o siempre me lo enviaría por correo electrónico y diría: Oye, creo que serías bueno para esto. Estuve involucrado en el desarrollo de nuestro podcast que se lanzó en Spotify y estuve involucrado en nuestro programa de Netflix que estábamos haciendo. Así que pude ver todo el bote pero no me inspiré particularmente por la demografía preescolar. Siempre me han atraído más los adolescentes. Pude mirar al director y decir, oh, eso es, quiero estar haciendo lo que él está haciendo. ¿Puede hablar sobre algunas de las cualidades que encontraría en un asistente ejecutivo realmente bueno? Claramente, tienes que ser organizado, pero ¿qué otras cosas encontrarías en una persona que realmente haría bien? Tienes que ser capaz de adaptarte. Así que algún día podrías estar trabajando en esto. Y luego, al día siguiente, algo sucede y sus nuevas prioridades son estas. Creo que otro es resistente en esta industria. Simplemente hay mucha presión en el set. Puede haber muchas cosas que simplemente se acumulan y pueden haber muchas cosas que te arrojan. Mucha ira fuera de lugar o puede cometer un error. Y tienes que ser capaz de no dejar que eso te apague por completo. Ya has hecho tantas cosas increíbles y eres tan joven. Entonces, ¿cuáles son algunas de sus metas en los próximos años? En los próximos años, Realmente quiero cambiar la narrativa sobre los jóvenes de crianza y realmente cambiar la forma en que la gente ve el cuidado de crianza y los jóvenes de crianza. Tengo muchas ganas de que sea la próxima Annie. Me encantaría hacer una nueva historia de jóvenes adoptivos a través de un musical. Pero realmente tipo de tomar y quitar todos los estereotipos negativos que implementaron allí. Y creo que la forma en que retratan las historias no es tan auténtica como podría ser. Y luego pienso, por último, simplemente estar en paz. Creo que es algo que siempre he querido hacer y siempre he querido sentir. Entonces, creo que llegar a un punto en el que pueda expresarme creativamente y ayudar a fomentar la juventud a través de Jennis Flower, ese sería uno de mis objetivos principales. ¡Increíble! Cheyenne, ¡vas a cambiar el mundo! Realmente creo eso. Muchas gracias por tomarse el tiempo de su día para sentarse y compartir todo esto con nosotros. Por supuesto, gracias Katelyn! title: The Six Personality Types
transcript: ﻿1 The Problem Solver. The investigative type, also known as Problem Solvers, are like detectives who love to solve mysteries. They calmly ask questions, methodically gather information, and figure things out with logical efficiency. Since every organization has problems, there's always a need for Problem Solvers! They make great scientists, doctors, accountants, technical writers, and software developers among numerous other career types.

title: Own Your Future: Famous Journeys of Passion and Persistence
transcript: ﻿1 Famous Journeys of Passion and Persistence. Let's take a closer look at an inspiring story of persistence and determination: Trevor Noah didn't have an easy path to success. He grew up in South Africa during apartheid, where his very existence as a child of a mixed-race couple was considered illegal. Despite these challenges, Trevor developed a knack for humor as a way to connect with people and navigate difficult situations. Starting out as a small-time comedian in radio DJ, he performed anywhere he could - sometimes in tiny venues with just a handful of people. But he didn't let the size of the audience or the odds against him stop him. He kept refining his craft, learning how to tell stories that made people laugh and think. Eventually, his persistence paid off. Trevor Noah's unique voice and perspective caught the attention of international audiences, leading him to become the host of The Daily Show. Now, he's one of the most influential comedians in the world, using his platform to shed light on global issues and inspire millions through his humor and storytelling. Trevor's journey is a testament to the power of persistence. No matter the obstacles he faced, he kept moving forward, proving that with resilience and determination, it's possible to turn your dreams into a reality.

title: Own Your Future: Imagine the Possibilities
transcript: ﻿1 Own Your Future. Imagine the possibilities. The future isn't something that happens to you - it's something you create. Imagine this: What if you could wake up every day excited about the work you do? What if your passions and motivations could guide you toward a career that feels fulfilling and meaningful? That's what today is all about. We're taking the first step in designing your future. But don't worry - you don't need to have everything figured out just yet. This is all about curiosity and exploration, not commitment. Think of your passions and motivations as a compass. They point you toward the careers where you can thrive. Maybe you're passionate about solving problems, helping others, or creating something new. These are the sparks that can lead to exciting possibilities.

title: Finding Your Drive: Reflecting on Passions and Interests
transcript: ﻿1 Interests and Passions: How They Intersect. The key differences between passions and interests lie in their depth and level of engagement. Passions are deeply energizing, long-term pursuits that align with your personal values and drive intense commitment. They're more than mere hobbies - they become integral to your identity and purpose, fueling your motivation and shaping your goals. Interests on the other hand, are more casual and exploratory. They spark curiosity and allow you to explore various topics and experiences without the emotional investment or pressure of deep commitment. Interests often provide a sense of variety and freedom, encouraging a broad exploration of the world around you. However, passions and interests are not entirely separate - they often build on each other. Interests typically serve as the starting point, sparking initial curiosity and exploration. Over time, as you go deeper into an interest and discover aspects that resonate with your emotions or values, it can evolve into a passion. For example, let's consider someone with a general interest in reading. They might enjoy exploring different genres and discovering new stories. Over time, they find themselves particularly drawn to comics captivated by the compelling backstories and morals of iconic characters like the Teen Titans or the X-Men. "These heroes aren't just entertaining - they represent resilience, justice, and complex human emotions." This deeper connection sparks a passion for storytelling. Inspired by the way these characters connect with readers. This person begins crafting their own stories, weaving together imaginative worlds and relatable characters. What started as a casual interest in reading transforms into a lifelong passion for writing, eventually shaping their creative journey and even their career path. By exploring your interests, you can uncover hidden passions that excite and energize you. Passions often grow stronger with time and emotional investment, while interests provide the foundation for that growth.

title: Finding Your Drive: Passions vs Everyday Work
transcript: ﻿1 Passion Versus Everyday Work. Let's be honest - no job is exciting 100% of the time. But when you're passionate about what you do, even the boring or repetitive tasks can feel worthwhile because they're part of a bigger picture. Imagine someone passionate about helping others, like a doctor or a nurse. The paperwork and long hours might not be fun, but every patient they help reminds them why they chose this path. Passion isn't about loving every moment of the journey - it's about believing in the larger goal. Staying connected to your purpose gives you the energy to push through tough moments and keep moving forward.

title: Finding Your Drive: 5 Ways Interests Grow Into Passions
transcript: ﻿1 Five Ways Interests Can Grow Into Passions. Interests are the starting point, but how do they grow into passions? Let's explore five ways this transformation happens. One. Exploration and Engagement. The more time you spend exploring an interest, the deeper your connection to it grows. By diving in and engaging fully, you uncover new layers and possibilities that resonate with you on a personal level. Two. Emotional Investment. As you dedicate time and energy to an interest, it starts to feel less like a pastime and more like a meaningful part of your life. This emotional connection brings a sense of fulfillment and attachment. Three. Positive Feedback. Encouragement from others can boost your confidence and deepen your commitment. Whether it's a teacher praising your progress or a friend recognizing your talent, positive feedback turns curiosity into motivation. Four. Alignment with Values. When an interest aligns with your personal goals or values, it becomes more meaningful. For example, if you care deeply about the environment, your interest in wildlife might grow into a passion for conservation. Five. Inspiration From Role Models. Sometimes, a mentor or role model can spark a deeper connection to your interest. Seeing someone succeed in an area you enjoy can inspire you to follow in their footsteps and take your own interest to the next level. By exploring these pathways, you can turn a simple interest into a passion that energizes and inspires you every day.

title: Finding Your Drive: How Do Interests and Passions Intersect?
transcript: ﻿1 Interests and Passions: How They Intersect. The key differences between passions and interests lie in their depth and level of engagement. Passions are deeply energizing, long-term pursuits that align with your personal values and drive intense commitment. They're more than mere hobbies - they become integral to your identity and purpose, fueling your motivation and shaping your goals. Interests on the other hand, are more casual and exploratory. They spark curiosity and allow you to explore various topics and experiences without the emotional investment or pressure of deep commitment. Interests often provide a sense of variety and freedom, encouraging a broad exploration of the world around you. However, passions and interests are not entirely separate - they often build on each other. Interests typically serve as the starting point, sparking initial curiosity and exploration. Over time, as you go deeper into an interest and discover aspects that resonate with your emotions or values, it can evolve into a passion. For example, let's consider someone with a general interest in reading. They might enjoy exploring different genres and discovering new stories. Over time, they find themselves particularly drawn to comics captivated by the compelling backstories and morals of iconic characters like the Teen Titans or the X-Men. "These heroes aren't just entertaining - they represent resilience, justice, and complex human emotions." This deeper connection sparks a passion for storytelling. Inspired by the way these characters connect with readers. This person begins crafting their own stories, weaving together imaginative worlds and relatable characters. What started as a casual interest in reading transforms into a lifelong passion for writing, eventually shaping their creative journey and even their career path. By exploring your interests, you can uncover hidden passions that excite and energize you. Passions often grow stronger with time and emotional investment, while interests provide the foundation for that growth.

title: Finding Your Drive: How Passion Fuels Motivation
transcript: ﻿1 How Passion Fuels Motivation. Passion isn't just about what excites you - it's a force that helps you stay focused, resilient, and connected with others who share your goals. Think about the last time you were so engaged in something that time seemed to fly by, and that's your brain's reward system at work. Releasing chemicals like dopamine and norepinephrine, these create feelings of pleasure, excitement, and focus. This biological response fuels your motivation, helping you push through challenges and stay committed, even when things get tough. Let's talk about some inspiring examples of people who turn their passions into powerful achievements. Amanda Gorman had a passion for poetry that helped her overcome a significant personal challenge - a speech impediment. She transformed this obstacle into a source of strength using her poetry as a way to express herself and connect with others. Her dedication eventually led her to become the youngest inaugural poet in U.S. history. "Through her words, she has inspired millions, proving how passion can turn limitations and profound opportunities." Ben Pasterneck discovered his passion for creativity and technology at a young age, inspired by his love of gaming. As a teenager, he taught himself coding and created popular apps like Impossible Rush. By the age of 17, his drive and innovation led him to found his own tech company. "Ben's story shows how following your interests can lead to exciting opportunities to build, create, and make an impact." When you're passionate about something, it becomes easier to keep going even in the face of obstacles.

title: Importance of Self Awareness
transcript: ﻿1 The Importance of Self-Awareness. The importance of self-awareness can be traced back to ancient times. When the Greek philosopher's Socrates stated, "The unexamined life is not worth living." Or, as Socrates student Plato liked to say, "Know yourself." One reason why it's so important to know ourselves is because other people usually don't know who we are deep down. And if they don't know us, they may not understand us. In fact, they might even misjudge us. It can be harmful if we hear and believe things about ourselves that aren't true, based on the opinions of people who really don't even know us. As Nobel Prize winning physicist Albert Einstein once said, "Everyone is a genius. But if you judge a fish by its ability to climb a tree, it will live its whole life believing that it is stupid." What did Einstein mean by this? The implication is that, though "everyone is a genius," we're geniuses in different ways. We each have our own personal set of talents and aptitudes, which we can develop and expand on. That doesn't mean we can't learn entirely new things, though. It simply suggests that if we're judged either by others or by ourselves on abilities we haven't developed yet, we might think there's something wrong with us, yet nothing could be further from the truth. The trick is assessing where our inherent strengths lie, and which areas we might want to improve on. Now let's see how this applies in real life by considering the story of Temple Grandin, a renowned animal behaviorist, inventor, an advocate for autism awareness. Growing up with autism, Temple faced many challenges, including difficulty with social interactions and traditional learning environments. However, her unique way of thinking allowed her to excel in visualizing complex systems, which she turned into a powerful strength. Temple pursued her passion for animals, earning a PhD in animal science, and going on to revolutionize the global livestock industry by designing more humane systems for handling cattle. Her ability to harness her inherent strengths, like her exceptional spatial awareness and attention to detail, helped her improve animal welfare around the world while enhancing efficiency for farmers. Today, Temple is celebrated not only for her contributions to science but also for raising awareness about neurodiversity. Her story shows how understanding and embracing our individual talents can lead to meaningful success and fulfillment!

title: Introduction to Self Awareness
transcript: ﻿1 Introduction to Self-Awareness. As students, you're doing your best to get through school so you can be prepared for your future. But what do you want to do in your future" And who do you want to be? Sometimes that's hard to figure out.. especially when we don't fully understand who we are now. So before you start exploring career paths and the required education and training for them, in this chapter, let's explore YOU! This chapter is all about you getting to know yourself better. Discovering what you truly want in life begins with exploring who you are, what you believe, and what matters most to you. Self-awareness is a lifelong journey, and each step helps you understand yourself a little better. To do that, you'll have to deep dive and explore your core values, motivations, and priorities. And also your interests, skills, and aptitude. Being self-aware also involves knowing your goals in life, your own definition of success, and how you learn from experiences. All these unique variables weave together to make you the fascinating person you currently are. And like the rest of us, "who you are" changes and evolves as you get older. Getting to know yourself better will help you align your future career choices with who you truly are. The more you understand yourself, the more confidently you can explore the world of career possibilities. But remember, exploring your own self-awareness isn't a one-time process; it's a lifelong journey.

title: Motivations and Passions: Spotlight Series
transcript: Chapter 1: Motivation and Passion. Our motivations and passions are the key to finding and succeeding in our future careers. The two main types of motivation are intrinsic and extrinsic. Intrinsic motivation comes from the internal satisfaction we get from doing something. Extrinsic motivation comes from external rewards or outcomes, such as your boss telling you to do something. The intrinsic type, the kind that comes from within us is more powerful because it fosters deep engagement and commitment. So when are we intrinsically motivated? When we're passionate about things. If we're passionate about an activity, subject or cause, we're more enthusiastic and committed to it. That passion fuels our internal, intrinsic motivation. That's why it's so important to identify our passions and motivations when it comes to our career journeys. But it takes more than passion to motivate us. It also takes belief. The belief that you can do something if you put your mind to it. That's where visualization comes in. Visualization is when you can see yourself doing something. This powerful habit is used by the most successful people on earth, and it can help you too. Visualization allows you to transform abstract ideas into clearly defined goals. From there, it's easy to map out actionable steps to reach these goals, and with each step, you'll feel more motivated as you get closer to your dream career. Our career spotlights showcase successful individuals who were willing to share their stories with you to help you visualize yourself in similar careers. So let's watch a few examples of these career spotlights. Later, you can explore others on your own. The fastest way to find them is under the BE INSPIRED section of your regional Gladeo platform website on the spotlights page. Note, at the end of the module, there will be a few knowledge check questions, so make sure to answer those and hit submit when you're done.

title: Part 3: Workforce Readiness
transcript: Full-time versus Part-time At the undergraduate level, a full-time student must be enrolled in at least 12 credits per semester term, which equates to four three credit courses, for example. However, 12 credits per term isn't enough to finish a bachelor's in four years. A bachelor's degree requires at least 120 semester credits to complete. So to graduate in four years, a student should take 15 credits per semester unless they attend summer classes. Thus, as you can see, a student who is technically full-time would take five years to finish a bachelor's if they're only taking 12 credits per term. Part-time students take less than a full course load of classes. A part-time student may only take one or more classes per term, but must pay attention to the school's maximum timeframe allowed to complete degree requirements. Each college sets its own policies regarding the maximum timeframe for degree completion. Students receiving federal financial aid should be careful not to exceed this maximum timeframe, or they will fail to meet satisfactory academic progress requirements and could lose their financial aid eligibility unless they qualify for an extension.

title: Part 2: Career Exploration - Deep Diving into the 16 Industry Sectors
transcript: ﻿1 Industry Trends. The Education and Training sector is evolving with technology being increasingly incorporated into the classroom. In addition, remote learning has become more of an option for students who face barriers to access or just need some extra flexibility. Another trend is that STEM programs are recognizing the value of creativity and artistic elements with many schools using the term STEAM to include the letter A for arts. Meanwhile, there's a push in early childhood education to provide more high-quality education earlier in life in order to boost the odds of academic and career success later! Take the major quiz education and training are essential for student success, which in turn helps build more qualified workforces and enriched communities. Curious about which pathways might match your personality and interest in this sector? Then let's take the Sector Quiz!

title: Part 1: Foundations: Who am I?
transcript: ﻿1 Imagine our personalities are like puzzle pieces that fit into specific jobs and activity types. The Holland Codes framework helps us solve our puzzles by categorizing our different personality types and explaining which careers or occupational themes they match up to. This unique system was developed by American psychologist, Dr. John Holland, who conceived of six personality groups based on specific interests and preferences. These groups are labeled as follows: Realistic, also known as builders or doers. Investigative, also known as problem solvers or thinkers. Artistic, also known as creators. Social, also known as people persons or helpers. Enterprising, also known as persuaders. And Conventional, also known as organizers. Most of us fall into more than one of these categories, which is normal. The Holland system wasn't designed to pigeonhole us or limit our options, but understanding your Holland Code types will help you identify careers that align with your interests and capabilities. So think of it as a thought provoking starting point for your career exploration journey. Now, let's review each type a bit more.

title: Luca's Story: Turning Passion into Purpose
transcript: ﻿1 Luca's Story: Turning Passion into Purpose. Let's take a closer look at Luca's journey. As a child, Luca found comfort in movies. For him, they were more than entertainment - they were a way to escape life's challenges and immerse himself in imaginative worlds. This love for storytelling sparked an interest in filmmaking, which grew into a passion for creating stories that could inspire others just as those films had inspired him. But pursuing that dream wasn't easy. In high school, a teacher told him, "People like you don't make it in Hollywood. You should find something more realistic." Those words could have crushed his spirit, but for Luca, they lit a fire. That moment wasn't just a setback, it became his motivation. He wanted to prove that he could succeed, not just for himself, but for others who might've been told the same thing. With this newfound determination, Luca decided to take a practical yet creative approach. He enrolled in art school not to study filmmaking, but to focus on business management. Why? He wanted to understand how the industry worked behind the scenes. Being surrounded by artists in such an environment deepened his passion for creative problem-solving, and introduced him to the production side of entertainment. By the time he graduated, Luca had landed a role as a Production Assistant at a major visual effects company. This wasn't just any job - it was his first step into the world of professional filmmaking. Production assistants or PAs are the glue that holds projects together, especially in the fast-paced world of visual effects. Luca quickly learned how to coordinate between artists, producers, and even directors, ensuring that projects stayed on track. He wasn't just watching the magic happen, he was part of making it happen. Over the next few years, Luca worked on some of the biggest franchises in Hollywood. From Marvel to Star Wars, seeing his name in the credits for the first time was surreal. A tangible reminder that his hard work, resilience, and passion had paid off. It wasn't just a job - it was a dream come true. But even as he thrived in the film industry, something began to shift. While Luca loved the creativity and collaboration that came with filmmaking, he realized his motivation had always been about more than just creating movies. He started thinking about how his skills and experiences could make a difference in other ways. His interest began to evolve, and his passion for storytelling transformed into a new purpose, helping others craft their own stories. That's when Luca decided to pivot. He transitioned into the education technology space, or EdTech for short. Where he now helps create engaging materials designed to inspire students to pursue their own career dreams. Drawing on his background in filmmaking, Luca uses his creative skills to design content that's both educational and captivating. Luca's journey illustrates the powerful connection between passion, interest, and motivation. His love for storytelling ignited his passion for filmmaking, which motivated him to overcome challenges and build a thriving career in Hollywood. And when his interest shifted, he adapted, channeling his creativity into a new field where he could have an even broader impact.

title: How to Embrace Career Uncertainty
transcript: ﻿1 How to Embrace Career Uncertainty. Of course, navigating change isn't always easy. It can feel intimidating to step into something so new, especially when you're not sure how it'll turn out. But uncertainty isn't something to avoid - it's something to embrace. When Luca transitioned from the film industry to EdTech, he faced plenty of unknowns. Would his skills translate? Could he adapt to a completely different work environment? Instead of letting those questions hold him back, he focused on what he could control. He leaned into his strengths, like project management, and creative problem-solving, which turned out to be incredibly valuable in his new role. He stayed curious, asking questions, and learning from others in the education space. He embraced lifelong learning, Viewing the transition as an opportunity to grow, not just professionally but personally too. Uncertainty can feel overwhelming, but it's also where the magic happens. It's where you discover your new passions, build new skills, and find opportunities you've never imagined.

title: What Can We Learn from Luca’s Journey?
transcript: ﻿1 What Can We Learn from Lucas's Journey? Lucas's story shows us that career paths don't have to be linear to be successful. Each step he took, whether it was working on a blockbuster film or learning the ropes of a new industry, helped him grow and prepared him for what came next. Think about it like this: every experience you have is like a piece of a puzzle. It might not make sense on its own, but over time the pieces come together to create a bigger picture of who you are and what you're capable of.

title: Transferable Skills: Your Career Superpower
transcript: ﻿1 Transferable Skills: Your Career Superpower. One of the most important lessons from Lucas's story is the value of transferable skills. These are abilities you develop in one role that can be applied to countless others. In Luca's case, his experience managing complex film projects translated seamlessly into organizing educational initiatives. Let's break it down. Project Management. In the film industry, Luca mastered the art of breaking down large scale projects into smaller, manageable tasks. He coordinated schedules, tracked deadlines, and ensured seamless communication between departments, like visual effects, production, and post-production. In his new role, he applied these same skills to manage educational content production, creating timelines for course development, aligning teams, and ensuring projects met launch deadlines. Creative Storytelling. As a visual effects coordinator, Luca worked closely with producers and artists to bring compelling narratives to life. On screen. He learned how to captivate an audience with visuals and pacing. In EdTech, this translated into designing student-friendly materials that blend education with entertainment. Keeping learners engaged and motivated. Team Coordination. Working under tight deadlines in the film industry required Luca to coordinate effectively across departments and troubleshoot problems in real time. These collaboration skills became invaluable in his new role. Where he liaised with instructional designers, graphic artists, and content writers to develop cohesive learning experiences. Adaptability. In film production, every project brought new challenges from last minute script changes to adapting to evolving technologies. This honed Lucas' ability to pivot quickly and stay focused. Transitioning to the EdTech industry required a similar mindset as he learned to navigate a new domain, master unfamiliar tools, and adjust to a different audience, students rather than moviegoers. By identifying these skills and recognizing his versatility, Luca didn't just transfer his talents, he elevated them in a way that allowed him to make a meaningful impact in a new field. Transferable skills aren't just about what you do, they're about how you do it. You probably have a lot of skills in your toolbox that can be transferred from one project to another. Think about your current strengths and how they can apply to future jobs.

title: Jobs That Might Grow
transcript: Jobs That Might Grow Because of AI AI careers are hot and projected to grow with job titles like big data engineer, business intelligence developer, data scientist, machine learning engineer, natural language processing NLP engineer, robotics engineer, software architect. These jobs require studying computer science, math, and statistics. Many require a college degree, but that is starting to change because of something called the talent gap. The talent gap means there are not enough qualified people to do a particular job. When there's a talent gap, companies might change their requirements to be qualified. In other words, jobs that require a college degree today might not in the near future. Instead of degrees, boot camps and certification programs are shorter, more specialized ways to get the education and training needed.

title: Jobs That Might Shrink
transcript: Jobs that Might Shrink Because of AI Many career fields have already been impacted by the explosion of AI. For example, creative careers. AI is surprisingly creative. DALLE 2 can create realistic images and art from a description in natural language. That means users can type in a text description and the program will make works of art in seconds. This will impact graphic design and visual media career fields. ChatGPT-4 is affecting writing jobs such as copywriting, content creation, and even news reporting. Open AI's Jukebox generates music including rudimentary singing as raw audio in a variety of genres and artist styles. MuseNet can produce four minute musical compositions with 10 different instruments and can combine styles from country to Mozart to the Beatles. These programs will impact musician's work. AI is also used for photo manipulation, deep fake videos and voice cloning, all of which will affect creative fields. Customer Service Careers Ever seen a self-service checkout counter at a store, fast food franchise or airport? Well, those counters are going to get more advanced because of AI. This may mean fewer human customer service agents. As AI gets better at answering questions, chatbots for store websites, apps, and call centers will keep taking over customer service tasks too. One day customers might not even know if they're talking to an AI customer service representative or not. The Turing Test and Digital Humans Consider the Turing test. If a machine can engage in a conversation with a human without being detected as a machine, it has demonstrated human intelligence, writes Investopedia. The Turing test involves a human tester communicating with something they can't see. That something is either another person or an artificial intelligence program. The tester doesn't know which. If they are communicating with an AI that fools them into believing they're communicating with a person, then the AI passes the Turing test. The Turing test has already been beaten in recent years. Now, companies want to use AI for customer service roles more than ever. Using AI is cheaper than using human customer service workers. The only thing missing, a human face, but even that's being worked on. Companies are using deep fake technology to create digital humans. A hybrid of AI and computer graphics that puts human-like faces on chatbots and other computer-based interfaces. Technology careers. It's ironic, but high tech jobs are at risk of being replaced by high tech AI. AI can write code, analyze data, and do much of the work that programmers and software engineers do. And AI can do it in less time. Does that mean those jobs will disappear? Not entirely, but AI is being trained to replace many of these workers. There will still be tech jobs in the future, but maybe not as many.

title: How Does AI Help Us?
transcript: How does AI help us? AI programs are efficient and helpful. They do boring, repetitive tasks so that we don't have to. This gives us more free time to focus on other things. AI can even keep us safer. For example, in car manufacturing, AI works together with industrial robots to build cars on assembly lines. They do the most dangerous tasks so humans don't have to. At home, school and work, AI-powered search engines help us find things on the internet. AI apps make customized shopping and social media recommendations for us by using computer programming called algorithms. Algorithms are instructions that tell the AI what to do so it can use data to make decisions. Algorithms can save lives too. They help medical professionals detect diseases in patients and offer healthcare plans, so patients get well faster. In business and finance, AI prevents fraud and cybersecurity threats. AI smart devices save energy and make life more convenient. AI also helps in fields like education, environmental protection, transportation, entertainment, you name it. But because AI is doing so much work, it's changing the work left for people to do Generative AI Have you ever heard of chatbots? These generative AI programs use natural language processing to write complex articles and essays. Chatbot platforms such as Open AI's ChatGPT, and Google's Bard have changed how people work and how companies do business. Related programs like Canva AI, Crayon and Dolly create works of art, while Jukebox, Musenet and Soundraw compose music. This exciting wave of technology is changing whole career fields. Employees in creative jobs will have to adapt and learn how to use AI tools because AI is starting to do their work. In some cases, AI might replace human workers entirely, but there will still be a need for workers who understand how to use and develop AI. Let's look at some career fields projected to shrink or grow because of AI.

title: Introduction
transcript: AI and Its Effect on the Working World What is AI? Computer scientists have been developing artificial intelligence or AI for years, but what exactly is it? AI has been defined as a machine's ability to perform the cognitive functions we usually associate with human minds. In other words, AI is when a computer program can think like a person. That includes its ability to learn new things, solve problems, be creative, and talk to humans the way we talk to each other. AI is already used in thousands of apps and programs we use all the time. It's also used in hundreds of different career industries like banking, manufacturing, real estate, healthcare and more. There are different types of AI for different purposes. The newest type is called Theory of Mind, which can understand humans better and figure out what we need. Theory of mind includes artificial narrow intelligence like the voice assistant program, Siri and Alexa, and generative AI like Google's Bard and ChatGPT. There's another type of AI being worked on called Artificial general intelligence or strong AI. Strong AI would involve a computer program being self-aware and able to solve problems, learn and plan for the future, as IBM explains it. Strong AI doesn't exist yet, but if it ever does, it'll be as close to a human mind as possible. AI breakthroughs have changed our daily lives a lot already. They're even changing how we work. Let's explore how.

title: Real-World Examples about Learning from Failure
transcript: ﻿1 Real-World Examples About Learning From Failure. Sometimes the best way to see how failure fuels growth is by looking at those who've stumbled, learned, and forged a new path forward. Let's take a look at three Gen Z icons who turned setbacks into stepping stones. Chappell Roan. Dropped by her record label, Chappell Roan refused to quit. She kept creating music independently, eventually building a passionate fan base and reigniting her career on her own terms. Most recently, she won her first Grammy in 2025 for "Best New Artist," solidifying her place in the pop music pantheon. Gitanjali Rao. Named "Kid of the Year" by time in 2020, this teen inventor faced skepticism and trial-and-error while developing "Tethys," a device that detects lead in water. By refining her prototypes and learning from each hurdle, she inspired other young innovators worldwide. Jack Andraka. In high school, Andraka endured repeated rejections while working on an early test for pancreatic cancer. Undeterred, he refined his research, found a supportive lab, and ultimately created a breakthrough test proving that resilience can overcome even the steepest obstacles. These stories underscore the fact that failure doesn't have to be final. Often, it's the trial run that reveals what you need to adjust to make your next attempt successful. By normalizing failure as part of the path, you remove much of its sting and discover that it can actually be a powerful catalyst for achievement.

title: Turning Failure into Fuel
transcript: ﻿1 Turning failure into fuel. Learning from failure starts with a decision to stop seeing your mistakes as proof of inadequacy. Instead, treat each stumble as an invitation to explore new solutions. Analyze what went wrong in a calm, factual way, pinpoint the changes you need to make, and then try again with renewed focus. This process of reflection, adjustment and retrying is what turns your setbacks into setups for success. The more you engage in it, the less intimidating failure becomes. You'll start to recognize a pattern. Each failure teaches you something that you carry into future endeavors, making you more capable than you were before. Remember that resilience and growth often emerge from the toughest moments. By facing failure with a learning mindset, you transform short-term disappointments into stepping stones toward your goals. Over time, you'll realize that the most meaningful triumphs are often built on a foundation of earlier attempts that didn't quite work out. That's the real secret. Fail, learn, adapt, and keep going because it's in the journey of continuous improvement that true success unfolds.

title: Overcoming the Fear of Trying Again
transcript: ﻿1 - Reflect on your failures. Look at them as a lesson to learn. Ask yourself, what can I take away from this experience? How can I improve the next time? Use the emotions from failure to become stronger. German philosopher, Friedrich Nietzche said, what does not kill me makes me stronger? Well, now there's science to back this up. Scientists at Northwestern University's, Kellogg School of Management utilized advanced analytics to assess the relationship between professional failure and success for young scientists. They found, in contrast to their initial expectations, that failure early in one's career leads to greater success in the long term for those who try again. The lead author Yang Wang said, the attrition rate does not increase for those who fail early in their careers, but those who stick it out on average perform much better in the long term. Suggesting that if it doesn't kill you, it really does make you stronger. Practice self-compassion. Research has shown that self-compassion is closely associated with emotional resilience, including the ability to soothe ourselves, recognize our mistakes, learn from them, and motivate ourselves to succeed.

title: Identifying Actionable Steps
transcript: ﻿1 Identifying Actionable Steps. Once you understand what happened, it's time to plan how you'll do things differently. Actionable steps should be concrete and realistic. If you failed a math test because you studied alone and got stuck on certain concepts, you might decide to join a study group or ask for help from a teacher or tutor. If you flopped in a job interview because you froze up under pressure, you could schedule mock interviews with a friend or mentor to build your confidence. The point is to translate your insights into actions that you can practice right away. These steps don't have to be complicated. They just need to address the specific reasons you failed the first time around. If you keep repeating the same approach, chances are you'll get similar results. Changing your tactics even in small ways, opens the door to new outcomes.

title: Analyzing What Went Wrong - Without Judgement
transcript: ﻿1 Analyzing What Went Wrong Without Judgment. When you're analyzing a failure, it's important to keep self-criticism in check. Blaming yourself harshly with thoughts like, "I'm just not good enough" or blaming outside forces with thoughts like, "The teacher hates me" won't help you grow. Instead, aim for a clear headed look at the situation. Ask yourself, what was my goal and where did I fall short? What factors contributed to the outcome? Were there warning signs I ignored? Which parts of my strategy or approach seemed weak? This kind of objective questioning turns your focus toward understanding the event rather than punishing yourself or making excuses. Maybe you didn't manage your time well, or perhaps you misunderstood the instructions. Pinpointing those issues gives you a roadmap for improvement.

title: Seeing Mistakes as Opportunities
transcript: ﻿1 Seeing Mistakes as Opportunities. When something goes wrong, your first reaction might be frustration or embarrassment. That's natural. But if you dwell on those feelings too long, you miss the opportunity to learn. It's far more useful to ask, "What happened here?" and "What can I learn from this?" than to repeatedly tell yourself, "I messed up." Sometimes, even a seemingly small mistake can be a big turning point if you're open to learning from it. Maybe you've flubbed a presentation at school because you tried to wing it instead of practicing. That tough experience could motivate you to rehearse thoroughly next time turning what felt like a disaster into a long-term improvement in your public speaking skills.

title: Top Skills Needed
transcript: ﻿1 Top Skills Needed. Many career options in this sector require skills for ensuring guests and customers feel welcomed, respected, and taken care of. Below are a few of the specific skills needed. Adaptability and Flexibility. When dealing with a high volume of guests or customers, workers need flexibility and adaptability to address unexpected situations. Communication. Most roles require communicating effectively with guests or customers as well as coworkers, supervisors, managers, and other parties. Cultural Awareness. Knowledge of other cultures helps businesses avoid actions that might be misinterpreted or perceived as inappropriate. Customer Service. This sector relies on providing outstanding customer service experiences. Workers should be knowledgeable, competent, polite, composed, and attentive to guests' needs. Language Skills. Many businesses receive non-English speaking guests, so it helps to have speakers of other languages on site to assist with customer relations. Organization and Time Management. There are a million "behind the scenes" things to take care of before guests arrive, during their stay and after they depart. It's critical to stay organized and manage time effectively. Problem-Solving. Problems are the greatest enemy of this sector, so workers must strive to anticipate, troubleshoot, and resolve issues as fast and effectively as possible. Teamwork. Cooperating with and supporting colleagues is essential in most roles within this sector.

title: Why Failure Matters
transcript: ﻿1 Why Failure Matters? In our culture, we often celebrate achievements - good grades, awards, promotions, and shy away from discussing the many stumbles it took to get there. Yet, nearly every major success story involves a period of trial and error. Whether you look at athletes, inventors, business leaders, or artists, you'll notice that a willingness to fail is key to their growth. Think about learning to ride a bike. Most of us wobbled, crashed and scraped our knees a few times before we got the hang of it. If we had given up after the first fall, we'd have never mastered that new skill. Likewise, in your academic or professional life, a failed attempt might be the very thing that helps you master a subject or refine a strategy you'll use for years to come.

title: How "Why" Helps Career Planning
transcript: ﻿1 How "Why" Helps Career Planning. Asking why is especially important when we're talking about career planning, and choosing our education and training pathways. You not only need to know why you're interested in a particular career field, but you should understand the larger purpose behind that interest. That way you can connect to it and use it to keep yourself motivated over the long haul, because most jobs require a lot of time and commitment to learn how to do them properly. Some jobs may only take a few weeks to learn the basics. For example, to become a barista, you can learn how to make different coffee orders pretty quickly. But it may take months of practice to perfect every drink on the menu or to create detailed latte art! If your why behind wanting to be a barista is simply to get a part-time job in college, that's fine because you don't have to invest much time and effort to learn the basics. But there are other jobs that require several years of intense training and education. Such as becoming a licensed commercial pilot, which involves rigorous coursework, hundreds of flight hours, and intense exams. The longer and more difficult the training and education requirements, the more vital it is to have a strong reason why you're doing it. Otherwise, you might wake up one day and realize, "Hey, I don't want to do this for a living!" The bottom line is keep your why, your purpose or reason for pursuing a goal in mind often. Tell yourself regularly why that goal matters to you!

title: Why Ask Why?
transcript: ﻿1 Why Ask "Why"? What's the big deal if you don't do any of this? Does everyone really have to sit down and analyze their own behavior? No, but the more self-aware and intentional you are about things, the more likely you are to establish and reach amazing goals! Otherwise, you're just leaving things to chance. You don't have a why behind your actions, you're just letting things happen to you while you "go with the flow." That's not a recipe for success! Success rarely happens randomly or by accident. As Benjamin Franklin once said, "If you fail to plan, you are planning to fail." By the same token, if you don't have a good reason why you are doing something, maybe you should be doing something else. That's why you should always ask yourself why? Here's a visual example to think about. Imagine you're on a small boat on the Pacific Ocean, just you. You've got everything you need to go anywhere you want. Plenty of food and water, a compass, maps, and navigation equipment. You've even got an instruction manual explaining how to use everything! All you have to do is read it and practice. Oh, and you also need to know where you wanna go! In other words, a clear, well-defined goal. Now, you've got a couple of options. You can do nothing and drift aimlessly, letting the ocean tie take you wherever it wants. You may end up drifting for a long time before landing at a random destination, which may not be to your liking. Or you can take control, be the captain of your boat and set a course for the destination of your choice. If you do that, there's no guarantee you'll get where you want to go. But your odds of success are much higher than if you let the wind and sea determine your fate!

title: Defining Your Why
transcript: ﻿1 Defining Your Why. Sometimes our whys aren't clearly defined. That's normal! If you really think about it, sometimes we just do things without understanding the exact reason why. We all do that! For example, your friends might have influenced you to think about something a certain way. But if you really think about it and ask yourself why you believe that, there might not be a particular good reason, besides you were told so.
