If that ever happens, you should think deeply about it and form your own opinions based on your observations and experiences over the opinions of everyone else. Knowing our why is important, especially when it comes to how we want to spend the rest of our lives. When we are not aware of our reasons for doing things, it's like we're on automatic pilot doing things without thinking about them. Instead, we should figure out our whys and focus on them. For every why in your life that affects your future career, you need to identify a clear purpose behind it. That way you can work toward a fulfilling career that excites you and gives you a greater sense of meaning and purpose!

title: Focus on your Why
transcript: ﻿1 How "Why" Helps Career Planning. Asking why is especially important when we're talking about career planning, and choosing our education and training pathways. You not only need to know why you're interested in a particular career field, but you should understand the larger purpose behind that interest. That way you can connect to it and use it to keep yourself motivated over the long haul, because most jobs require a lot of time and commitment to learn how to do them properly. Some jobs may only take a few weeks to learn the basics. For example, to become a barista, you can learn how to make different coffee orders pretty quickly. But it may take months of practice to perfect every drink on the menu or to create detailed latte art! If your why behind wanting to be a barista is simply to get a part-time job in college, that's fine because you don't have to invest much time and effort to learn the basics. But there are other jobs that require several years of intense training and education. Such as becoming a licensed commercial pilot, which involves rigorous coursework, hundreds of flight hours, and intense exams. The longer and more difficult the training and education requirements, the more vital it is to have a strong reason why you're doing it. Otherwise, you might wake up one day and realize, "Hey, I don't want to do this for a living!" The bottom line is keep your why, your purpose or reason for pursuing a goal in mind often. Tell yourself regularly why that goal matters to you!

title: Taking Control of Your Inner Voice
transcript: ﻿1 Taking Control of Your Inner Voice. Your inner voice is extremely powerful, but in a way, it's a lot like an AI program like ChatGPT. ChatGPT and other generative AI programs say things based on what they've been programmed with. So if they're trained with a bunch of inaccurate information, they'll just recycle that stuff and their results won't be accurate, either. The same goes for your inner voice! We can't always control what goes in. But by practicing self-awareness, we can look back at the criticisms and even the compliments we've received to see how those inputs might influence our thoughts and attitudes about ourselves. In other words, we can try to filter out the negative inputs and reprogram ourselves to have a more positive outlook - a "growth mindset," as Dr. Carol Dweck calls it. It was Dr. Dweck who first explained how adopting such a mindset can radically change the way we respond to challenges, setbacks - and even our own success! This mindset lets us experience failure as an opportunity to learn, instead of letting it get us down. It allows us to feel self-compassion instead of bringing ourselves down. And it empowers us to develop the resilience we need to adapt to any situation and overcome any obstacle that gets in our way. In fact, with a growth mindset, we'll be more open to looking for challenges, because we know we can deal with them. Just like how our muscles need to be exercised so they can grow back bigger and stronger, we can expand our minds and capabilities when we mentally push ourselves to do difficult things. But it all starts with taking control of your inner voice and removing those self-limits, so your mind can be free to grow!

title: Who's in Charge of Your Inner Voice
transcript: ﻿1 Who Is In Charge of Your Inner Voice? We all have an inner self that controls how we think and feel about ourselves. For some, it's a type of inner voice. For others, it's more of a feeling than something that relies on actual words, such as a sense of self-esteem. Either way, our inner self is an important part of our internal identity. But, interestingly enough, we aren't the only ones who created it! That inner self is a mix of our own unique thoughts and things we've been told by others since the day we were born. You've probably had someone criticize something you do! It's normal for that criticism to get stuck in your brain somewhere influencing your inner voice, whether you realize it or not. And that might manifest itself as negative self-talk or feelings, establishing limits for you. Setting up limits to your ability to believe in yourself. Limits you may be afraid to challenge. For example: let's go back to the Einstein quote from Chapter 1. Einstein said, "Everyone is a genius. But if you judge a fish by its ability to climb a tree, it will live its whole life believing that it is stupid." Imagine that's you, that "little fish." Would you want someone else's criticism to influence your inner self? No way! And in reality, no one is "stupid." Everyone has their own strengths. So if you were this little fish, would you doubt yourself because you can't climb a tree? Would you let that make you afraid to try new things? No way! The criticism wasn't even valid in the first place! Now let's examine another way that others can impact your thoughts. This one might sound innocent enough, but even though it seems positive, it can still have unintended consequences. Let's say you've received a steady stream of compliments your whole life about something you do well. Sounds nice, right? But your inner voice might overemphasize that one aspect, while ignoring others like skills you could be developing further. For example: someone who has always been praised over their athletics might prioritize that over their academics. Ultimately, they might come to rely on external validation to maintain their confidence while neglecting their personal growth. This could limit their career opportunities later.

title: Do you have a Growth Mindset?
transcript: ﻿1 Developing A Growth Mindset. Do you have a growth mindset? If that term isn't familiar, it basically means when someone believes in their heart that they can accomplish anything if they put their mind to it! Picture this, you are in math class, struggling with a tough problem. In that moment, there are two common types of thoughts that might run through your head. One type is sort of negative, like, I'm not good at math. I'll never understand this. I hate math. Those kinds of thoughts are common and normal. They're just not very helpful if you want to pass that math class. The other type of thought is more adaptable, like "Let me ask the teacher to explain this concept again" or "I should try to look at this problem differently." See the difference? The first type of thought starts with doubting yourself. It's negative and doesn't help solve the problem at all. But the second type is all about believing in yourself. You find it in you to come up with a way to help you solve the problem, leading to more positive results. It's natural if you start with the first one, but what's important is that you switch to the second one - the one where you believe in your ability to figure out the problem and overcome it. So let's talk more about what it means to believe in yourself and why that's so important in everything we do!

title: Own Your Future: Your Journey Begins Now!
transcript: ﻿1 Your Journey Begins Now. Exploring these options is about more than just choosing a career. It's about discovering who you are and what makes you thrive. Even if one option doesn't work out, it will teach you something valuable about yourself and open the door to new possibilities. This is your journey, and it's yours to shape. By choosing these three paths, you've already taken the first step towards discovering a career that fits your passions, motivations, and skills. Write down your three career options now, and carry them with you as you continue your journey into this course and beyond. They'll guide you through the activities ahead, helping you learn, refine, and imagine the possibilities. Remember, this is just the beginning. Every step you take will bring you closer to owning your future!

title: Own Your Future: Choose Your Exploration Paths
transcript: ﻿1 Your Next Step: Choose Your Exploration Paths. If you've already chosen three career paths earlier in this course, now is a great time to revisit them. Take a moment to reflect on those options and think about how they align with your passions and motivations. Have any new ideas or insights come to mind? If you haven't chosen your three career options yet, don't worry - you can still do that now. Think of three possibilities that excite you and inspire curiosity. As you move through the rest of this course, keep these career paths in mind. Use them as a lens to evaluate new information and activities. You might stick with them, refine them, or discover entirely new options along the way. Each step brings you closer to understanding what truly excites and you.

title: Own Your Future: What About You?
transcript: ﻿1 What About You? Ask yourself: What gets you excited? What activities make you lose track of time? Take a moment to dream big. Imagine your ideal workday: Where are you? What are you doing? Who are you working with or helping? Now, visualize how it feels to be in that moment. Visualizing your dream career is the first step to making it real.

title: Reframing Change as Opportunity
transcript: ﻿1 Reframing Change As Opportunity. Change is hard, but it's also exciting. Every twist and turn in your career is a chance to learn, grow, and get closer to what truly drives you. So what about you? How will you embrace the twists and turns of your own journey? Start by focusing on what you can control. Invest in Your Skills. Your skills are your most valuable asset. Keep learning, whether through on-the-job experiences, courses, or hobbies. Luca did just that when he transitioned into EdTech. Diving into the K through 12 world to understand student needs, and create impactful materials. The more you invest in yourself, the more equipped you'll be to tackle any challenge. Stay Open to Possibilities. Career paths aren't always straight lines, and the unexpected detours can lead to incredible opportunities. Lucas shift from the film industry to the education sector wasn't planned, but by staying open, he discovered a new way to apply his skills and passions. Remember, a change in direction isn't the end - it's often the start of something amazing! Trust the Process. Every experience, whether a success or a challenge is helping you grow. Lucas's years in film production taught him skills like teamwork, problem-solving, and managing deadlines. Skills that became invaluable in his new career. Trust that each step, even the difficult ones, is shaping you into the professional you're meant to be. Your career path doesn't have to be linear - it just has to be yours. By embracing uncertainty, leaning into your transferable skills and staying open to new opportunities, you'll be ready to navigate whatever comes next. Remember, the road to success isn't about avoiding detours - it's about finding the beauty in the journey!

title: Fail, Learn, and Rise Up Again!
transcript: ﻿1 Why Failure Matters? In our culture, we often celebrate achievements - good grades, awards, promotions, and shy away from discussing the many stumbles it took to get there. Yet, nearly every major success story involves a period of trial and error. Whether you look at athletes, inventors, business leaders, or artists, you'll notice that a willingness to fail is key to their growth. Think about learning to ride a bike. Most of us wobbled, crashed and scraped our knees a few times before we got the hang of it. If we had given up after the first fall, we'd have never mastered that new skill. Likewise, in your academic or professional life, a failed attempt might be the very thing that helps you master a subject or refine a strategy you'll use for years to come.

title: Different Pathways in the Sector
transcript: ﻿1 Different Pathways in the Sector. The four main career pathways to enter this sector are Restaurants and Food Beverage Services, Lodging, Travel and Tourism, and Recreation, Amusements and Attractions. What are Restaurants and Food/ Beverage Services? Restaurant and food beverage services cover all aspects of food and beverage experiences for customers, from cooking and serving to hosting and cleanup. Careers in this pathway include Baker, Bartender, Caterer, Executive Chef, Kitchen Steward, and Restaurant Server. What is the Lodging Pathway? Lodging workers assist with making reservations for accommodations, checking-in, explaining services, and ensuring guests have a safe and comfortable visit. Careers include Bell Captain, Concierge, Food and Beverage Manager, Laundry Attendant, Lodging Manager, and Valet Attendant. What is Travel and Tourism? Travel and tourism professionals may perform a wide range of duties such as selling vacation packages or arranging big events. They help ensure tourists have safe, enjoyable, and memorable experiences. Careers include Convention Services Manager, Director of Tourism Development, Events Manager/Planner, Interpreter, Transportation Specialist, and Travel Agent. What is the Recreation Amusements and Attractions Pathway? Workers in this pathway support operations at recreational facilities such as concert venues, sports arenas, amusement parks, museums, and other types of attractions. Careers include Club Personnel, Gaming and Casino Manager, Historical /Cultural/Architectural/Ecological Guide, Museums/Zoos/Aquariums Personnel, Parks and Gardens Ranger, and Ticket Vendor.

title: Industry Trends
transcript: ﻿1 Industry Trends. There are many unique trends affecting the hospitality and tourism sector. For instance, many travelers look for environmentally friendly vacation options like eco-lodges, carbon-neutral travel, and responsible wildlife tours. Experiential tourism is another hot trend. Travelers want meaningful, immersive, authentic experiences, which can include volunteerism, hands-on classes, and exciting cultural immersion packages. Evolving technologies are affecting several areas of the sector. From digital marketing and online booking to augmented reality experiences, personalized mobile app functions, AI chatbots, and data-driven corporate decision-making. Curious about which pathways might match your personality and interest in this sector? Then let's take the Gladeo Quiz!

title: Overview
transcript: ﻿1 Welcome. We will be learning about the National CTE Sector, Human Services. The Human Services sector covers numerous different areas ranging from child and family services to mental health counseling, community programs, personal finance matters, fitness trainers, and even hairstyling. Because of the wide range of career options available, this sector appeals to many people. There are jobs for virtually anyone who has an interest in advocacy, promoting healthier lifestyles, teaching life skills, or just helping people in general. The five main career pathways in this sector are Early Childhood Development and Services, Counseling and Mental Health Services, Family and Community Services, Personal Care Services, and Consumer Services. Common job titles include Preschool Teacher, Mental Health Counselor, Barber, Skincare Specialist, Banker, and Small Business Owner. "The human services sector is a vital part of our economy, providing jobs and opportunities for millions of people. It is also essential to our social fabric, helping to create a more just and equitable society.", says Marian Wright Edelman, Founder of the Children's Defense Fund.

title: Public vs Private
transcript: Public versus Private Colleges You'll notice that like K-12 schools, some colleges are public and others are private. The main difference is their primary source of funding. Public colleges receive significant funding from the federal government and their respective states, leading to lower tuition for in-state students. Private colleges rely more heavily on tuition, donations and endowments typically resulting in higher tuition. However, private schools sometimes offer better scholarship opportunities. Now, let's review some of the terminologies we touched on previously.

title: How to Apply to Apprenticeships
transcript: How to Find and Apply to Community College Before Applying Applying to a community college is usually a straightforward process. The tricky part is choosing the right one with the right courses for your goals. That's why it's so helpful to know your end goal in advance. Do you want to earn a certificate or associate? Do you intend to transfer to a four year college or university? Do you plan to go full-time or part-time? Do you want to attend in person, online, or both via a hybrid class or program? Note, some courses require in-person attendance, so students can gain real world skills. Knowing your purpose, schedule, and manner of attendance is key to selecting the right school. Once you're ready, it's time to look at your local community colleges. Find out if they offer programs that align with your interests and career goals. If you plan to attend online, you won't have to worry as much about where the school is located, but if it's in a different state, make sure to determine if they charge you in-state tuition or out-of-state tuition. Visit Campuses If you plan to attend in person, try to visit the campus to get a sense of the environment and facilities. Check if there are guided campus tour dates you can attend. Check Accreditation Make sure the community college is accredited because that can affect credit transferability and employability. Review Admissions Requirements Review the school's admissions criteria carefully, such as high school GPA requirements, placement tests for subjects like English or math, proof of residency, et cetera. Review Program Requirements Some programs may have requirements beyond those of the school itself. Make sure to read them carefully before applying. Financial Aid and Scholarships Apply for financial aid through the FAFSA process and search for scholarships or grants you might be eligible for including school sponsored scholarships. The Application Process Complete Application Carefully fill out the application form, which may be available online on the college's website. For entries that require long responses, such as essay questions, we suggest writing them out on a separate document, editing and spell checking, then cutting and pasting them into the online form. At the time of submission, there's usually an application fee you must pay. In some cases, this can be waived, but check with the school. Submit Transcripts and Other Materials Request your official high school transcripts or equivalents, and have them sent to the college's admissions office by the listed deadline. Don't wait until the last minute to order them because it can take time for official transcripts to be prepared and mailed out. Upon Acceptance Attend Orientation New student orientation is a helpful way to get acquainted with campus resources and policies, so try to attend. Meet with an Advisor Schedule an appointment with your academic advisor to discuss your educational plans and get help with suitable course selection. Take Placement Tests If the school requires placement tests prior to registering for classes, be sure to knock those out as soon as you can, but leave enough time to study! Register for Classes Once accepted and advised, register for classes in accordance with your academic plan. Buy Textbooks and Supplies When you register, you should get details about the textbooks or supplies you'll need. Many students buy used books or rent them versus buying them, but be sure to select the correct book additions. Pay Tuition and Fees Now it's time for everyone's favorite part, paying tuition and fees. Schedule an appointment with the financial aid office if you need assistance, such as ensuring scholarship funds are flowing smoothly, or if you need to establish a payment plan. Be careful not to miss the payment deadlines. Prepare for the First Day The first day of classes is always an exciting and sometimes stressful time. Plan ahead so you'll feel more relaxed. Make sure your schedule is organized, your commute to class is laid out, and you have everything you'll need, such as a notebook, pen, charged laptop, et cetera.

title: Types of Apprenticeships
transcript: Types of Apprenticeships Registered Apprenticeships These programs are formally recognized by the Department of Labor. They are always paid and include regular wage increases. Completing a registered apprenticeship earns a nationally recognized credential and potential college credit. The duration typically ranges from two to four years. Pre-Apprenticeships These are designed to prepare individuals for registered apprenticeship programs or other jobs. They may or may not be paid and are generally shorter than registered apprenticeships. Non-Registered Apprenticeships Programs aren't required to undergo the lengthy registration process with the Department of Labor. As a result, non-registered programs vary in quality and structure. Some are good, some may not be. They may not always be paid or lead to a certificate or job in the field. Be careful and do your research before applying to a non-registered program. Note, the short-lived industry recognized apprenticeship program was recently discontinued, but you may still see the term online.

title: Education Apprenticeships
transcript: Education Apprenticeships Education related apprenticeships for teachers include K-12 Teacher Skills learned: Develop coursework, prepare lesson plans, and instruct and assess students. Median annual salary, $57,600. Teacher's Aide Skills learned: Assist teachers, prepare materials, support student learning and manage classrooms. Median annual salary, $30,920. Early Childhood Educator Skills learned: Design educational programs, develop children's social and cognitive skills and maintain safe learning environments. Median annual salary, $35,330. As mentioned, apprenticeship opportunities also exist for jobs in advanced manufacturing, agriculture, construction, cybersecurity, energy, financial services, hospitality, telecommunications, and transportation. To learn more, visit www.apprenticeship.gov/apprenticeship-industries. Creative job related apprenticeships aren't currently listed on apprenticeship.gov, but you can find opportunities by searching job portals such as indeed.com. You may also find programs through search engine results, but pay attention to which country is hosting the program because many are in the United Kingdom.

title: Healthcare Apprenticeships
transcript: Healthcare Apprenticeships Healthcare related career field apprenticeships exist for roles such as certified nurse aide CNA. Skills learned: provide basic care to patients, assist with daily activities and measure vital signs. Median annual salary, $35,760. Emergency Medical Technicians / Paramedic Skills learned: respond to emergency calls, perform medical services and transport patients to facilities. Median annual salary, $39,410. Nurse (Registered nurse) Skills learned: provide and coordinate patient care and educate patients about health conditions. Median annual salary, $81,220. Medical Coder Skills learned: review patient information for pre-existing conditions, retrieve patient records and liaison between clinicians and billing offices. Median annual salary, $47,180. Dental Assistant Skills learned: prepare patients and equipment, assist dentists with routine procedures and keep records. Median annual salary, $44,820.

title: IT Apprenticeships
transcript: Apprenticeships Apprenticeship is a time honored method of practical learning that combines academic instruction with three to four years of supervised on-the-job training. The top benefit is that apprenticeships don't cost money, so there's no student debt. In fact, apprenticeships are paid employees earning wages as they undergo training, learn skills, and gain experience. Statistics show that while there's no guarantee, a high percentage of apprentices are offered a full-time job by their employer after the apprenticeship is complete. Even if the apprentice decides not to take the job, they've still gained highly valuable skills and expertise they can use in the job market. Numerous professions rely on this method to train workers to perform their duties independently. In some cases, students attend a trade school before beginning their formal apprenticeship, which can reduce the time it takes to finish.

title: Trade Apprenticeships
transcript: Trade Apprenticeships Trade related apprenticeships are still the most common. Career options include, but are not limited to, Automotive Technician. Skills learned: diagnose, repair, and perform maintenance on automobiles and light trucks. Median annual salary, $46,970. Carpenter. Skills learned: Construct and repair building frameworks and structures including stairways, door frames, partitions and rafters. Median annual salary, $51,390. Electrician. Skills learned: Install, maintain and repair electrical systems in buildings and other settings. Median annual salary, $60,240. Glazier. Skills learned: Cut and install glass in windows, skylights and fixtures in buildings and vehicles. Median annual salary, $48,720. HVAC technician. Skills learned: Install and repair heating and air conditioning systems. Median annual salary, $51,390. Masonry Worker. Skills learned: Lay and bind building materials such as brick, structural tile and concrete block, construct and repair walls, partitions, arches, and other structures. Median annual salary, $49,490. Millwright. Skills learned: install, dismantle, repair, reassemble and move machinery in factories, power plants and construction sites. Median annual salary, $61,750. Musical Instrument Repair. Skills learned: repair and restore musical instruments. Adjust parts for proper sound and functioning, tune instruments. Median annual salary, $43,120. Pipefitter/steamfitter Skills learned: Install and repair high pressure pipe systems used in manufacturing, electricity generation and heating and cooling buildings. Median annual salary, $60,090. Plumber. Skills learned: Install and repair pipes and fixtures for water, gas, steam, and other systems. Median annual salary, $60,090. Sheet Metal Worker Skills learned: Fabricate, assemble, install, and repair sheet metal products and equipment. Median annual salary, $55,350. Welder Skills learned: Welding techniques and skills for joining metal parts. Median annual salary, $47,540.

title: Pros and Cons
transcript: Pros and cons of going to community college. The pros of attending a community college depend on your goals and circumstances. Common benefits include affordability. Community college can be a good option for students who want to save money. Lower tuition reduces overall education costs and potential debt. Keep in mind, students who plan on transferring should make sure their credits will actually transfer. It doesn't save money if you have to retake courses. Flexibility. Community college students are often busy with work, family, or other obligations, so the flexibility of part-time and online classes is convenient. Also, community colleges offer a range of courses that allow students to explore different fields before committing to a specific area of study. Admissions Four year colleges and universities can be tough to get into, especially if your high school GPA isn't super competitive. It should be easier to get into a community college. Academic Improvement Classes at four year colleges can be academically challenging. Community college offers an easier transition. This helps students maintain better grades, making their GPA more competitive by the time they apply to transfer. Small Classes Four year college classes are often jam packed with students while community college classrooms are smaller. This allows for more personalized attention from faculty, which can help raise grades. Community Feel Community colleges often feel more inclusive because students tend to be from the local area, whereas universities may have students from other states or countries. Focused Support Services Many community colleges feature focused support in tutoring, academic counseling, and job seeking skills. Career Training Several programs are tailored to thoroughly prepare students for careers after graduation. They may also grant industry recognized credentials or prepare students for industry related exams required to work in a profession or to gain state licensure. Potential Downsides to Community College The potential cons to community college depend on the individual. For some students, there are no cons at all, but possible downsides include Class Availability Smaller classes fill up faster, so students may have to register early to get the classes they want. Limited Campus Life Community colleges don't offer the range of activities, clubs, and events that larger campuses might, but that's not always a negative thing, since it can help students focus on their academics. Fewer Degree Options Most community colleges don't offer degrees above the associates level. Some states have a few community colleges with career oriented bachelor's degree programs, but this is relatively rare and the major options are limited. In addition, no community colleges offer graduate programs. Post-Graduation Career Options Degree requirements vary by career and by employer. Many employers want applicants to hold a bachelor's even if the job duties don't necessarily require it. That said, many community college grads start working, gain some experience, then return to college later to finish a higher level degree. Transfer Issues If a community college student knows they plan to transfer, it is important to get the timing right. The process of transferring to a four year university could potentially prolong the time it takes to finish a bachelor's degree. Credit Transfer Issues Certain credits may not transfer, thus affecting degree completion time. Competitive schools may be less likely to accept a transfer application, so it's important to do your research ahead of time. Less Research Community colleges offer varying degrees of undergraduate research opportunities, but they are typically less funded or emphasized. Fewer Resources Compared to larger institutions, community colleges tend to have fewer facilities, IT resources and student services in general.

title: What is an Apprenticeship?
transcript: Welcome. Let's review our introduction to apprenticeships. What is an apprenticeship? As we mentioned in the previous module, an apprenticeship is a program that combines on-the-job training with academic instruction for someone learning a trade or profession. Put another way, "An apprenticeship is an industry driven high quality career pathway, where employers can develop and prepare their future workforce, and individuals can obtain paid work experience, classroom instruction, and a portable, nationally recognized credential." says apprenticeship.gov. Sometimes apprentices attend a trade school first to cut down the time it takes to finish the apprenticeship, which can last three to four years, depending on prior experience.

title: College Transfer Basics
transcript: College Transfer Basics College transfer applications are as rigorous as applying to college for the first time. So here are some tips to get ready. Assess your eligibility, such as GPA requirements, Pay attention to which college credits will transfer, and the maximum number of transfer credits the new school will accept. Consult with academic advisors and financial aid officers to discuss transfer implications and financial aid adjustments. Decide when you want to transfer and start the application process at least two semesters before that date. Note, some schools only accept transfers for the fall semester. Learn the transfer deadlines. They could differ from regular admission deadlines. Gather and submit the full list of transfer application materials, which typically include transcripts, letters of recommendation, a statement of good standing, an essay, SAT ACT scores, et cetera. Keep up your grades because schools reserve the right to resend your transfer acceptance if your GPA drops. If needed, withdraw from any classes you registered for at your old school before the deadline. On acceptance, verify transferred credits and follow the tips from the upon acceptance section we previously covered.

title: Finding & Applying
transcript: ﻿1 Finding Your Own Approach. Everyone's style of reflection is a little different, so experiment to see what works for you. Some people like a traditional diary format - writing a few paragraphs each night before bed. Others prefer bullet journaling, listing key points about their day, including obstacles and triumphs. You could also try recording voice memos if you're not a fan of writing or vlogging to capture your thoughts in the moment. The goal is to capture your experiences and insights in a way that resonates with you. It doesn't have to be daily or even weekly, as long as you're consistent enough to see a pattern over time. Let yourself be creative. Doodle, write poetry, or add photos and mementos. The important thing is that you're pausing to reflect and giving yourself credit for the challenges you've overcome.

title: Pros & Cons
transcript: Pros and cons of going to community college. The pros of attending a community college depend on your goals and circumstances. Common benefits include affordability. Community college can be a good option for students who want to save money. Lower tuition reduces overall education costs and potential debt. Keep in mind, students who plan on transferring should make sure their credits will actually transfer. It doesn't save money if you have to retake courses. Flexibility. Community college students are often busy with work, family, or other obligations, so the flexibility of part-time and online classes is convenient. Also, community colleges offer a range of courses that allow students to explore different fields before committing to a specific area of study. Admissions Four year colleges and universities can be tough to get into, especially if your high school GPA isn't super competitive. It should be easier to get into a community college. Academic Improvement Classes at four year colleges can be academically challenging. Community college offers an easier transition. This helps students maintain better grades, making their GPA more competitive by the time they apply to transfer. Small Classes Four year college classes are often jam packed with students while community college classrooms are smaller. This allows for more personalized attention from faculty, which can help raise grades. Community Feel Community colleges often feel more inclusive because students tend to be from the local area, whereas universities may have students from other states or countries. Focused Support Services Many community colleges feature focused support in tutoring, academic counseling, and job seeking skills. Career Training Several programs are tailored to thoroughly prepare students for careers after graduation. They may also grant industry recognized credentials or prepare students for industry related exams required to work in a profession or to gain state licensure. Potential Downsides to Community College The potential cons to community college depend on the individual. For some students, there are no cons at all, but possible downsides include Class Availability Smaller classes fill up faster, so students may have to register early to get the classes they want. Limited Campus Life Community colleges don't offer the range of activities, clubs, and events that larger campuses might, but that's not always a negative thing, since it can help students focus on their academics. Fewer Degree Options Most community colleges don't offer degrees above the associates level. Some states have a few community colleges with career oriented bachelor's degree programs, but this is relatively rare and the major options are limited. In addition, no community colleges offer graduate programs. Post-Graduation Career Options Degree requirements vary by career and by employer. Many employers want applicants to hold a bachelor's even if the job duties don't necessarily require it. That said, many community college grads start working, gain some experience, then return to college later to finish a higher level degree. Transfer Issues If a community college student knows they plan to transfer, it is important to get the timing right. The process of transferring to a four year university could potentially prolong the time it takes to finish a bachelor's degree. Credit Transfer Issues Certain credits may not transfer, thus affecting degree completion time. Competitive schools may be less likely to accept a transfer application, so it's important to do your research ahead of time. Less Research Community colleges offer varying degrees of undergraduate research opportunities, but they are typically less funded or emphasized. Fewer Resources Compared to larger institutions, community colleges tend to have fewer facilities, IT resources and student services in general.

title: Bridge to Four-Year College
transcript: Bridge to Four-Year College For many students, a community college is a convenient bridge to a four-year college. Some students use community college as a way to build up their GPA. A higher GPA can help students get into more selective four-year colleges and universities. Moving from community college to a higher level institution is called transferring because the student is transferring academic credits from their old school to the new one. However, not all community college credits are transferable. All too often, community college students take classes only to learn later that the four year college or university they want to go to will not accept all their credits. You don't want to have to retake courses. So to ensure credits transfers, students should: Verify that the community college is regionally accredited as credits from accredited institutions are more widely accepted. Regularly consult with academic advisors regarding your course selections and transfer plans. Check if the community college has an articulation or transfer agreement or pathway with a local public four year college. Research your target four year college or university, and review their transfer policies carefully. Reach out to the admissions office if anything is unclear. Consider focusing on general education courses that are more likely to transfer. If you have an undergraduate major in mind, choose courses that align with the requirements at the four year college. Maintain good grades. Most four year colleges only accept a transfer credit if a minimum grade has been earned. Keep records, of course, syllabi, descriptions, and transcripts. This documentation will be useful if the four year college needs to evaluate the content of your courses for transfer. Complete all necessary applications and paperwork for the transfer process by the required deadlines. Connect with other students who have successfully transferred to gather insights and tips. Note, some schools are more transfer friendly than others, but be careful. Certain for-profit schools and degree mills advertise themselves as transfer friendly because they don't put much emphasis on academic rigor. As a result, their degrees may not be as valuable. Community college students don't have to earn an associate degree to transfer. In fact, they can take as little as one term at a community college, then apply for transfer. They can stay in community college long enough to knock out their general education courses, some electives, and even courses in the major they intend to pursue at the next institution.

title: Using Gladeo to Find Education and Training
transcript: Using Gladeo to find career path education and training. The first step to using Gladeo to find out about the education and training requirements for any given career path is to take the Gladeo Quiz Personal Assessment. This fun and short quiz helps you discover your career related personality types. The personality types are drawn from the Holland Occupational Themes developed by psychologist John Holland. Like Dr. Holland, we believe one's choice of a career is an expression of personality. The six Holland personality types are realistic - doer or builder; investigative - thinker or problem solver; artistic - creator; social - helper or people person; enterprising - persuader; conventional - organizer. Remember, everyone possesses traits from more than one personality type. So the Gladeo quiz shows you your top three types, ranked with a percentage score. For example, 36% organizer, 20% creator, and 16% builder. Next, the Gladeo platform matches you to potential career options and industries based on your unique quiz results. From there, it's easy to browse careers by personality type or industry. Each in-depth career profile provides tips on what to look for in a college or program. Common suggestions for things to review include the cost of tuition, discounts, and local scholarship opportunities in addition to federal aid. Whether the program features on campus, online or hybrid programs, whether the school is accredited or not, whether the major program is accredited. Note, this is especially important for STEM fields, which should ideally be accredited by ABET. If the programs offer opportunities for internships or hands-on cooperative educational experiences. Depending on the field you study, the programs ties to local employers and recruiters, the program faculty's awards and accomplishments, the quality and modernity of their facilities, the funding of their research programs, the professionalism of affiliated centers and external partner institutes, graduation rates and graduate job placement statistics, the size of the alumni network and the scope of their activities, student reviews and college rankings, student clubs, organizations and services, career support services. Based on your searches, Gladeo provides you with regional based suggestions for educational, training and apprenticeship programs for careers. The descriptions include helpful details such as geographic location of program offerings, average completion times, degree types offered - certificate, associates, bachelor's, graduate, average tuition and fees, delivery formats - online, in person, hybrid, enrollment timeframes, age and educational prerequisites, work authorizations, residency eligibility, admission requirements. Many community colleges feature career program mappers, which Gladeo links to when available. We also show relevant high school career and technical education information for each program. Pathway coordinators and counselors can also easily search and filter programs by CTE, industry sector and CTE career pathway.

title: Military Programs
transcript: Military Programs Reserve Officers' Training Corps ROTC is a type of college program that prepares students to become officers in the US military. Participants are regular college students who take military science classes along with their other college classes. They also engage in physical training and ROTC related extracurricular activities. ROTC programs emphasize leadership, discipline, and physical fitness, preparing student cadets and MIDSHIP men for future roles as military officers. ROTC programs often offer scholarships to cover tuition and fees and provide allowances for books and living expenses. In return, students commit to serving in the military for a certain number of years after graduation. Military service academies are higher education institutions that prepare students for service in one of the branches of the armed forces. The four military service academies are The US Military Academy, AKA West Point for army officers, The US Naval Academy for Navy and Marine Corps Officers, the US Air Force Academy for Air Force and Space Force Officers, the US Coast Guard Academy for Coast Guard Officers. Each military academy features a rigorous curriculum covering military tactics, leadership skills, and physical training, along with standard academic courses. As with ROTC, graduates are commissioned as military officers and required to serve a certain number of years in their respective branches. In addition to the military service academies, there's also the civilian US Merchant Marine Academy.

title: Types of Credentials and Degrees
transcript: Types of credentials and degrees you can earn in college. Certificates Certificates are official recognitions provided by companies, colleges, and other organizations to verify that an individual has completed a brief educational program on a specific topic. They do not lead to a degree, but are useful for career advancement or specialization. Most certificate programs feature a selection of required courses plus a few relevant electives to choose from. Note, certificates are not the same as certifications. However, certificate courses can generally be used for college credit and be applied toward a degree. Associate's Degree An associates is a two year degree, often offered by community colleges covering foundational coursework in a particular field. It can stand alone or be a stepping stone to a Bachelor's. Bachelor's Degree A bachelor's is a four year undergraduate degree offering a comprehensive education in a specific major along with general education and various subjects. Common types are Bachelor of Arts, BA and Bachelor of Science BS. Master's Degree A Master's is considered a graduate degree because it is pursued after a bachelor's, often taking one to two years to complete. It involves advanced study in a specific field, culminating in a thesis or capstone project. Examples include Master of Arts, MA, Master of Science, MS, and Master of Business Administration, MBA, Doctor of Philosophy, PhD. A PhD is a research-focused graduate degree that typically takes several years to complete. It requires original research and a dissertation, which is a long detailed research paper, presenting original findings or analysis on a specific topic. PhDs are often pursued for careers in academia or research. Professional Degrees Professional degrees like Juris Doctor, JD, Doctor of Medicine, MD, or Doctor of Education, EdD, are geared towards specific professions. They usually involve practical training and internships and are necessary for certain careers like law, medicine, or higher level educational administration. Specialized Degrees Some fields require specialized degrees, such as Master of Fine Arts, MFA, Master of Public Health, MPH, or Doctor of Veterinary Medicine, DVM, each tailored to a niche professional area.

title: Additional Terminology
transcript: Additional Terminology You'll probably hear many different terms related to the world of post-secondary education. So let's break down a few of the most important ones. Accreditation is a rigorous and voluntary quality control process where authoritative organizations evaluate a school's curriculum, faculty, administration, and student services. Accreditation results can affect a school's reputation, the validity of their degrees to employers and student eligibility for federal financial aid. Liberal arts colleges emphasize well-rounded educational programs focused on developing intellectual abilities over technical or professional skills. Public and private colleges serve the same function, but are funded differently. As College Board explains, "Public colleges are funded by local and state governments and usually offer lower tuition rates than private colleges, especially for students who are residents of a state where a college is located. Private colleges rely on tuition, fees and non-government funding sources. Generous financial aid packages for students are often available thanks to private donations." Artsd design, film and music schools are similar to vocational schools, but dedicated to teaching the principles, techniques, and practices of creative disciplines. These can range from visual arts to fashion, filmmaking, music production, and many other artistic endeavors. Conservatories are specialized schools that focus on instruction and training in the performing arts, such as live music, acting, and dance. They are intensive learning environments devoted to cultivating the artistic talent and technical skills required for professional success. Some universities have conservatories on campus. For-profit colleges are educational institutions operated by private businesses. Many legitimate for-profit colleges offer accredited programs that meet educational standards and provide valuable degree options to students. But it's crucial to research their reputation before applying. As Forbes cautions, "They are not schools, as you may traditionally think of them. They are businesses that sell education and hope for a better life for a profit. Like all for-profit businesses, they exist to make money. If the school goes bankrupt, it can leave students in serious jeopardy." Degree mills offer degrees of little to no value. They are not accredited by any recognized accrediting organization, have virtually no admissions criteria, offer transfer credit for almost anything and promise fast, easy degrees with all coursework usually done online. Religiously affiliated schools are private institutions connected to particular religious faiths. Some incorporate religious activities and studies as part of their programs while others do not. Religious requirements should be listed in the admissions policies. Historically black colleges and universities or HBCUs are schools originally founded to serve black students. Though they are open for anyone to apply, they are recognized for their commitment to supporting black students to achieve academic and postgraduate success. Hispanic-serving institutions are schools where Hispanic students comprise at least 25% of the total enrollment. They are recognized for their commitment to supporting Hispanic students.

title: Choosing your Path After High School
transcript: Welcome. We will be learning about choosing your path after high school. High school students have several options for what they want to do after graduation. But don't wait until graduation to decide. The sooner you figure out what you wanna do, the sooner you can select an appropriate education and training pathway. With that in mind, let's explore some of the many post-secondary education and training options out there.

title: Certifications
transcript: Certifications Certifications are formal recognitions provided by organizations to verify that an individual has acquired specific skills and knowledge. Certifications are generally granted after the person passes a specific industry related exam, such as an IT certification exam. Certifications are not tied to a specific educational program, but if needed, exam takers can first take an optional course or use self-study materials to prepare. Licensure For some careers, workers must be certified and licensed before they can legally work. "Licenses are typically given by the state government after you undergo training and pass an exam." writes College Board. A license proves that you are qualified to work in a specific field. For example, you might get a state issued license to be an electrician.

title: Internships
transcript: Internships Internships are limited time work opportunities that many organizations offer to students or entry-level job seekers. Most are paid and many even provide school credit. Internships are a great way to gain hands-on experience and practical skills while making valuable connections in a field that interests you. They provide free training for you and free or inexpensive labor for the sponsoring organization. Sometimes internships can lead to full-time job offers.

title: For-Profit Colleges
transcript: An Overview of For-Profit Colleges "Students don't often go into the college going experience worried that the college is going to try to put one over on them." says Mamie Voight, policy Research VP at the Institute for Higher Education Policy. Unfortunately, this is exactly what can happen to students who attend for-profit colleges. For-profit colleges are operated by private owners to make money. "The market size measured by revenue of the for-profit universities industry was $12.4 billion in 2022." writes IBIS World. Virtually anyone can launch a for-profit college as a business and start raking in the dollars at the expense of the students. The quality of such schools can vary considerably. Some are accredited and offer valuable programs. Some are predatory degree mills, advertising easy admissions and easy degrees that may not be worth the paper they're printed on. Important factors to consider when evaluating a for-profit college's reputation include Accreditation. Verify whether the college is accredited by a legitimate agency. Financial Aid If a school isn't reputable, students may not qualify for many types of financial aid, scholarships or grants. Credit Transferability If a school isn't properly accredited or is considered a degree mill, other schools may not accept transfer credits. Ask the admissions office of the transfer school if they'd take credits from the for-profit school. Faculty Credentials Your educator's qualifications are important. Are they professors, associate or assistant professors, adjuncts or instructors? Look up faculty biographies and read about their work and accomplishments. Graduation and Job Placement Rates Does the college have a high graduation rate? If not, maybe students are dropping out for a reason. How are graduates employment outcomes? Does the school post strong job placement statistics for recent grads? If not, that could be a red flag. Note, predatory schools may use misleading statistics to artificially pump up their graduation or job placement rates. So don't rely only on these numbers. Program Quality Research the school's reputation by looking at national rankings, student reviews and alumni success stories. Talk to your counselors, career advisors and financial aid experts too. Search consumer review sites like the Better Business Bureau and check out state and federal court websites to see if the school has a history of legal issues such as claims of defrauding students. Student Services Does the school appear to offer robust, knowledgeable academic advising, career counseling and other support services? Or is it only offering minimal or superficial services? Certification and Licensure If your field of study requires certification or licensure, do the school's programs lead to or prepare graduates for those credentials? Make sure that any credentials the school lists are valid and actually recognized by employers. Costs Compare the cost of tuition and fees to those of public schools. For-profit college tuition rates may be noticeably higher. Marketing Do you keep seeing ads for the for-profit school making promises that sound too good to be true? Maybe they are. So don't take anything at face value. Remember, these schools are businesses and marketing is how to land customers. "For-profits spend $400 per student on commercial advertising compared to $14 per student by public institutions." notes Professor Stephanie Riegg Cellini of George Washington University. Financial Responsibility Scores Is the school financially sound or on the verge of going bankrupt? Check out the Department of Education's financial responsibility composite scores for the for-profit school you're curious about to see how it's doing. Default Rates on Student Loans If a school's graduates have high default rates on their student loans, that's a red flag. Maybe the graduates couldn't find good jobs after graduation. You can search for a school's cohort default rates via the National Student Loan Data System.

title: College Credits
transcript: College Credits Credits are a unit of measurement colleges use to calculate how much coursework students complete. Each class counts for a certain number of credits. Most undergraduate classes count for three semester credits or four to five quarter credits if the school's on a quarter system. Generally speaking, the number of credits reflects how many hours of weekly classroom instruction that class will involve. So at a semester school, a three credit class would mean you'd attend that class for three hours a week. At a quarter school, a 4.5 credit class might mean 4.5 hours of classroom time. Note, this doesn't count the amount of homework and study you'll do outside of class. Educators suggests that for every hour in class, a student can expect to spend two to three hours studying and doing schoolwork outside of class.

title: Full-time vs Part-time
transcript: Full-time versus Part-time At the undergraduate level, a full-time student must be enrolled in at least 12 credits per semester term, which equates to four three credit courses, for example. However, 12 credits per term isn't enough to finish a bachelor's in four years. A bachelor's degree requires at least 120 semester credits to complete. So to graduate in four years, a student should take 15 credits per semester unless they attend summer classes. Thus, as you can see, a student who is technically full-time would take five years to finish a bachelor's if they're only taking 12 credits per term. Part-time students take less than a full course load of classes. A part-time student may only take one or more classes per term, but must pay attention to the school's maximum timeframe allowed to complete degree requirements. Each college sets its own policies regarding the maximum timeframe for degree completion. Students receiving federal financial aid should be careful not to exceed this maximum timeframe, or they will fail to meet satisfactory academic progress requirements and could lose their financial aid eligibility unless they qualify for an extension.

title: Introduction to Marketing Yourself
transcript: How to write your LinkedIn profile's About section. LinkedIn is a fantastic platform for professional networking. It's a valuable tool for promoting yourself and getting the attention of potential employers, clients, customers, or followers. But your profile has to capture people's attention. It must present your experiences in a way that compels others to reach out and connect with you. LinkedIn profiles features several elements, including an about section where you can write anything you want. This critical section allows up to 2,600 characters worth of text. That gives you roughly 500 words to play around with, which is more than enough if you keep things clear and concise. So let's review some tips about writing an amazing about section for your LinkedIn profile.

title: Nutrition
transcript: ﻿1 Nutrition. Our bodies are like machines that need fuel to keep going. The better the fuel, the better they'll respond. Unfortunately, these days most of us eat a lot of highly processed food filled with salt, sugar, preservatives, dyes, and unhealthy fats. It may be convenient, but such food items may do little to fuel your body properly. In fact, that stuff might be doing more harm than good, robbing you of the energy it's supposed to be giving you! Instead of providing the nutrients your body needs, processed foods can lead to sluggishness, poor concentration, and long-term health issues. They're usually packed with empty calories that fill you up temporarily, but pretty soon you're hungry again because they didn't really fuel you up at all. That's why, to recover from exercise and build stamina, you've got to fill up on fresh, nutrient-dense foods. Like fruits, vegetables, lean proteins, whole grains, and healthy fats. These foods also give you essential vitamins and minerals that boost immunity and keep your energy level steady - instead of them crashing after a sugar rush. Our brains also need the right fuel to stay alert and focused. But most of the fast or processed foods and drinks we consume, eventually drain our mental energy, cause mood swings and make it hard to concentrate and stay productive. Some of them, like so-called energy drinks, might give you a temporary boost because of the sugar and caffeine. But that artificial boost is often followed by a hard crash. These drinks can also dehydrate you. And since your brain is about 75% water, even mild dehydration can cause brain cells to shrink, affecting how they operate. So do your brain a favor! Keep it sharp with plenty of water and healthy foods like leafy greens, nuts, fatty fish, whole grains, and antioxidant-rich fruits. These choices support cognitive function, improve memory, and help regulate mood - keeping you mentally energized in a healthier, more sustainable way. Building both physical and mental stamina takes time, but the benefits are worth it. With improved stamina, you'll find yourself better equipped to handle any curveball life throws at you!

title: Resting
transcript: ﻿1 Resting. To build stamina, you've also got to know how to recover properly. Exercise does microscopic damage to your muscle fibers and connective tissue. The recovery phase is when your body does, its adapting to all that damage! Your muscles repair themselves and rebuild stronger so they can deal with what you do to them next time. As you can imagine, getting at least 8 hours of sleep is important to a healthy routine. Do your best to establish good sleeping habits by going to bed on time, avoiding caffeine when you can, and winding down before you go into rest mode. A relaxing nighttime routine can help you wind down, but study show that screen time before bed can disrupt your sleep cycle. So grab a book, do some journaling, or listen to some calming music instead of watching TV or scrolling through social media. Your mind needs rest to recover, the same as your body does. Restful sleep improves mental stamina by letting your brain kick back for a few hours. That way you can wake up refreshed, able to focus better, regulate your emotions better, and make better decisions! Quality rest also improves memory, boosts mood, and enhances cognitive performance. So make sure to establish a relaxing wind down routine before bed so you can get in a full eight hours of sleep! If you have a bit of insomnia, try drinking herbal teas such as lavender or chamomile, which have been shown to support relaxation and sleep.

title: Exercising
transcript: ﻿1 Exercising. Engaging in aerobic exercises like going on walks, working out, swimming, riding a bike, playing sports, or even just playing a video game that requires motion control are great ways to build endurance and physical stamina. Meanwhile, exercises like weight lifting or other types of resistance training can build strength, which is useful for many jobs. Some jobs like being a Firefighter, require both stamina and strength. Having the right fitness routine for such jobs could be a deciding factor on whether you'll succeed in that career field. As far as "exercising" your mind, activities that challenge your brain include playing board games, listening to podcasts and audio books, learning a new language, having an intellectual discussion with a friend, or thinking critically about a subject. You can also "stress proof" your mind by practicing deep breathing and mindfulness exercises. For example, if you're working on a complex project or preparing for a big presentation, take a few minutes to get away and clear your mind by just focusing on your breathing. If you have enough time, meditation and yoga are also proven methods of reducing stress, improving focus and attention, and giving your willpower a boost!

title: Ways to Build Physical and Mental Stamina
transcript: ﻿1 Building Physical and Mental Stamina. Our daily lives can be very tiring sometimes. Too much studying at school, too many hours at work, or too much stress in general can make our minds and bodies feel fatigued. In many cases, a short break is enough for us to recover. But sometimes, we might need a couple of days or even longer. And then there are even some situations, like finals week or "crunch time" at work, we need even longer to rest and recharge. In this chapter, we talk a lot about resilience. For this part, let's talk about a different kind of resilience. Your physical health and stamina, what they are, how they can help you, and ways to develop them!

title: What Exactly is Stamina
transcript: ﻿1 What Exactly Is Stamina? Miriam Webster defines stamina as, the bodily or mental capacity to sustain a prolonged stressful effort or activity. Since stressful activities can affect both your body and mind, we need both physical and mental stamina to deal with them. Physical activities include things like using a standing desk to give yourself more time on your feet during long work hours, working out at the gym, or even spending a lot of time doing physical activity in your job. Careers that are particularly physically active include jobs and industries like construction, medicine, agriculture, or hospitality and tourism. These types of roles require long periods of activity with tasks like heavy lifting or being on your feet. Having enough stamina allows these workers to do their jobs safely so they can avoid mishaps. Mental activities include things like solving complex problems, studying for exams, or dealing with upset customers. Sometimes even things you love to do can give you mental strain, like playing video games. You've probably experienced something like this in your academic, personal, or professional life. It's a different kind of stress, but just like your body, it's something you can exercise. Some jobs that require a lot of mental stamina include jobs in industries like healthcare, finance, technology, and emergency services. These types of jobs involve multitasking, solving problems, and dealing with people in stressful situations. Stamina helps these workers keep their minds focused so they can stay strong and avoid burnout! Case study. Consider air traffic controllers. Their job is to safely coordinate aircraft flight paths and respond to emergencies. Even a tiny error can have major and potentially disastrous consequences, so their work requires intense concentration, quick decision-making, and the ability to stay calm under pressure. Note, a lot of jobs require workers who can tolerate both physical and mental stress at the same time! Examples include group fitness coaches, stage actors, and critical care nurses.

title: Mindfulness Break: Building Physical and Mental Stamina
transcript: ﻿1 Building Physical and Mental Stamina. Our daily lives can be very tiring sometimes. Too much studying at school, too many hours at work, or too much stress in general can make our minds and bodies feel fatigued. In many cases, a short break is enough for us to recover. But sometimes, we might need a couple of days or even longer. And then there are even some situations, like finals week or "crunch time" at work, we need even longer to rest and recharge. In this chapter, we talk a lot about resilience. For this part, let's talk about a different kind of resilience. Your physical health and stamina, what they are, how they can help you, and ways to develop them!

title: Meet Rafael - Family Medicine
transcript: 还有什么你想知道但我今天没问到 ？ 嗯，我 只想说，医学作为一个整体，是一个非常有价值 职业。0 你可以从事医学的各个方向。0 可以从事病人护理， 你可以担任领导职务 你可以将领导和病人护理结合起来，或者 你可以只担任行政职务 还有专业，你可能，家庭 医学，初级保健 不适合你，但还有其他所有专业 都非常有价值， 也很酷 。 如果不是作为一名医生，那么作为一名医生的 助理或私人助理或执业护士， 这些都是其他不错的领域，而且，它们是需要的， 就像我说的， 这个国家缺少这样的人才， 所以想要让更多的人从事初级 或家庭医学或一般医学

title: Meet 3 Media Planners
transcript: ﻿1 Why is it important? The Arts, Media and Entertainment sector continues to transform the ways we express ourselves, how we spend our free time, and how we consume news and other media. It enables creators and audiences to engage with art, media and entertainment in unprecedented ways. By having instant access to content and the ability to share with anyone in the world. As former Secretary of State, Hillary Clinton stated, "The arts are not just about entertainment. They are about education, inspiration, and social change."

title: Apprenticeships
transcript: Information Technology Apprenticeships Some information technology related career fields are starting to use apprenticeships such as Computer Technician Skills learned: diagnose and repair computer hardware and software issues. Perform maintenance and install software. Median annual salary, $59,660. Software Developer Skills learned: design, develop and test applications. Use programming languages and collaborate with development teams and clients. Median annual salary, $127,260. Network Administrator Skills learned: install support and manage networks and computer systems to keep information flowing. Median annual salary, $90,520. Cybersecurity Analyst Skills learned: Protect systems from cybersecurity threats. Monitor networks for security breaches and respond to attacks. Median annual salary, $112,000.

title: Case Studies
transcript: Case studies. In today's fast paced world where trends change rapidly and technology is constantly evolving, most career paths require constant upskilling. By learning new skills, you can stay ahead of the curve and become an even more valuable asset to your company. Let's review three short case studies of professionals in very different career fields to see what new skills they're learning. Example number one, digital marketer. When digital marketing first emerged, the following skills were essential for digital marketers: content creation and writing, SEO, PPC advertising, email marketing, and social media marketing. But as technology and trends have evolved, today's digital marketers must acquire new skills such as influencer marketing, Web3, live streaming, chatbots, and data-driven marketing. They have to continuously upskill to keep up with the latest technologies. Example number two, welder. Welding is a skilled trade that requires knowledge of techniques such as arc welding, gas welding, MIG metal, inert gas, and tig, tungsten inert gas welding, and flux cord arc welding. But the field is advancing rapidly to include more automation, robotic welding, laser welding, and other advanced processes like friction stir, electron beam, and plasma arc welding. To stay competitive, experienced welders can greatly benefit from upskilling to learn about these new processes. Example number three, human resources professional. Human resources professionals need strong interpersonal skills along with a deep understanding of employment laws and employee compensation and benefits. But even though this is a relatively non-technical career field, HR professionals must continually adapt to new technologies such as human resource information systems that help organizations streamline processes. If HR workers can't adapt to and learn how to use emerging systems, they may fail to address changing trends and priorities and end up falling behind.

title: Soft Skills Intro
transcript: Soft skills. Soft skills are just as important as hard skills, but more subjective and harder to measure. Soft skills sometimes called interpersonal or people skills are personal attributes and qualities that help us interact effectively with others. For example, soft skills empower us to adapt to new situations such as starting a new school or job. They help us effectively navigate the challenges of life and work. There is no universally agreed upon list of all soft skills, but a few of the most common are adaptability, analytical, collaboration, communication, continuous learning, critical thinking, customer service, emotional intelligence, flexibility, leadership, problem solving, research, sales, self-motivation, teamwork, time management. For the most part, our education systems are primarily designed to teach hard skills while soft skills are developed along the way. However, soft skills can and should be intentionally developed because we use them all the time. For example, no matter what job or occupation you're in, you're going to need at least half of the soft skills we just mentioned. In fact, most of us use soft skills like communication, flexibility, problem solving, and time management every day of our lives at home, at school, on the job, and everywhere else.

title: Hard Skills Intro
transcript: Hard skills, what they are, why you need them, and how to get them. There are too many hard skills to name them all now, but some of the most in-demand technical skills include Artificial Intelligence Development, developing computer systems to do tasks that previously required human intelligence. Computer Aided Design Proficiency, using software to create precision technical illustrations. Cybersecurity Implementation, protecting computers and networks from unauthorized access. Data analysis, the ability to collect and interpret statistical data. Deep Learning Techniques, creating neural networks that learn from fast amounts of data. Digital Marketing, using online platforms to promote products or services. Electrical engineering, developing electrical systems and components. Graphic design, creating visual communications using typography and imagery. Machine Learning Algorithm Design, designing algorithms that allow computers to learn from data. Natural Language Processing Implementation, developing algorithms for computers to understand and generate human language. Programming, writing computer code using programming languages like Python and Java. Robotics Engineering, building and programming robots to perform autonomous tasks. Popular non-technical hard skills include skills like: Cooking, the ability to prepare food by combining, mixing and heating ingredients. Copywriting, writing compelling text for advertising and other marketing purposes. Event planning, organizing and managing events including logistics, budgeting and execution. First aid, providing basic medical care in emergencies. Gardening, cultivating plants and maintaining outdoor or indoor gardens. Photography, capturing images with an understanding of framing, lighting and perspective. Project Management, planning, executing and closing projects. Foreign Language Proficiency, the ability to speak, read and write in additional languages. Public speaking, communicating to an audience in a clear, engaging manner. There are several ways to learn technical skills. Depending on the subject matters, students can learn through high school courses or extracurricular activities, local community college or vocational school classes, or university courses. Some subjects are even taught at community centers, nonprofits, or other local organizations. Volunteering, internships, part-time jobs, summer programs, pre-apprenticeships and cooperative educational experiences are also proven ways to pick up certain specialized technical skills through hands-on practice. For those Do-It Your Selfers, with enough commitment and discipline, most technical skills can be learned through self-study, via books, videos, standalone online courses, and a lot of practice. Popular learning platforms such as Udemy, Coursera, edX, Khan Academy, Skillshare, Kajabi, Masterclass, and LinkedIn Learning are just a few of the countless sites offering free or affordable online courses, many of which were created by top ranking universities like MIT. For those who need personalized assistance, private tutoring lessons are another great option. Or if one-on-one lessons are too expensive, consider group lessons which can actually make learning more fun and engaging.

title: Introduction: The Importance of Skills and Lifelong Learning
transcript: The importance of developing skills and engaging in lifelong learning. The terms job, occupation, and career are often used interchangeably, even though they are not the same things. Your career covers the entire time that you work from your first job until your last. Your occupation is the line of work you're in, such as being an engineer, doctor, or musician. Your job is the specific thing you currently do for a living within your occupation. Employers hire workers to do jobs. Most people work many jobs within their occupation over the span of their careers. Some people change their occupations altogether, altering their career trajectory. One thing jobs and occupations have in common is they all require skills. There are two skills categories, hard skills and soft skills.

title: Scholarship Search Engines
transcript: Popular Scholarship Search Engines Students can quickly locate scholarships they might be eligible for by using scholarship finder websites. Some of the most popular include BigFuture, bold.org, Chegg, FastWeb Niche, scholarships.com, universities.com. Don't forget to check your college's financial aid office and departmental websites too. They often list scholarships specific to your institution and field of study. Student Loans Borrowing money via student loans might seem like an easier way to get funding fast, but they have to be paid back, which can create long-term debt. Also, the terms and interest rates can be complicated. The two main types are federal and private loans. Federal student loans are backed by the government and typically offer lower interest rates and more flexible repayment options than private loans. The main types are direct subsidized loans designed for undergraduate students with demonstrated financial need, direct unsubsidized loans, which are not based on need and any student can apply for and direct PLUS loans for parents of dependent undergraduates and graduate or professional students. Note, for direct subsidized loans, the Department of Education pays the interest on the loan if the student is enrolled at least half time. Private loans are offered by banks, credit unions, and other financial institutions. Interest rates can be higher than federal loan rates, plus they may start accruing interest while the borrower is still in college. Private loans require a credit check, so students without established credit need a co-signer. Paying Your Own Way Part-time jobs are a common way for students to at least partially cover expenses. However, it's vital to keep up with academics. If grades slip because of work, students may have to retake classes and end up paying even more. Some students delay college or take a gap year and go to work first to save money and then use their savings to fund their education. Internships allow students to work part-time in roles related to their field of study gaining valuable experience. The main downside is that not all internships pay, and if they do, the pay scale is usually low. Work Study programs offer part-time employment to students with financial needs. The jobs are often related to the student's course of study or community service. Parents.
