Parents can sometimes save enough to pay for their kids to go to college, but this requires years of planning and it is not an option for everyone. A 529 College savings plan is a popular investment option and is usable at most colleges. Withdrawals are tax free when used for qualified educational expenses like tuition, room and board, and books. Keep in mind that 529s invest in mutual funds and other financial products, so there's always the possibility of losing money. A prepaid 529 plan allows parents to lock in current tuition rates at specific state colleges, saving money when tuition goes up. Prepaid 529s are less flexible, but less risky than traditional 529s. However, there's still some risk if payments aren't guaranteed by the plan sponsor. Also, funds can't be used for room and board. Lastly, if the beneficiary decides not to go to a participating college, the plan's value may be worth less at a non-participating school. Education Savings Accounts ESAs are another tax advantaged way to save for education expenses. These are basic trusts or custodial accounts created to pay qualified education expenses for a beneficiary, but the maximum contribution is $2,000 per year. Other ways parents can help: just save money in the bank or invest in whatever options they choose without using a 529 plan. Take out a loan themselves or co-sign a loan with their student. Work side by side with their student who is applying for scholarships and grants. Provide their student with the necessary financial paperwork to submit for the FAFSA each year. Hire a college consultant to advise their student. Transfer employer or military educational benefits to their student when applicable. Employer Tuition Reimbursement Tuition reimbursement is an employee benefit offered by many employers. It's a great way for companies to invest in their workforce by helping to pay for classes. In general, the worker pays upfront, then submits a reimbursement form after finishing the class. Each company has its own eligibility policies and amounts vary. Some might only reimburse classes that are related to the employee's career or industry, while others reimburse almost any class. Military Tuition Assistance, Scholarships and the GI Bill. The military has a long tradition of offering educational benefits as an incentive to join the Army, Navy, Air Force, Space Force, Marine Corps, or Coast Guard. Military tuition assistance is available for most active service members and covers tuition and fees for courses taken during off-duty hours, up to a certain maximum amount each year. The GI Bill is a separate benefit that covers tuition and fees up to certain amounts, and provides a housing allowance and a stipend for books. It is mainly for veterans who served and then separated from the military. Though eligible active members can use it if they run out of tuition assistance. In many cases, unused GI Bill benefits can be transferred to one's spouse or children. In terms of scholarships, college students can apply to a Reserve Officer's Training Corps, ROTC program, located at hundreds of colleges around the US. Many ROTC students receive a scholarship along with a stipend for books and living expenses in exchange for a certain term of service as a military commissioned officer after graduation. Another option is applying to a military service academy, such as West Point, the Naval Academy, or the Air Force Academy. These highly competitive institutions are tuition free and include room and board in exchange for a term of service as a commissioned officer in the military. Additional Options Some educational institutions offer income share agreements where students agree to pay a percentage of their future income for a set period after graduation, in exchange for funding their education. Payments go up or down based on income. Income driven repayment plans are for federal student loans. Monthly loan payment changes based on your income and family size. If you earn less, you pay less. It is less common, but using crowdfunding platforms like GoFundMe can be used to raise funds for education. This method makes it convenient to reach a wider network of family, friends, and even strangers. Loan forgiveness programs may be used to cancel federal student loan debt for those who work in public service roles such as teachers, nurses, social workers, and law enforcement or who meet other criteria. In addition to scholarships and grants, there may be other state specific financial aid programs in your state, such as tuition waivers, work study programs, apprenticeships and other options. Education Tax Credits and Deductions like the American Opportunity Tax Credit and Lifetime Learning Credit can allow taxpayers to reduce their owed amount or increase their refund.

title: Typical Ways of Paying for College
transcript: Typical ways of paying for college. Over the years, tuition and fees have steadily increased outpacing inflation and wage growth. As a result, the financial burden on students has grown too. That's why college hopefuls should explore all avenues of funding available. So let's dive into the options. Federal Financial Aid The Department of Education's Free Application for Federal Student Aid, or FAFSA, is used to determine a student's eligibility for need-based federal aid. Every student should fill out the FAFSA each year, even if they don't think they'll qualify. As Morningstar reports, "This one free form can determine a student's eligibility for myriad grants and aid for all income levels. Also, the information you report determines eligibility for federal subsidized and unsubsidized loans, which can be very affordable alternatives to other types of loans." Federal grants are financial awards that don't need to be repaid. The most common is the Pell Grant aimed at undergraduate students with financial need. Funds are paid directly to the school, and amounts vary based on: expected family contribution, calculated based on family income, assets and other financial factors. Cost of attendance, full or part-time enrollment, and the length of time the student plans to attend per academic year. Some statistics about the Pell Grant: $4,491 is the average award amount. $6,895 is the current maximum award amount. 34% of undergraduates receive Pell Grants. 88% of recipients go to public colleges. 51% of recipients are from families earning under $20,000 a year. The Federal Supplemental Educational Opportunity Grant is another need-based option. It is administered by the participating school's financial aid office and provides up to an extra $4,000 a year. For students seeking to become teachers, the Teacher Education Assistance for College and Higher Education (TEACH) Grant is an option that pays up to $4,000 per year too. TEACH grant recipients must work as full-time teachers in a high need field at a school serving low income students for at least four years. Completing the FAFSA can also be required for state and institutional aid, as well as scholarships and work study options. Scholarships are an essential part of college funding for many students, and there are thousands of options available. Scholarships exist for almost every purpose and reason from financial need to academic achievement, athletic skills, artistic talent, personal demographic criteria, or simply promoting study in a certain field. The main goals of scholarships are to attract talent, support diversity, increase accessibility, and promote certain programs. Applications may require essays, recommendations, or portfolios. Some scholarships are awarded automatically based on admission applications or financial aid forms. Need-Based Scholarships These are awarded based on the student's demonstrated financial need, determined through forms like the FAFSA. They aim to make education accessible to students from lower income families. FAFSA plays a role in scholarship eligibility, especially for need-based scholarships. The process calculates an expected family contribution based on the family's reported financial information to estimate how much the family could contribute to the student's education. Whether they actually do contribute or not isn't factored in. Providers of need-based scholarships subtract the expected family contribution from the cost of attendance at the school to determine the financial need and their suitability for the scholarship. Some scholarships depend on whether the student is receiving federal aid or not. Additionally, many schools use FAFSA information to determine their own aid offers, if any. Merit based scholarships are awarded based on a student's academic achievements, such as high grades, standardized test scores, extracurricular activity involvement, leadership roles, community service, artistic or athletic talents, and the quality of personal essays or recommendations. State scholarships require FAFSA information to determine eligibility and award amounts. Applicants can show eligibility based on factors like residency, academic achievement, or financial need. College issued scholarships are financial awards provided by colleges or universities funded by their resources such as endowments and donations. They may be merit-based for academic or athletic excellence, financial need based or targeted towards specific groups or study areas. Diversity scholarships support students from racial, ethnic, or underrepresented groups aiming to promote diversity, equity, and inclusion. Governmental scholarships are based on criteria including academic merit, financial need, or to promote specific areas of study. Athletic scholarships are offered so colleges can attract top athletic talent. Recipients typically participate in their sport while at college. They must maintain good grades and good behavior. Note, full ride athletic scholarships are rare. Private scholarships can come from local businesses, foundations, and community organizations, and may be based on factors like a company's interests, a founder's background, or specific themes. Community service scholarships can come from non-profits or other organizations wanting to recognize and reward students involved in community service or volunteer work. Field specific scholarships are given to students pursuing specific careers such as teaching, nursing, or engineering. Funds are offered by industry related organizations to promote study in the applicable fields. Talent-based scholarships are awarded for unique talents such as in music, art, theater, writing, et cetera. Scholarships are often renewable each year, but may come with conditions like maintaining a minimum GPA. Receiving a scholarship can affect one's overall financial aid package, reducing the need for loans or work study.

title: College Cost Breakdown
transcript: College Costs Breakdown Tuition Tuition is the fee students pay for educational instruction. It's usually the largest college expense with tuition rates varying between public and private schools. Public colleges are mainly state funded. They have lower tuition costs than private schools, especially for residents who qualify for a discounted. "in-state rate". Public colleges charge non-residents an out-of-state rate that is two to three times higher than the in-state rate. That is because out-of-state families do not contribute to the state tax base that funds the colleges. To qualify for in-state tuition, students must prove they've been state residents for a specific length of time. They can prove residency by presenting a driver's license, voter registration, utility bills, or other documents. Note, some students qualify for exceptions. For example, active duty military personnel stationed in a state can qualify for in-state rates. There may also be reciprocity agreements for students living in counties that border other states. Since public colleges are state funded, they're more likely to collaborate with local community colleges and fellow state colleges to ensure credits transfer more smoothly. This process saves transfer students from losing credits for courses they've paid for. Private Colleges These rely on tuition, donations, and endowments for operating revenue. They tend to have higher tuition rates regardless of residency. They charge the same rate for everyone. That said they may also offer better financial aid packages, reducing the net cost for students. In addition, graduating from a prestigious private college may give job applicants a competitive edge. Research suggests it can also lead to a higher return on investment. Online program tuition policies vary. Public colleges may differentiate between in-state and out-of-state students for online courses. Other college fees pay for services and activities not covered by tuition, such as library access, student health services, athletic facilities, student union operations, technology resources and administrative expenses. Room and Board This refers to on-campus housing and meal plans. Off-Campus living usually requires budgeting for rent, groceries, and utilities. Note, some colleges require freshmen to live on campus. Books and supplies include textbooks and other course materials. Textbooks are expensive, which is why some students rent or buy used books. It's important to obtain the correct edition. Students can find used books at the local campus bookstore, as well as on Amazon, eBay, Slug Books and BookFinder. Chegg also has used books for sale or rent. Transportation Transportation includes costs for commuting to campus and around town. Public transportation is a common option for students who live on or near campus. Students with vehicles must budget for fuel, maintenance, insurance, and parking fees. Note, transportation costs also include personal travel such as visiting relatives or taking trips during academic breaks. Entertainment and personal expenses vary from person to person, but include clothing, toiletries, mobile devices, internet service, and recreational activities. Technology and equipment costs add up fast, but computers, software, and other electronic devices are a part of the modern college experience. Always ask if a student discount is available. Many organizations offer free or discounted laptops and other devices to students who meet eligibility requirements. Suppliers include PCs for People and Computers with Causes. Numerous electronics manufacturers and retailers offer student discounts, including Apple, Dell, HP, and Lenovo. Buying refurbished devices is another great way to save. Refurbished devices are often as good as new. They just can't be sold as new. Additional expenses may include student health insurance, lab fees, study abroad expenses, extracurricular activities such as organizational dues, graduation fees, professional exams and licensing fees. Course specific materials such as art supplies or musical instruments, emergency fund, internship related expenses, student loan interest, summer courses.

title: Student Debt
transcript: Student Debt College in America has never been affordable, but the costs are going up faster than inflation. Some industry reports note that college tuition has increased at 4.6 times the rate of inflation during the last 50 years. The Education Data Initiative writes that after adjusting for currency inflation, college tuition has increased 747.8% since 1963. So it's easy to see why student debt continues to spiral out of control. Student debt is burdensome and can take years to pay off, or even decades if the person is already struggling to make ends meet. That debt can impact major financial decisions such as buying a home or saving enough for retirement. College can still be a worthwhile investment though. College grads often have access to better paying job opportunities, higher earnings potential, and improved career stability. This long-term financial benefit can outweigh the debt for many students, but it depends on many factors such as one's choice of major, and the state of the job market. That's why students should carefully weigh the potential return on investment of their degree before taking on too much debt. Sometimes degrees don't necessarily lead to an income that meets a college graduate's expectations. Once you are sure what you want to study, it's time to figure out how you're going to fund your college experience and your living expenses during those years and beyond. To do that, let's first break down the costs you can expect.

title: Build Multi-Layer Backups
transcript: ﻿1 Build Multi-Layer Backups. One plan is good. Two is better. Three- that's powerful! When something's important- like getting to school, turning in a project, or managing a health need- it helps to have more than one path to success. Think of it like layers. Plan A is your first choice. Plan B steps in if something goes wrong. Plan C is your emergency fallback. You don't need to over-plan every detail of your life, but for key situations, having layers keeps you steady when things shift. Here are some examples. Transportation. Plan A: Catch the 8:15 bus to class. Plan B: Call A ride-share or ask a neighbor for a lift. Plan C: Let your professor know early and attend virtually if allowed. Technology. Plan A: Use your laptop to submit a report. Plan B: Switch to a campus computer or tablet. Plan C: Contact your instructor with a screenshot and request extra time. Health and Accessibility. Plan A: Bring noise canceling headphones to study in a shared space. Plan B: Move to a quieter location or use a white noise app. Plan C: Reschedule study time and request a more accessible setup next session. These layers aren't just about logistics- they also help with emotional regulation. Many students may experience strong emotional reactions when plans fall apart, especially when the original plan took a lot of effort or energy to set up. That's why it helps to include a calming step before jumping into your backup. For example: step away and count to 10, use grounding techniques like naming five things you see in hear, repeat a calming phrase- pause, breathe, regroup. This gives your nervous system a chance to reset before you move to Plan B or C. When your brain is calm, problem-solving feels more doable. Over time this kind of layered preparation builds resilience. It reminds you: you're not helpless when things shift. You've trained for this. You've got options. And that makes a difference.

title: Additional Terms to Know
transcript: Additional Terms to Know Alumni networks consists of graduates who provide ongoing support, mentorship, and networking opportunities. College sports encompass intercollegiate athletics, promoting school spirit, and providing opportunities for student athletes to excel. Cooperative educational opportunities or co-ops integrate academic learning with practical real world work experience. Greek life refers to fraternities and sororities, organizations focused on community, leadership, academic and social activities. International exchanges involve students attending a foreign institution for a semester or year. Internships are temporary work positions related to a student's major or career interests. Post-graduate schooling or career seeking involves applying to graduate schools or searching for job opportunities after graduation. School-based activities include events and programs organized by the schools, such as workshops, lectures, cultural events, and campus festivals. Student organizations and clubs are student led groups focused on interests, hobbies, and academic fields. Summer study abroad opportunities allow students to study in another country during the summer.

title: Degree Types
transcript: Degree Types The four main categories of college degrees are associate, bachelor's, master's, and doctorate. Here's a brief overview of each. Associate Degree An associate aka associate's degree is a two year degree offered by colleges, universities, community colleges, and technical schools to cover foundational coursework. It requires at least 60 semester credits to complete and can stand alone or be a stepping stone to a bachelor's. Associate degree types include Associate of Applied Science, Associate of Arts, and Associate of Science. Bachelor's Degree A bachelor's is a four year undergraduate degree offering a comprehensive education in a specific major along with general education subjects. It requires at least 120 semester credits to complete and is often used as a stepping stone to a Master's. Bachelor's degree types include but are not limited to: Bachelor of Arts, Bachelor of Science, Bachelor of Applied Science, Bachelor of Architecture, Bachelor of Business Administration, Bachelor of Education, Bachelor of Engineering, Bachelor of Fine Arts, Bachelor of Science in Nursing, and Bachelor of Social Work. Master's Degree A Master's is a graduate degree that takes one to two years to complete. It involves advanced study in a specific field, culminating in a thesis or capstone project. Some master's programs come with an option to finish a thesis or not. They require between 30 and 60 credits to complete. Master's degree types include but are not limited to: Master of Arts, Master of Science, Master of Business Administration, Master of Education, Master of Engineering, Master of Fine Arts, Master of Public Administration, Master of Science in Nursing, and Master of Social Work. Note, in some cases, students can enroll in a dual bachelor's and master's program, which can save time and money. Doctorates or doctoral degrees or PhDs. Doctorates are the highest level of academic degree requiring several years of study beyond a master's. They require original research and a dissertation. For example, a long detailed research paper presenting original findings or analysis on a specific topic. Professional Doctorate Degrees These are a type of doctoral degree geared toward a specific profession. Distinct from research doctorates, professional doctorates involve practical training and internships and are necessary for careers like law, medicine or higher level education administration. Common types include Doctor of Business Administration, Doctor of Education, Doctor of Engineering, Doctor of Nursing Practice, Doctor of Psychology, Doctor of Public Health, Doctor of Social Work, and Juris Doctor. There are also several medical related doctorates such as Doctor of Medicine, Doctor of Chiropractic, Doctor of Dental Medicine, Doctor of Optometry, Doctor of Pharmacy, Doctor of Physical Therapy, and Doctor of Veterinary Medicine. Some programs offer a dual master's and doctorate allowing students to work simultaneously towards both degrees. When researching graduate degrees, you may run across the term: terminal degree. As Coursera explains, "A terminal degree is the highest achievable degree in an academic field or professional discipline." Examples include Master of Fine Arts, Master of Architecture, Juris Doctor, Doctor of Philosophy, Doctor of Education, and Doctor of Medicine. In addition to degrees, most colleges now offer some form of micro-credentials and certificates. Micro-Credential Badges These are a digital form of certification based on specific competencies. These credentials are highly specialized and can be earned by taking one to five classes. They can be stacked or combined to build toward a larger qualification. Certificate. Certificate programs offer a concise selection of related courses. They're often used for professional development with courses generally eligible for credit toward a degree. Certificates may take between four to 12 classes to earn.

title: Degree Course Requirements
transcript: How Degree Course Requirements Work College undergraduate degree course requirements are divided into four categories: general education classes, core classes related to one's major, optional minor, or areas of specialization courses and elective classes. Let's break things down and go over each category. General Education Classes These provide a broad, diverse foundation of knowledge across various disciplines. Common subjects include mathematics, sciences, humanities, social sciences, and sometimes physical education or wellness. General education courses account for 25 to 40% of a 120 credit bachelor's degree. Core Classes These are directly related to one's major. They provide in-depth knowledge and expertise in that field of study and account for 30 to 60% of the total credit hours. STEM majors may have a higher percentage of core classes. Minor and Area of Specialization Classes These provide a secondary focus of study that's less extensive than a major. They are usually optional and may require 15 to 30 semester credits. Electives. Electives allow students to explore subjects beyond general education major and minor courses. That said, if the student prefers, their electives can be related to the major or minor. The number of electives varies depending on how many credits are required in the other categories. In addition, college programs may involve things like lab work to experiment in a controlled environment. Capstone projects that integrate knowledge into a final project. A thesis paper, requiring extensive research and writing on a topic. Internships and cooperative education to gain work experience and professional settings. Workshops and practicums to get hands-on training and specific skills. Pre-professional courses to prepare for advanced study or certifications. Professional workshops and seminars to participate in industry events. Field work to gain experience in the field. Study abroad programs to gain exposure to different cultures. Student organization engagement, such as holding leadership posts. Certain programs have unique major related requirements such as clinical rotations for healthcare students to receive practical training in healthcare settings. Student teaching for education majors to teach in classrooms. Performance and exhibition for arts majors to engage in live events. Legal clinics for pre-law students to participate in mock trials or legal assistance programs and entrepreneurship projects for business majors to execute ideas.

title: Developing Current Skills and Learning New Ones
transcript: Developing current skills and learning new ones for our careers. Learning a skill is different from learning information such as a fact or other piece of information. Once you learn a fact, you know it and it's locked in. But skills are like muscles. Once you learn a skill, it is always possible to develop it further. You can get better at it if you keep working on it, but even if you don't continue to develop a skill, you still have to practice it so you don't lose it. Otherwise, your ability may decline over time. It's important to identify, learn, and develop the skills you'll use in your career. It's also crucial to keep learning new skills so you can develop your career, opening up new opportunities for yourself.

title: Including Keywords
transcript: ﻿1 Including keywords. ATS programs aren't that sophisticated. They're just scanning for certain keywords within the text of your resume. Think of ATS as a search engine like Google. When a person types a phrase into the search bar, Google searches the web and finds matches by scanning websites for keywords. That's why websites use search engine optimization or SEO practices to increase their visibility. Resume keywords work the same way. Employers put keywords in their job ads, then tell their ATS to scan for those words. The ATS decides in a split second how well your resume matches up, then it ranks your resume along with the others that come in. If your resume gets filtered out, a human will probably never see it! So copy and paste the job ad into a document, then go through the sections we mentioned and take note of all the potential keywords you can find. Add as many as you can to your resume, but do it in a way that sounds natural. Don't just stuff them into your text awkwardly. Example one. Say you're applying for a Software development job! In the minimum qualification section, the job post lists the programming language, Python. If you have experience with Python, make sure your resume includes that as a keyword. Example two. In addition to single words, also look for phrases. For example, if you see the phrase project management experience listed as a requirement, then included in your resume, don't change the wording; mirror the phrase the same way the employer wrote it. Once you have a list of the keywords and phrases, take note of which ones apply to your experience and which ones don't. Go through your resume and find areas where you can sprinkle in those keywords and phrases if they apply but don't list skills or experiences you don't actually have.

title: Technical, Vocational, and Trade Schools
transcript: Technical, vocational and trade schools. The terms technical, vocational, and trade schools are often used interchangeably. Each type of school offers short training programs designed to teach the specific skills needed to start a particular career. Technical colleges blend practical technical training with theoretical knowledge. They prepare professionals to work in industries such as healthcare, information technology, and manufacturing, among others. Vocational schools provide hands-on skills training with less emphasis on theory. Students may prepare for careers in the culinary arts, hospitality roles, and beauty and personal care professions. Trade schools focus on the manual technical aspects of training for trade jobs, such as automotive and mechanic trades, welding, construction, plumbing, and electrical work.

title: Assessments & Tests
transcript: Assessments and Tests State Assessments Annual state assessments are tests given to students, so school districts can identify areas for instructional improvement. Aggregated results are used to spot regional trends. Individual results can also offer insights to the test taking students. Under the Every Student Succeeds Act, state assessments are required by law. However, parents do have the option to request an opt out if they don't want their child to take an assessment. SAT and ACT The Standards Admissions Test, SAT, and the American College Test ACT have been used for decades by college admissions committees to assess high school students readiness for college. SAT and ACT scores provide a common data point that can be used to compare applicants. Neither test is necessarily harder than the other, and colleges do not give preference to one test over the other. "The SAT will include informational graphics such as charts and graphs," writes Shorelight, and "The ACT answers will be text-based only." SAT and ACT scores are only one part of the college application, but they do carry significant weight. Other factors include overall GPA and Advanced Placement course grades listed on transcripts, application essays, recommendation letters, extracurricular activities, and demonstrated interest. Some schools are test blind or test free, meaning they don't require SAT ACT scores. Others make it optional or offer waivers. However, to keep your options wide open, it's worthwhile to consider taking one of the tests. ASVAB The Armed Services Vocational Aptitude Battery, ASVAB, is "a multiple aptitude battery that measures developed abilities and helps predict future academic and occupational success in the military." writes the official side of the ASVAB Enlistment Testing program. It is administered annually to more than 1 million military applicants, high school and post-secondary students. In addition to being offered at many high schools, the ASVAB exam can be scheduled at 14,000. military entrance testing stations (METS) or military entrance processing stations (MEPS) around the US. The ASVAB test is free, but test takers can enroll in preparatory courses if desired. The scores help recruiters determine which jobs a person may qualify for, but simply taking the test doesn't obligate anyone to join the military.

title: Industry Trends
transcript: ﻿1 Industry Trends. A major trend in this sector is the rise of industry 4.0, which uses technologies like automation, artificial intelligence, cyber physical systems, cloud computing, 3D printing, and the internet of things to improve efficiency and productivity. Meanwhile, the industrial internet of things enables manufacturers to connect machines, devices, and sensors to the internet. This allows real-time data collection helping to optimize operations. Sustainable manufacturing is another growing trend as companies seek to reduce waste, improve energy efficiency, and utilize renewable resources. Curious about which pathways might match your personality and interest in this sector? Then let's take the Gladeo Quiz!

title: Different Pathways in the Sector
transcript: ﻿1 Different Pathways in the Sector. The six main career pathways into this sector are Production, Manufacturing Production Process Development, Maintenance, Installation and Repair, Quality Assurance, Logistics and Inventory Control, and Health, Safety and Environmental Assurance. What is Production? Production workers use tools and operate machinery to assemble, manufacture, or process goods in factories or production facilities. Careers in this pathway include Assembler, Calibration Technician, Electrical Installer and Repairer, Hoist and Winch Operator, Machine Operator, and Welder. What is Manufacturing Production Process Development? Professionals in this field design, optimize, and refine the processes used to make products. Their goal is to improve efficiency and product quality. Careers include Design Engineer, Electronics Engineer, Industrial Engineer, Labor Relations Manager, Manufacturing Technician, and Production Manager. What is Maintenance, Installation and Repair? Professionals in this field install new equipment, perform preventive maintenance, troubleshoot issues, and make adjustments or repairs as needed. Careers include Biomedical Equipment Technician, Computer Installer/ Repairer, Facility Electrician, Industrial Machinery Mechanic, Maintenance Repairer, and Plumber, Pipe Fitter and Steam Fitter. What is Quality Assurance? Quality Assurance workers ensure processes and products meet quality, performance, and safety standards. Careers include Calibration Technician, Inspector, Lab Technician, Process Control Technician, Quality Control Technician, and Quality Engineer. What is Logistics and Inventory Control? Logistics and Inventory Control workers manage the movement of materials to production lines, unload materials, wrap and load products for shipment, and communicate with traffic managers. Careers include Dispatcher, Freight, Stock, and Material Mover, Logistical Engineer, Materials Handler, Process Improvement Technician, and Traffic, Shipping, and Receiving Clerk. What is Health, Safety and Environmental Assurance? Professionals in this pathway Strive to establish safe, healthy workplaces by designing and implementing programs to prevent mishaps and protect the environment. Careers include Environmental Engineer, Environmental Specialist, Health and Safety Representative, Safety Coordinator, Safety Engineer, and Safety Team Leader.

title: Top Skills Needed
transcript: ﻿1 Top Skills Needed. Workers in these fields require focus, attention to detail, and a strong work ethic. Other skills needed include: Business Acumen. Many positions require business acumen to understand how to design and manufacture a product cost effectively, then sell it at a profit. Communication Skills. Communicating ideas to others is essential, such as explaining designs to engineers, manufacturers, and consumers. Creativity. Whether they're improving existing products or designing new ones, workers must be creative, curious, and imaginative. Flexibility. Any issue that causes production delays is expensive, so workers must be flexible and ready to adapt to changing circumstances while continuing to make progress. Problem-Solving. Problems can come up when making any product, so it's important to anticipate, identify, and resolve issues in a timely fashion. Teamwork Skills. Most roles in this sector serve as part of a larger, often interdisciplinary team that has to get along and get things done on time! Technical Skills. Workers commonly rely on software for computer aided design in manufacturing, simulations, visualizations, 3D printing, robotics, and automation.

title: Who enjoys working in this sector?
transcript: ﻿1 Types of People Who Enjoy Working in this Sector. Manufacturing professionals are results-driven problem-solvers who pay close attention to detail and quality assurance, they make sure things are done correctly. Some jobs focus on applying complex engineering principles. Other roles include creative designers, visionary makers, and inspired tinkerers. Two things they all have in common are, one, they're comfortable using equipment and technology, and two, they enjoy working as part of a team that brings new ideas to life!

title: Why is it important?
transcript: ﻿1 Why is it important? The Manufacturing sector literally creates the products we all use every day of our lives. From appliances and furniture to computers and cars. We rely on these products in our modern society and would be lost without them. Manufacturing is massively important to the world's economy. Accounting for 15% of the global gross domestic product each year. The industries within this sector provide around half a billion jobs worldwide, including over 12 million in the United States. The sector is also an integral part of the global supply chain. It enables international trade and ensures consumers have access to the goods they want and need!

title: Overview

Common job titles include Assembler, Design Engineer, Industrial Machinery Mechanic, Calibration Technician, Materials Handler, and Safety Engineer. "Manufacturing is the backbone of our economy. It provides jobs, creates wealth, and drives innovation.", says Jay Timmons, President of the National Association of Manufacturers.

title: Industry Trends

title: Different Pathways in the Sector
transcript: ﻿1 Different Pathways in the Sector. Correction services professionals may supervise and care for incarcerated individuals or help to ensure released prisoners are successfully reintegrated back into general society. Careers in this pathway include Case Manager, Corrections Officer, Detention, Deputy, Jail Administrator, Probation or Parole Officer, Warden. What is Emergency and Fire Management Services? Firefighters and emergency services professionals swiftly respond to emergencies, often being the first on the scene. They play vital roles in public safety, performing dangerous or critical tasks, such as putting out fires and treating the injured. Careers include Dispatcher, EMT, Firefighter, Forest Firefighter, Hazardous Materials Responder, Rescue Worker. What is Law Enforcement Services? Law enforcement officers uphold the law, ensure public safety, and help protect lives and property. They also regulate traffic, investigate criminal activity, and undertake court-related duties. Careers include Animal Control Officer, Bomb Technician, Criminal Investigator and Special Agent, Immigration and Customs Inspector, Police and Patrol Officer, Private Detective and Investigator. What is Legal Services? Legal services workers help people understand and protect their rights, resolve disputes, and navigate our complex legal system. Careers include Attorney, Court Reporter, Investigator, Judge, Legal Secretary, Paralegal. What is Security and Protective Services? Security and protective services professionals maintain public safety and safeguard individuals and property. Their responsibilities include deterring crime, shielding people from harm, investigating crimes, and preventing theft and other losses. Careers include Computer Forensics Examiner, Executive Protection Officer or Specialist, Lifeguard, Loss Prevention Specialist, Private Investigator, Security Officer.

title: Industry Trends

transcript: ﻿1 Different Pathways in the Sector. What are Network Systems? Network systems workers design and maintain computer networks, collections of interconnected computers, servers, and devices that let us exchange data. Careers in this pathway include Communications Analyst, Data Communications Analyst, Information Technology Engineer, Network Architect, Network Engineer, and Systems Administrator. What are Information Support and Services? Workers in the information support and services pathway provide tech support and guidance to users and organizations, ensuring IT resources are up and available. Tasks can range from troubleshooting to configuring new systems or teaching users about new programs. Careers include Account Manager, Data Systems Designer, Information Systems Architect, Help Desk Specialist, Product Support Engineer, and Systems Analyst. What are Web and Digital Communications? Web and Digital Communications professionals create interactive multimedia products and services for business, training, entertainment, communications, and marketing. Careers include Animator, Media Specialist, Virtual Reality Specialist, and Web Architect or Designer. What is Programming and Software Development? Programmers and developers write computer code for operating systems, software and applications, so computers and other devices can perform tasks. These tasks range from performing calculations to powering websites, mobile apps, and video games. Careers include Applications Analyst, Computer Engineer, Game Developer, Programmer, Software Applications Specialist, and Software Design Engineer.
