title: Extracurricular Activities
transcript: Extracurricular Activities Participation in extracurricular activities can not only help students develop crucial soft skills, but they are also important elements of any job or college application. Such activities can either be school sponsored or not. School sponsored activities may include things such as academic competition teams, audio, visual club, band, book club, chess club, debate team, drama theater club, language clubs, robotics club, school newspaper, sports teams, and student government. Non-School related activities can be anything from community engagement to developing personal hobbies. Ideas include community arts or music groups, community sports leagues, community theater, cooking classes, dance, environmental conservation groups, local youth groups, martial arts, multicultural centers, outdoor activities groups, physical fitness groups, political activist groups, religious youth groups, small business initiatives, volunteering. Being involved in state, national, or international organizations is also a great way to show engagement beyond the basics of doing your required high school work. Note, whatever activities you select, they do not all have to relate to your desired college. Major. Colleges love to see well-rounded applicants. That said, it can be beneficial to have some activities related to your major. For instance, say you want to apply to a computer science program if you participate in a computer club in school, or take coding courses in your free time, that will demonstrate to college application reviewers that you're committed to that area of study. Language Immersion Programs Language immersion programs for high schoolers are an adventurous, effective way to learn another language while also raising one's cultural awareness. They range from short term programs lasting a few weeks to semester or even year long formats that are part of an international student exchange program. Most immersion programs involve some sort of home stay where the student lives with a family that is native to the country being visited. Classroom Learning is often combined with travel experiences to enhance learning objectives. Notem with a little searching, language students can frequently find scholarships or even programs sponsored by governments or foundations. Competitions and Awards Participating in contests and striving to win awards enables high school students to develop a sense of accomplishment and self-worth, boosting their self-esteem and fostering a competitive spirit. These achievements not only enhance their college applications by showcasing their talents and dedication, but also prepare them for future challenges by instilling a mindset of perseverance and excellence.

title: Special Schools
transcript: Special schools Early college High Schools There are 400 early college schools in the US making them relatively rare, but what are they? "Early colleges are partnerships of school districts, charter management organizations or high schools, and two or four year colleges or universities." notes the American Institutes for Research. A type of dual enrollment program, early colleges offer students the opportunity to earn an associate's degree or up to two years of college credits towards a bachelor's degree in high school at no or low cost to students. Early college high schools go beyond dual enrollment because they also offer extra student support services. International Baccalaureate programs. The International Baccalaureate IB Program is a rigorous pre university course of study that challenges students to be well-rounded, engaged, and globally minded individuals. In high school, the two year IB diploma program requires a 4,000 word extended essay, a theory of knowledge course and creativity activities service, a range of activities designed to enhance personal and interpersonal development. Students study six subjects from six subject groups and are assessed through a mix of exams, presentations, essays, practical lab work, and exhibitions. Courses are similar to advanced placement, but with a global focus and a goal to develop critical thinking. After completing all requirements, students may earn an IB diploma. Many colleges offer credit for high scores on IB exams. It's important to meet with school counselors or academic advisors who can help you determine the best available programs and courses for your goals. Parental or caretaker involvement is also helpful when it comes to decision making.

title: Programs to Prepare for College
transcript: Programs to Prepare for College For students whose main focus is to prepare for college, there are several things to do in high school. For starters, it's important to plan your course selection strategically. That's because college classes can be much tougher than high school ones, so you wanna be ready and have a strong academic foundation. Many college freshmen aren't sufficiently prepared and their college GPA suffers accordingly. That can have negative consequences, especially for students on a scholarship because maintaining a good GPA is usually a criterion for receiving ongoing scholarship funding. So if you're planning to attend college after high school, it's a good idea to sign up for honors or advanced placement AP classes to help you prepare for the challenges ahead. Honors Classes Honors classes are advanced courses that offer a more rigorous and in-depth curriculum then standard classes. Overall, they stimulate the difficulty of college level coursework and they cover subjects at a faster pace. This encourages students to develop diligent study habits, and strong test taking skills. Unlike advanced placement courses, you cannot earn college credit through honors course exams. Advanced Placement Advanced placement courses are college level classes designed to help prepare students for the rigors of college. Getting strong grades in AP classes can make your college application more competitive. AP classes don't directly count for college credit. However, students may take an end of course AP test, which can count for credit or placement at many colleges. What does placement mean? When a student signs up for a college program, they must take several core classes such as algebra or english composition. Placement tests determine the appropriate academic starting point for students in these courses. In other words, the tests assess English, reading and math skills to determine which classes match the students' current skill level. That way they aren't thrown into a course that is far too hard for them. Dual Enrollment or Early College Credit Courses Dual enrollment is when a high school student takes a college class, often through a local community college that counts for both high school and college credit at the same time. This is an excellent way to get a jumpstart on college, and since dual enrollment courses are often offered to students at no cost, they can save a fortune on tuition while exploring careers of interest. Unfortunately, not all colleges will accept credits earned in this manner. So it's important to discuss your future plans with your school counselor to ensure your dual enrollment credits will transfer to the college you want to attend, after graduation.

title: Additional Options
transcript: Additional Options Summer Programs Summer programs can be truly transformative in a young person's life. Combining learning with personal development that may broaden your horizons by exposing you to new ideas and experiences, they come in all shapes and sizes, ranging from local programs such as a parks and recreation camp to state, national, and international options. Many community colleges also offer exciting summer programs for career exploration, STEM skills development, and earning college credits. They're an amazing opportunity for growth and exploration in diverse fields, from academic subjects to adventurous activities. Through the right summer programs, students can develop essential life skills like independence, responsibility, and self-confidence. Certifications Certifications are formal recognitions that validate your proficiency in a specialized field or skill. They can significantly enhance your credibility with potential employers. Certifications are frequently offered by companies to acknowledge expertise in a product or service. An example is the Microsoft Certified Technology Specialist certificate. Many certifications have no minimum age restriction and are simply based on passing a standardized assessment of knowledge and skills. Certifications are also offered by organizations for workers to demonstrate mastery in professional domains. An example is the certified professional in Healthcare quality certificate. These types of certifications may have prerequisites such as prior relevant work experience.

title: Work & Volunteer Experiences
transcript: Work and Volunteer Experiences Part-time Jobs Your high school years are a hectic time filled with schoolwork, extracurricular activities, and socialization, but as indeed.com notes, finding time for a part-time job can provide high school students with valuable work experience, transferable skills, and a sense of independence, as well as income. Holding a part-time job before graduation may give you a boost against the competition when you go to apply for jobs later too. High School Internships Internships are short periods of work for companies or organizations. They're designed to provide interns with introductory work experiences in professional fields, offering a chance to gain insights, knowledge and skills while networking and building social capital. Internships can be an invaluable launchpad for a future career. Some are unpaid, but the free training and experience are usually very educational and worth the time and effort. Job Shadowing Job shadowing offers students an inside glimpse into an organization's activities as they follow an employee performing their normal daily duties. Once you identify careers or industries you want to learn more about, reach out to a school counselor or career advisor who may be able to share information about shadowing opportunities or help schedule one for you. You can also ask people in your network such as family members, friends or acquaintances, or attend career fairs and ask the recruiters there. Lastly, it's easy to do an online search for shadowing opportunities, including virtual ones. Volunteering. Volunteering can be hugely beneficial for students of all ages. "Volunteering presents a tangible way for students to make a positive impact in their community and help organizations accomplish their missions, all while bolstering students' resumes for college applications and jobs." states US news. By definition, volunteers aren't paid, but like unpaid internships, they're an amazing way to get some free experience while beefing up your credentials and having fun.

title: Career-Related Programs
transcript: Career Related Programs Career and Technical Education Career and technical education, also known as vocational education, can prepare students to start great paying careers immediately after graduation. "Students learn skills that prepare them to work in a particular field after high school." explains understood.com. Vocational schools still offer training in trades like carpentry and culinary skills, but they also feature programs and fields like healthcare, technology and graphic design. Many high schools either offer career and technical education programs in-house or partner with local programs and let students attend classes during regular school hours. Such programs can also lay a good foundation for students who want to pursue an associate or bachelor's degree. High School Apprenticeships and Pre-apprenticeships "High school apprenticeship programs combine work-based, on-the-job learning with relevant technical education in the classroom." writes apprenticeship.gov. Students who participate in these programs graduate with a high school diploma, earn college credits and industry credentials. Apprentices get to earn while they learn receiving a wage as they gain practical work skills and experience. The cost of education and training are typically 100% covered by the employer, by government funding, or by a combination of the two. Many programs offer additional financial support, such as stipends or scholarships to cover incidental expenses like transportation or work attire. It's a good investment for employers looking to train students and then potentially hire them as full-time workers later. By completing a high school apprenticeship, graduates can keep going and finish a full formal apprenticeship path, if they want. In many cases, they can even do that while also attending college. Similar to high school apprenticeships, which are integrated into the school system, pre-apprenticeship programs also prepare individuals for entering into formal apprenticeships. The main difference is that pre-apprenticeship programs can apply to individuals who may or may not be in high school. Both types of apprenticeships can start students on a great career path. Just remember, apprenticeships are competitive and require good behavior and academic performance in school. Special programs Job Corps. "Job Corps's mission is to help young people ages 16 through 24 to improve the quality of their lives through vocational and academic training aimed at gainful employment and career paths," Job Corps explains. There are 161 Job Corps locations around the US, and they have a high success rate of placing program graduates. YouthBuild "YouthBuild champions today's opportunity youth who aspire to improve their lives and communities by building the skills and resources to reach their full potential." says YouthBuild, USA. Through hundreds of YouthBuild programs, these young people pursue their education, prepare for future careers, and grow into community leaders.

title: Overview
transcript: Gladeo's FutureReady Career Development Course is designed to navigate the vast world of career possibilities and arm you with the knowledge, skills, wisdom, and motivation essential for the journey ahead. The course is broken down to a series of chapters designed not only to impart crucial lessons on career development, but also to challenge you to dig deep within yourself, to discover your passions, understand what truly drives you, and unearth the motivations that will fuel your journey. Chapter 1, Who Am I? This chapter sets the foundation for your self-awareness and helps you visualize your future in a world of endless possibilities. We will reflect on your life experiences and aspirations, and learn more about your personality and strengths as you understand what motivates you. You'll also have the chance to explore your passions through inspiring career stories. Chapter 2, Building Resiliency. Learn to embrace failure as a stepping stone to success. By learning and mastering the art of falling up, you'll discover the resilience inside you through tales of famous failures that turned into future fortunes. Chapter 3, The Importance of Skills and Lifelong Learning. From hard skills that sharpen your technical abilities to soft skills that enhance your interpersonal interactions, this chapter covers essential lessons for thriving in any career. Chapter 4, Marketing Yourself. From crafting a stellar LinkedIn profile to writing impactful resumes and cover letters. Learn how to make a memorable impression on potential employers and get an edge over the ever-changing and competitive landscape of career development. Chapter 5, Getting Experience. Explore avenues to gain practical experience from internships to freelance work. Learn how to navigate and leverage virtual volunteering and online learning opportunities to build your career from the ground up. Chapter 6, the Brave New World of AI. In a rapidly growing world shaped by the emergence of artificial intelligence, it might be intimidating to learn about and embrace AI, we will explore how AI is reshaping the workplace and how it can be used responsibly to be a powerful tool in your hands. Chapter 7, Technology and Social Media. Enhance your skills in navigating the complex world of digital platforms and social media. You'll learn about the concept of a digital footprint and how the trail you've made on the internet can impact your future. Chapter 8, Networking and Interview Preparation. By developing your networking skills and learning effective interview techniques, you will be able to confidently present yourself to future opportunities. Chapter 9, Post-Secondary Readiness. Prepare for life after high school with comprehensive insights into various pathways, including community colleges, four year universities, and apprenticeships. Chapter 10, Capstone Project and Epilogue. Use all the skills you developed and lessons you learned to create a compelling resume, impactful cover letter, and awe inspiring portfolios that showcase your unique abilities and aspirations. Every lesson is a step towards creating your future and every challenge, a chance to grow, prepare to be inspired, to learn, and to transform your dreams into reality. Let's shape the future together. Confident, equipped, and ever ready for what lies ahead.

title: Pursuing Advanced Education In High School
transcript: ﻿1 - Pursuing advanced education and training in high school and beyond. Before we jump into that next module, let's touch on the benefits of pursuing advanced education and training. To start, it's a competitive world out there, so to establish a good career and strong income potential, students need to go beyond the basics when it comes to education. There are many ways to do that, such as starting a training program at a technical or vocational school, entering an apprenticeship, signing up for a certification such as a Microsoft IT certification, going to community college to take classes for a certificate or associate degree, enrolling in a university to work on a bachelor's. As you can see, post-secondary education can take many forms. It isn't just about earning a four year degree. There are diverse options to meet the needs and goals of everyone. The key is simply finding the right path that aligns with your unique situation. No matter which path you choose, it's important to set yourself up for success by preparing in advance. How you set yourself up for success depends on what path you intend to take. For instance, all students should engage in activities that will help set them apart from the competition, such as extracurricular activities to build soft skills like teamwork and time management. In addition, high school students can take classes or work part-time, jobs, or internships that will establish a solid foundation for their next educational adventure. Students interested in apprenticeships or vocational training can take classes or pre-apprenticeship programs related to their chosen field of post-secondary study. Those aiming for college should prepare by enrolling in advanced placement AP courses and joining relevant student clubs. No matter what you choose to do, post-secondary education should play a major part in your plans.

title: Developing Career Skills
transcript: ﻿1 Developing Career Skills The goal of seeking advanced education and training is to prepare for a career. A career is not the same as a job. A job is a specific position of employment. While a career is a long-term professional journey with progressive development and growth. Many workers switch jobs over the course of their careers. As they grow professionally and develop new skills. As they progress on their career trajectories, they can qualify for higher earnings, better opportunities and options with increased job satisfaction and security. Education and training are the keys to career success. They provide the foundational knowledge and skills needed to grow and adapt in an ever changing job market. Employers of today and tomorrow are looking for applicants with a mix of hard and soft skills, practical experience, and academic knowledge. Unlike hard technical skills, soft skills like effective communication and attention to detail are developed over time through personal experiences, social interactions, and both formal and informal education. It's time to explore opportunities in high school.

title: Industry Trends
transcript: ﻿1 Industry Trends. There are several trends affecting the Transportation Distribution and Logistics sector. Automation and robotics are integrating into warehouses to reduce manual labor, while data analytics and AI are optimizing supply chain routes and enhancing real-time tracking. Meanwhile, E-commerce keeps pushing companies to change their distribution strategies. Further down the road, America may see a nationwide infrastructure for electric vehicles as well as the adoption of more delivery drones and self-driving vehicles. Eventually, we might even have a high-speed rail network like the ones popular in other countries. Curious about which pathways might match your personality and interest in this sector? Let's take the Sector Quiz!

title: Choosing Your Path
transcript: Types of credentials and degrees you can earn in college. Certificates Certificates are official recognitions provided by companies, colleges, and other organizations to verify that an individual has completed a brief educational program on a specific topic. They do not lead to a degree, but are useful for career advancement or specialization. Most certificate programs feature a selection of required courses plus a few relevant electives to choose from. Note, certificates are not the same as certifications. However, certificate courses can generally be used for college credit and be applied toward a degree. Associate's Degree An associates is a two year degree, often offered by community colleges covering foundational coursework in a particular field. It can stand alone or be a stepping stone to a Bachelor's. Bachelor's Degree A bachelor's is a four year undergraduate degree offering a comprehensive education in a specific major along with general education and various subjects. Common types are Bachelor of Arts, BA and Bachelor of Science BS. Master's Degree A Master's is considered a graduate degree because it is pursued after a bachelor's, often taking one to two years to complete. It involves advanced study in a specific field, culminating in a thesis or capstone project. Examples include Master of Arts, MA, Master of Science, MS, and Master of Business Administration, MBA, Doctor of Philosophy, PhD. A PhD is a research-focused graduate degree that typically takes several years to complete. It requires original research and a dissertation, which is a long detailed research paper, presenting original findings or analysis on a specific topic. PhDs are often pursued for careers in academia or research. Professional Degrees Professional degrees like Juris Doctor, JD, Doctor of Medicine, MD, or Doctor of Education, EdD, are geared towards specific professions. They usually involve practical training and internships and are necessary for certain careers like law, medicine, or higher level educational administration. Specialized Degrees Some fields require specialized degrees, such as Master of Fine Arts, MFA, Master of Public Health, MPH, or Doctor of Veterinary Medicine, DVM, each tailored to a niche professional area.

title: Different Pathways in the Sector
transcript: ﻿1 Different Pathways in the Sector. The seven main career pathways are Transportation Operations, Logistics Planning and Management Services, Warehousing and Distribution Center Operations, Facility and Mobile Equipment Maintenance, Transportation Systems or Infrastructure Planning, Management and Regulation, Health, Safety and Environmental Management, and Sales and Service. What are Transportation Operations? Transportation operations professionals use different types of vehicles to physically move people and goods from one place to another. Careers in this pathway include Airplane Pilot or Co-Pilot, Locomotive Engineer, Marine Captain, Transportation Manager, Truck Driver. What are Logistics Planning and Management Services? Logistics manages the behind the scenes details involved with shipping, transporting, and receiving people in goods from one point to another. Through efficient planning and management, businesses can reduce costs and better meet customers' demands. Careers include International Logistics Specialist, Logistician, Logistics Analyst, Logistics Consultant, Logistics Engineer. What are Warehousing and Distribution Center Operations? Goods must be stored in warehouses until they are purchased and shipped out. While on route, they may wait in other storage facilities or at ports. Warehouse and distribution professionals ensure these goods are labeled, loaded, and accounted for. Careers include Car, Truck, and Ship Loader, Laborer and Material Mover-Handler, Packer and Packager-Handler, Production, Planning, Expediting Clerk, Traffic, Shipping, and Receiving Clerk, Warehouse Manager. What is Facility and Mobile Equipment Maintenance? These workers service and repair vehicles or maintain facilities to keep them clean, organized, and safe. Careers include Aerospace Engineering and Operations Technician, Automotive Service Technician, Bus and Truck Mechanic, Diesel Engine Specialist, Facility Engineer, Industrial Electrician. What is Transportation Systems or Infrastructure Planning, Management and Regulation? This aspect of the transportation sector ensures roads, bridges, airports, ports, and other infrastructure are safe, sufficient, reliable and sustainable. Careers include Aviation Inspector, Civil Engineer, Environmental Engineer, Freight Inspector, Port Manager, Transportation Engineer. What is Health, Safety and Environmental Management? Workers in this field are concerned with the health and safety of workers, and the protection of the environment. They help prevent accidents, keep workers from exposure to hazardous substances, and reduce pollution. Careers include Department of Transportation Inspector, Environmental Compliance Specialist, First Responder, Health and Safety Manager, Industrial Hygienist, Risk Manager. What is the Sales and Service Pathway? Sales professionals generate leads, qualify prospects, and close deals. They may provide product demonstrations, answer customer questions, and offer continuing support to customers. Careers include Cargo and Freight Agent, Cashier, Counter, and Rental Clerk, Customer Service Manager, Customer Service Representative, Marketing Manager, Sales Manager.

title: Who enjoys working in this sector?
transcript: ﻿1 Types of People Who Enjoy Working in this Sector. People who work in these career fields are often analytical, objective, and interested in logistics and how things get moved around safely and efficiently. They care about the impact of their work and wanna ensure people, goods, businesses, and communities are protected and well taken care of. They're thoughtful, dependable, organized, and focused on both the little things and the big picture.

title: Why is it important?
transcript: ﻿1 Why is it important? Without this sector, it would be virtually impossible to get to work, school, home, shopping centers, medical facilities, or anywhere else! We also wouldn't have the means to receive food or other goods delivered from their places of origin to local warehouses, stores, or other outlets where businesses and individuals can access them. But thanks to transportation, distribution and logistics, we have a bustling economy based on domestic and international trade, and we can travel where we want and access items and services we need in our daily lives.

title: Overview
transcript: ﻿1 Welcome. We will be learning about the National CTE Sector, Transportation, Distribution, and Logistics. This massive sector provides the vital infrastructure and means to move people and goods all around the country, which is essential for businesses to operate and for people to get to wherever they need to go. It covers all the planning, management, and movement of vehicles by road, rail, air, and water. It also deals with the processes of shipping and storing goods along their roots from producers to end consumers. Transportation, distribution and logistics play a crucial role in facilitating global trade, supporting economies, and ensuring timely delivery of passengers, products, and services. The seven main career pathways are Transportation Operations, Logistics Planning and Management Services, Warehousing and Distribution Center Operations, Facility and Mobile Equipment Maintenance, Transportation Systems or Infrastructure Planning, Management and Regulation, Health, Safety and Environmental Management, and Sales and Service. Common job titles include Airplane Pilot, Freight Inspector, Facility Engineer, Marine Captain, Bus and Truck Mechanic, and Warehouse Manager. "Transportation, Distribution and Logistics is essential to our way of life. It allows us to get the goods and services we need when we need them." says Chris Spear, President and CEO of the American Trucking Association.

title: Industry Trends
transcript: ﻿1 Industry Trends. The STEM sector is constantly evolving, so workers must keep up with ever-changing technologies, shifting priorities, and regulatory requirements. In recent years, STEM fields have moved towards more interdisciplinary collaboration in order to solve the world's biggest problems. And because STEM careers require creative thinking, there's a push to change the acronym to STEAM adding an A to incorporate training in artistic fields. There's also more emphasis on diversity, equity, and inclusion in STEM education and careers to foster new perspectives and better solutions. Curious about which pathways might match your personality and interest in this sector? Then let's take the Gladeo Quiz!

title: Different Pathways in the Sector
transcript: ﻿1 Different Pathways in the Sector. The two main career pathways are Engineering and Technology, and Science and Mathematics. What is the Engineering and Technology Pathway? Engineers and technicians use their expertise to design, develop, test, and refine technologies for use in various products and services in numerous different industries. Careers in this pathway include Aeronautical Engineer, Architectural Engineer, Civil Engineer, Computer Engineer, Electrical Engineer, and Mechanical Engineer. What is the Science and Mathematics Pathway? There are many career types in this broad pathway related to biology, chemistry, physics, computer science, and or mathematics. Workers in many of these jobs are helping to solve the world's most challenging problems. Careers include Applied Mathematician, Archeologists, Chemist, Geneticist, Math Teacher, and Meteorologist.

title: Top Skills Needed
transcript: ﻿1 Top Skills nNeded. There is a wide range of hard and soft skills needed to succeed in any STEM role. Such skills include: Analysis. The ability to examine information and reach a logical conclusion. Collaboration. Many STEM workers must collaborate in teams to achieve results. Creativity. Innovative solutions are often required to address tough problems. Critical Thinking. Critical thinking means objectively evaluating information to come to rational conclusions without making assumptions or basing decisions on emotion. Engineering-Design Thinking. This is an iterative, collaborative approach for solving complex problems through brainstorming, prototyping, testing, and refining. Industry Knowledge. It's crucial to stay up-to-date on trends and breakthroughs related to the job you're doing. Math Skills. STEM professionals regularly use advanced math skills in their daily work to solve complex equations. Research. To work in STEM often requires doing in-depth research to prepare for a project.

title: Who enjoys working in this sector?
transcript: ﻿1 Types of People who Enjoy Working in this Sector. People who enjoy working in STEM fields are typically logical, analytical thinkers with a deep curiosity about the world. They thrive on challenges and problem-solving. Alongside these intellectual traits, they often exhibit creativity, innovation, persistence, open-mindedness, and a strong work ethic. They are good communicators and collaborators, and they tend to be socially responsible. These traits help them excel in their fields and contribute positively to society.

title: Why is it important?
transcript: ﻿1 Why is it important? STEM fields provide the foundation for our modern way of life. Without STEM, we wouldn't have machines or equipment of any kind. There would be no safe, comfortable buildings to live and work in, no vehicles to drive, fly or sail in, and or life-saving medicines and procedures for when we're sick or injured. We wouldn't have TVs, computers, smartphones, or any of the gadgets and appliances we take for granted. There are hundreds of possible STEM career choices, and they're all vital for the development and well-being of our global community.

title: Overview
transcript: ﻿1 Welcome. We will be learning about the National CTE Sector, Science, Technology, Engineering, and Mathematics, or STEM. The STEM sector covers work ranging from scientific research and technological development to engineering designs to mathematical applications. These fields drive innovation and address critical global challenges. The two main career pathways to enter this sector are Engineering and Technology, and Science and Mathematics. Common job titles include Aerospace Engineer, Anthropologist, Civil Engineer, Computer Engineer, Ecologist, Geneticist, and Meteorologist. "STEM is not just about learning facts and figures. It's about learning how to think critically and solve problems. It's about preparing our students for the jobs of the future." Says Michelle Obama, Former First Lady of the United States.

title: Industry Trends
transcript: ﻿1 Industry Trends. This sector evolves and changes regularly, so it is important to keep up with trends. For example, businesses are getting more sophisticated in gathering and analyzing customer data so they can adjust marketing strategies. Social media platforms have become essential for finding consumers, marketing to them, and building brand loyalty. Increasingly, customers want more personalized experiences, compelling businesses to tailor marketing, sales, and services to meet the demand. Consumers are also paying more attention to how companies express their values and what they're doing to become more sustainable and eco-friendly. The marketing sector is important to promote economic growth and job creation. Curious about which pathways might match your personality and interest in this sector? Let's take the Sector Quiz!

title: Different Pathways in the Sector
transcript: ﻿1 Different Pathways in the Sector. The five main career pathways into this sector are Marketing Management, Professional Sales, Merchandising, Marketing Communications, and Marketing Research. What is Marketing Management? Marketing professionals direct all aspects of business operations related to advertising, promotions, marketing, sales, and public relations. Their work keeps the marketplace competitive as they help consumers learn about new products and services. Careers in this pathway include Brand Manager, Database Analyst, Director of Market Development, Marketing Services Manager, Product Planner, Research Project Manager. What are Professional Sales? Professional salespeople use their deep understanding of target markets and consumer habits to develop strategies for acquiring customers. Careers include Account Executive, Broker, Client Relationship Manager, Retail Sales Specialist, Sales Person, Telemarketer. What is Merchandising? Merchandising workers display, promote or otherwise work with products to maximize sales in retail environments. Careers include Customer Service Representative, Department Manager, Merchandise Buyer, Operations Manager, Receiving clerk, Store Manager. What our Marketing Communications? Marketing communications professionals come up with creative marketing plans and campaigns for advertising and public relations. Careers include Art or Graphics Director, Copywriter, Creative Director, Marketing Associate, Public Relations Manager, Sales Representative. What is Marketing Research? Marketing researchers analyze consumer and marketplace data, then offer insights to businesses about services they should provide or desirable product designs and features. Careers include CRM Manager, Director of Market Development, Interviewer, Marketing Services Manager, Planning Analyst, Strategic Planner.

title: Top Skills Needed
transcript: ﻿1 Top Skills Needed. The hard skills needed to work in this sector depend on the job you're doing, but many roles share the same soft skills, such as: Communication. It's vital to share clear, relevant information promptly, and understand the details of what others are communicating. Customer Service. Many of these careers are customer facing, so patience, courtesy, professionalism, and other people's skills are important. Flexibility. Flexibility is key when it comes to dealing with disruptions and other unexpected situations. Organizational Skills. Planning and organization are critical to creating and maintaining itineraries, timetables, and schedules. Problem-Solving. When problems arise, workers must act quickly to find causes and determine the best solutions to get things back on track. Safety. Traveler and employee safety is paramount, so workers must understand how their actions and decisions might create or reduce potential hazards and risks. Teamwork. Transportation-related projects require a massive amount of collaboration, so teamwork skills are vital to achieving goals. Technical Skills. Employees use a wide range of software programs and systems depending on their specific job.

title: Practice Activating Plan B
transcript: ﻿1 Practice Activating Plan B. Knowing your backup plan is one thing. Using it under pressure is another. That's why practice matters. When you rehearse small setbacks ahead of time, you train your brain to respond with action instead of shutting down. This isn't about pretending the worst will happen- it's about proving to yourself that even if it does, you've got options. Start with everyday scenarios and work your way up. Think of it like building a muscle. The more you lift, the stronger it gets! Here are some simple ways to practice: Your printer jams before a deadline, email the file to yourself and print it at school at library or a local shop. Your lab partner is out sick, practice how you'd ask your instructor to pair you with someone new or adjust the assignment. Your headphones are missing before a noisy commute. Use a grounding tool like a fidget, a focus app, or try reading a familiar book to stay calm. Try this next: Write down five common hiccups that throw you off. Missed alarms. Tech failures. Sudden schedule changes. For each one, come up with at least two backup options. Then rehearse them, say them out loud. Walk through them with a friend or teacher. The more familiar these responses become, the easier it'll be to activate them under stress. And don't be afraid to make it collaborative or even fun. Challenge a classmate or peer mentor, "Let's each come up with one plot twist today and role-play how we'd handle it." Take turns, tossing curve balls, and problem-solving on the fly. It's not about getting it right the first time. It's about getting used to thinking on your feet and staying calm when things shift. If you're working with a job coach, teacher, or family member, ask them to throw in a surprise scenario once in a while. "The elevator's out." "There's a loud fire drill during your quiz." "Your wifi crashes during an online class." You'll be surprised how quickly your brain starts scanning for solutions- not just problems. Each time you put a backup plan into action, you build trust in yourself. Not just that you can bounce back- but that you will. Because flexibility isn't just a skill, it's a superpower. Expect setbacks. Prepare backups. Keep going. Use "if-then" plans to handle disruptions before they happen. Map your resources- people, tools, and calming strategies. Build multi-layer options for transportation, tech, and health. Practice pivoting through small hiccups so you're ready for bigger ones. Confidence grows when you know you've got options! You've got the skills, you've got the mindset. Now you've got the backup plan.
